Even if when I ran it outside my shirt, I found that the cord got caught too often, pulling the earbuds out, which is a terrible pet peeve of mine. Extra cord length, even just 6 inches longer, would have made all the difference.Using this while working out, one earbud side crapped out already. Actually, it still plays, just at a lower volume than the other side, so they're still technically usable, but gives me uneven sound now.However, if you're just going to be using this in front of your laptop, the cord length won't matter, and you'd be less like to break these earbuds than I was. For that, I upgraded my 1-star rating to 2-stars, but I still think a good pair of headphones should at least be taken outside the home to fulfill its purpose.Because they're close to crapping out, I'll have to buy yet another pair of earbuds. All earbuds crap out inevitably, but I didn't get my money's worth in the time I've used them. Skullcandy earbuds last me at least 8-10 months, and, without any of the problems I've stated above, are therefore more worth getting to me. Looks like I'll be going back to that cycle of buying Skullcandy's again.PS: When I received the Panasonic earbuds, I thought I mis-ordered on Amazon but I didn't; "red" really looks more pink if any. Doesn't really matter to me, but its a consideration.

title: Don't trust all these good reviews.  12k reviews for one pair of unremarkable earbuds?
body: I'm giving these two stars because they're inexpensive, which might let them get away with such terrible sound quality.I got these because my previous earbuds, a pair of JVC HAFX1X Headphone Xtreme-Xplosivs which were great while they lasted, started losing sound in the left side. I searched for earbuds by average reviews, and was AMAZED to see one pair of inexpensive earbuds with nearly 12 thousand reviews, a 4 1/2 star average rating, most of them raving about how wonderful they are, especially for such a great price! So I bought them.When I first tried them out, I switched to the large buds, as I do with most earbuds, which I could feel fit my ears quite nicely. I point this out to assure any possible readers that my problem can't be blamed on wearing them incorrectly or not fitting them in right. I listened to some music on them and instantly noticed how inferior they sounded compared to any other inexpensive earbuds I've owned.These earbuds are so QUIET, and lifeless. Like many of the other low reviews state, there's hardly any bass, or sound in general for that matter. With all the earbuds and earphones I've used, I've always been able to listen to my music around the same volume levels without too much disparity. I usually have my volume, on a meter from muted-100, around 25-30. With these earbuds, in order to hear my music as loudly as I normally do around 25-30, I had to turn my volume up to 90. And even at a louder volume, there still wasn't any punch to it. Just flat.I know, for $10, how can I really complain too much. But this review isn't about the money. It's about the confusion in why these earbuds have not only so many good reviews, but so many reviews in general. 12 thousand reviews? Are you kidding? Who are these people and what about these earbuds earned such attention? I know for me, it was the reviews. Don't make the same mistake.

title: You get what you pay for.
body: I bought these based on the glowing reviews, even though I was skeptical because they are so cheap. Now, it's hard to regret an $8 purchase, but I am disappointed.Bad:1) They do NOT have a straight plug, unlike the photos in the description. I was specifically looking for headphones with a straight, thin plug so they would fit in my phone case. I tried it anyway, and it does not fit. It is false advertising to have a photo in the listing that does not portray the product accurately. I would not have bought these if I knew they didn't have a straight plug.2) They are cheap. I knew that before I bought them. But they make a clicking sound every time a sound starts or stops. It's fine while listening to music constantly, but I also use them for games and other apps which only make a noise every few seconds, so it's constantly clicking. Maybe that's tolerable for you, but know that you get what you pay for.3) This is very minor, but just an fyi. The green color is a little strange. The color of the actual earbuds is close to what's pictured, but the cord is more of a light pea or spring green.There are some positives:1) The sound quality really is very good, especially for the price. You just need to make sure they're fitting in your ears properly, with a tight seal. They block out a great amount of background noise too, if worn properly.2) They are pretty comfortable. I only wore them for a couple hours, just sitting on the couch, but they never felt loose or fell out. My ears were a tiny bit sore, but probably just because I've previously been using the same pair for many years, and these are shaped differently. The hard center part is an oval shape instead of just circular.I'll keep these as a backup pair because $8 is not worth going through the hassle of returning them, but the first two negatives I listed are dealbreakers for me.

title: Do yourself a favor ... don't bother trying these.
body: These buds fit very loosely in my ears. If I try to push them in slightly, to make them snugger, then the sound becomes very low level and muddy; probably pushing in the buds obstructs the small hole where the sound comes out.Sound balance is very much lacking in low frequencies. By comparison the highs (of course) seem much louder; that makes it difficult to assess the *quality* of the highs. At any rate, I find the balance very unpleasant for music listening. They might be OK for audio books, but I wanted them for music.They have no "noise cancellation" because that feature require active circuitry and small microphones on each earpiece. (In fact I can't think of any earbuds that have noise cancellation, although everybody has heard of the Bose noise cancelling *headphones* which are pretty good.If Amazon really wanted to ask about "noise isolation" ... ability of the device to keep external noise from sneaking into the ear canal ... then these earbuds could have that feature. They could ... but they don't. Isolation is almost nonexistent.The Amazon review form asks about "moisture wicking." How could earbuds have that feature? Headphones might have a headband with a moisture wicking pad; headphones might have moisture wicking ear pads. But this product is not a headphone ... it's an earbud. As such, "moisture wicking" doesn't apply AFAIK.In conclusion, these are very disappointing. Sonically they are think and basically awful for music. If you want something that sounds a whole lot better, try a pair of Tascam HD-02 *headphones*. Sure, they're not earbuds, and they're not the world's best headphones. But they are acceptable, they are MUCH better than these earbuds, and they cost roughly $18.00.Of course if you want bad-sounding orange toys in your ears, these Panasonic earbuds are perfect for you.

title: Nice fit, sound, and features...but they fail pretty quickly
body: I like everything about these buds but their durability. They simply do not last. I suppose if I simply sat still in a comfy chair, the wires would never sway, the connections would never be challenged, and the durability might be eternal. But the wires are a very thin gauge, the connections (especially to the angled jack) vulnerable, and even if you never catch a wire on a doorknob or counter corner - and I took special care not to - these earbuds will fail, apparently from the simple natural swaying motion of walking or jogging, within months. This my third failure, this last one having been comped to me by Panasonic after the failure of its predecessor, and three strikes you're out. Years ago I bought a straight-up no frills version of these Ergos - no mic, no pause - and today that's what I'm using, until I decide on a replacement. Is the newer version made of cheaper materials, or with lousy quality control? Take your pick. The old ones are still functional. The new ones have all failed within 6 weeks.I wish it were not so. Love the fit, can wear them to sleep, love a mic and a pause, and the price is brilliant. That's probably the issue. I'd happily pay double for a more durable product, but I generally buy less expensive buds because, well, they fail.Keep on truckin'...Postscript, 1 December 2019: As the Panasonics are the only earbuds I've used that have both decent fidelity and a structure sufficiently small to sleep on, I still buy them. And treat them tenderly. About a month ago, I bought the pretty blue option, which is two dollars cheaper than the $13 standard black. They perform beautifully and differentiate by sight from my other black and white earbud sets. But I NEVER go walkabout with them: they're my sleepbuds...and good for that if I'm not rollin' and a-tumblin' all the whole night long...

title: For me, not pleasant to hear.
body: I'm basing this rating on comparative listening and ownership experience with Skull Candy S2DUDZ012, Phillips SHS3200BK/37, and $300 Bose QC15s which are too expensive to replace considering such astonishingly poor build quality. The Bose deliver a superb sound and are intended to be used by affluent listeners of critical tastes and untroubled thought in tossing and replacing them every three months or so. After mine rapidly went to dust, I replaced them with the Phillips. I liked the ear clip approach with the compact over-the-ear design. They do not come off while listening in bed. I used these for three years pleased at how close they were in sound quality to the 30 times as costly QCs. Finally, the cushion rings came off and split upon attempting to re-glue them. OK, ten bucks. I discovered the Phillips were no longer available locally. So upon the advice of a seemingly knowledgeable salesperson, I purchased the Skull Candys. I expected that an in-ear isolating setup would offer better sound than an over-the-ear design. I listen at low levels to symphonic classical, intimate jazz, and for a total frequency band orgy, the late Laura Branigan. Alas, the Skull Candy disappointed compared to the late Phillips, but Candy would do in a pinch while waiting for the highly acclaimed HJE120s from Amazon. Upon connecting the Panasonics to my mp3 player I discovered, despite the opinions of the masses, they were worse than the Skull Candy. I soon experienced unbearable ringing from the overwhelming 1-2khz emphasis from the Panasonics. I heard few frequencies below 200hz at normal (for me) listening volume. The only worse sound I've heard was from airline issued head phones. The Panasonics are going back for health reasons if for nothing else. For sound quality alone I give the Bose 5, the Phillips 4, Skull Candy 3, Panasonic 2 and airline 1.

title: Try the SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WE earbuds
body: Hello, These are the Best sounding ear-buds you can buy under $30. Trust me these sound Better than the Klipsch S3 ear-buds, and better than the more expensive Panasonic RPHJE355A High Fidelity Ergo-Fit ear-buds, and better than most Sony ear-buds.These Panasonic ear-buds have good bass, treble, balance and play loud. At only $7ea. you can not go wrong in buying a set of these ear-buds. They sound great for only $7Would be nice if these had tangle free cords with mic and volume control for mobile phone. Like the SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WE earbuds. For this reason I will have to say the SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WE earbuds are better because they have tangle free cords and volume controls with mic.SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WEYou can find these for $5 on ebay or amazon.-------------------------------------------------------------Update: 2-23-2015I will be honest with you. These Panasonic earbuds sound Great for the price. Good Bass, Tribe and play loud. They sound better than the Klipsch S3 for sure. It would have been nice if these had tangle free cords with mic and volume control for mobile phone. Like the SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WE earbuds. For this reason I will have to say the SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WE earbuds are better because they have tangle free cords and volume controls with mic. I like the SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WE so much better simply because I became frustrated un-tangling the thin cords of the Panasonic earbuds.I use the Samsung earbuds on a daily basis and own about 5 pairs. These are my goto earbuds on a daily basis.SAMSUNG EO-HS3303WEYou can find these for $5 on ebay or amazon.( http://www.amazon.com/Samsung-3-5mm-Stereo-Headset-Galaxy/dp/B00F9G20LM/ref=sr_1_2?ie=UTF8&qid=1424752091&sr=8-2&keywords=Samsung+earbuds+EO-HS3303WE-1 ).

title: A little better than free iPod ear buds...
body: So many people commented how great these headphones were for $6, so I had to try a pair. I am not impressed. Maybe I was expecting too much, but I really wouldn't recommend this using.Fit: Not too comfortable. Sure it comes with 3 different rubber ear pieces. But the shape and fit does not insulate/isolate the music vs outside noise. I am not talking about noise cancellation, but noise suppression. I was not impressed. The rubber seems to be not as soft as other ear buds I have tried.Sound quality: It is definitely not as great as others have mentioned on their reviews. The lows are non-existent, mids are normal, highs are not crisp. Very dissappointing. The sound quality is on par with the free iPod OEM ear buds, but these are more comfortable in your ears.Cable quality: The cable quality is a cheap as the 99 cent "made in china" audio cables you buy on eBay. If it didn't say "Panasonic" on the ear buds, I would think it is straight from overseas with 3 week shipping time. I don't know how to explain it, but these cables look "brittle" compared to other "softer" looking wire on other ear buds. The plug is NOT the slim type, so if you want that "snap" fit on your iphone/ipod, you'll need to remove your casing.. this was a BIG minus for me.. the sound still comes out, but you don't get that secure "snap."Conclusion: Personally, I would spend the extra $ and buy a model that cost a little more. I am all for "cheaper" ear buds. This is my 4th ear bud, because I use different ones at work, on plane, walking around, etc... I think you would be more happier if you bought $15~$20 altec lansings and Sennheiser branded ones. Yes they cost a bit more. But I don't think I will be using this $6 Panasonic, where is the value in that. :)

title: Weirdly shaped
body: I'll start off by saying that I HATE EARBUDS!! I have oddly shaped ears so I generally use actual headphones. However, as a college student, I needed something more versatile and less heavy, also something easy to put away and take out quick. So I bought earbuds.The Good:-The color. It's nice and pink, just as shown.-The sound--ish. They sound pretty good. Not amazing, but not horrible. (keep in mind you'll be paying under 10 bucks, not 50 so you can expect less than amazing quality)-They stay in the jack. My other headphones always slip from my phone jack. These haven't slipped ONCE, which is great.The Bad:-The odd shape that's supposed to go in your ear. Don't be fooled (like I was) the round clearish-gray bulb you see to the left of the pink back on the earbud IS NOT ROUND, it is oblong shaped. The clear plastic (oblong shaped) bulb comes off and they give you 2 other sizes (3 sizes. Large, medium, small) I'm using the small ones because I have oddly shaped ears. They don't really sit in my ears right, if it's windy, they will fall out my ear. It's pretty difficult for me to get them in my ears right.-If you want earbuds that you can blast without people hearing you listening to the Pokemon theme song, you might not want these. People can hear what you're listening to. I tried them at different levels and asked my sister if she could name the songs I'm listening to. She named just about everything, just not when it was ridiculously low.Overall, I don't like them. They have amazing reviews so maybe it's just me; keep in mind that I already hate earbuds and have irregular shaped ears. If you really want to try them, by all means, do it! It's not the end of the world; they're pretty cheap.

title: They are good, but they hurt -sad face-
body: I don't hate it, but I didn't like it. What is the difference? This wasn't a bad product. It worked with my iPhone 5s. It has this funny ability to plug out the rest of the noise by like 50%. It doesn't cancel noise, but the rubber covering plugs right into your ear, so everything sounds a lot less loud. I was walking home and I had to walk along the road, so I unplugged my left ear, so I could hear for incoming cars. What a laugh it gave me, when I could hear the wind so loudly, car engines loudly. I was like... was this supposed to be a noise cancelling earphone? It's not a noise-cancelling earphone though, just a heads up if someone read it that way.Also, another heads up for iPhone 5s users who uses the Slim Armor case by Spigen Inc. or similar cases like the Slim Armor: the plug won't fit. More precisely, the case will block the plug from going in, so you can either use the earphones without a case on your iPhone 5s or find a special case that will allow additional room for the plug to go in. So, my reason for giving this earphone 2 stars, instead of 3, 4, or 5, is because they really hurt. When you put them on... how do I explain this? The edges of the earphone (the part where the rubber is supposed to cushion) sort of digs into my eardrums, so it hurts. The pain is worst when you sleep with them on. I woke up and my right ear (earphone came off on left ear during sleep, haha) was sore.So, yeah. I am the minority where I have an issue with the earphone. Everyone else seems to love them and the earphone sure loves their ear, because I don't see any comments about it hurting their ears. I actually bought them, because of the great reviews, haha. Hope this review will help others.

title: I used to love these, but they don't make them like they used to
body: I used to love these earbuds, every time I'd need new earbuds I'd replace them with these and I'd get about a good year or maybe a bit more before they ended up wearing down or one of the buds would start fading out, which is pretty good life considering the price and that I'd use them every day and they'd get hours of use a day. Hell, these earbuds used to last better than expensive ones, I once bought $20 earbuds to replace a pair of these that broke and that pair only lasted a month before one of the wire snapped, and when I replaced it with one of these again I had no issues. But this past year I've had 2 of these break on me within a few months. The first pair lasted only 2 months and then one of the earbuds literally broke apart, the piece connecting the earbud (the part you put in your ear) broke off, revealing the wire inside and unable to be fixed. I thought okay, maybe I got unlucky and got a dud, so I ordered another one and it worked well, but a month of use and the same exact thing happened, one of the earbuds broke apart, and I did absolutely nothing to it.I used to love these earbuds and have used these earbuds since 2015 replacing the Sony ones I used to use before and for the past 5 years I've used these earbuds and no other brand and would get one year out of each one. Now they are made like garbage and barely make it over a month before falling apart, the quality of these has dipped greatly in the 5 years since I started using these and after my past 2 experiences with these, I will no longer be using these earbuds any longer. These used to be the best earbuds on the market for under $10, but now they are among the worst and not even worth $1 because they will break within a month or 2.

title: Nice Sound, okay noise cancelling but OUCH
body: I purchased these ear buds after my second pair of GOgroove audiOHMs walked away from my desk at work.I purchased these because they were less expensive, had good ratings, had different sizes of silicone buds, and were noise cancelling. The sound is good, the noise cancelling is just okay - they definitely help buffer sound but I can still hear what is going around me. What I am unhappy with is that they claim to be ergo fit, because of the dif sizes of silicone buds, but this is just not true. What you really don't see in the picture is that they buds are an oval shape and attached to hard plastic circles. These hard plastic circles are very much like the ones that you get when you buy an ipod - the cheap plastic ear buds. Well in order for these headphones to get into your ear properly the hard circle must also fit into your ear - and unfortunately for me that piece is one size fits all (some?!) I am actually in pain with them in my ear, just like I feel with the cheap ipod ear buds. Even with the smallest silicone soft ear bud on they hurt - because that hard disc has to be in the ear as well.I am going back to GOgroove audiOHM ear buds because only the silicone part sits in your ear and they are much more comfortable. They also are very good at blocking out background noise. [[ASIN:B005QBAGRK GOgroove audiOHM Ergonomic Metallic Purple Earbuds with Interchangeable Noise-Reduction Silicone Ear Pieces]]Lesson learned here. It is unfortunate because I ordered two of these Panasonic ear buds, since they were inexpensive, but I am going to return the one I didn't open yet. I guess I can keep the other for back up but they are really very uncomfortable.

title: Blah
body: Ok, I'm not gonna lie, I'm slightly disappointed. The majority of the reviews on these rave about the sound quality, comfort, and level of build quality. While these earbuds are extremely comfortable (you may need to adjust the tips to a bigger or smaller size, which I did to make them more comfy, and doing so does alter the sound. Bigger tips give more bass, but do what makes you comfortable) the sound quality is just bad. I will admit that I'm biased because I'm used to getting Sony earbuds, but for the level of satisfaction most people report on these earbuds, I expected more. The bass on these is awesome but a little muddy (as in the sound gets a little mixed up when you're listening to music and you have to turn the volume down to hear the bass, mids and highs in sink). The mids are okay. They're not horrible, but no matter how far you push these into your ears with correct fitting tips, they sound distant. The highs are non-existent. I really wanted to like these guys! I will say one thing, if you watch tv on your computer, tablet, or phone, these are awesome. But honestly I can't totally hate. They were only $8! Even though these particular earbuds were mentioned in a couple of "best headphones of 2015" websites and I had high expectations, they're not totally bad considering the price. I'll probably use them when I'm on the go or working out. If you need decent cheap earbuds, I say give them a shot, because sound quality is subjective with every person. But don't fall into the hype and expect super high quality sound. And about the build quality. They seem solid. Not gonna lie, they look and feel cheap, but I can tell they'll last, as long as you're not too rough with them.

title: Low cost comes with many compromises.
body: These are cheap, no doubt. Are they worth it...that really depends. For me the answer is no. These might be good deal for some at $8, but honestly I wouldn't want these for free.Noise-isolating earphones come with their own set of quirks. Here are a list of the few I discovered after receiving these:- when the headphone cord rubs on something, that sound is amplified and transmitted to your ears.- when you sing along with your music, you will hear your voice, but it will sound muffled and crappy.- you won't be able to hear what is going on around you, which might be a plus depending on your preferences and circumstances.Pros:- You get to pick out of 5 colors.- They fit snugly in the ear. You can switch up the fit for your ear using the different plug sizes provided. Unfortunately, none of the sizes really fit that comfortably in my left ear.- These are light.- They isolate outside noise reasonably well.Cons:- Sound, especially the vocals, are quite muddled. This sucks a lot. I was happier with my old Koss KSC35's and the free earphones that came with my Sandisk Sansa.- Fit is hit or miss. Miss for me.- Noise-isolation specific issues mentioned above.Bottom line:These will suffice to listen to your audio tracks in privacy. The sound is pretty bad, but if you are distracted with other things to do (like work, lifting, or posting 5-star reviews for cheap stuff on amazon), you may be able sufficiently ignore the mediocre sound out of these headphones. If you want to enjoy your music, keep looking and save your money for something better.

title: Tragus Hold Design....This particular product hurt my ears
body: 2 stars is considered "I didn't like the product"I usually am generous with my stars in reviews but I honestly didn't like this product. I should have read the reviews and taken more time to research this product. I was in a pinch and wanted ear-phones quickly and these were in my price range. I thought Panasonic would be a safe product to purchase without ever using before but I wished I hadn't. The ear pieces are shaped oddly and hurt my right ear. The part that fits into your device (cell phone jack, iPod, whatever) is hardened plastic and curved to the side. My last pair of ear-buds had a straight jack and that served better when I moved around and didn't stick out so far as this tends to do from my phone. I am not a technical expert on this but I will do my best to describe my personal experience. The color is true to the picture on Amazon and the product comes with 4 extra ear bud silicone covers. An extra pair of size Small and a pair of size Large. This is to try and fit different ears comfortably. The ear bud speaker itself is small and shaped vertically and placed to the side of the ear bud. This makes it hard for me personally to place in my ear and I spend a lot of time adjusting it. I do like the sound quality and the clarity of listening to the radio is better than my last pair of no-name ear buds.Spec from product insert:Driver units: 9mm (3/8in)Sensitivity: 96 dB/mWFrequency Response: 20 Hz- 20 kHzCORD: 1.1M (3.6FT)PLUG: 3.5mm (1/8in)I cant send them back so I'm just making do until I find something that works better for me.I wouldn't buy these again.

title: Yes they're only six bucks, but...
body: Sorry to rain on the party here, but after owning these for a week I think some of the reviewers have gotten a bit carried away. The problem is sound is subjective, and many people don't understand how to evaluate it or simply tell themselves it's good because they spent money on it.Granted, it's not a lot of money. For those who remember how Apple sold replacement earbuds of poor quality for years at a forty dollar price point (do they still?), it would seem impressive if these earbuds function at all. No, they're not horrendously uncomfortable. And no, the sound isn't terrible. But is there a noticeable difference between these and an eighty dollar pair of headphones (as one reviewer mentioned)? Of course, and it's significant.While panasonic gives us the now standard 3 sizes of rubber inserts, they're not nearly as comfortable as my old sony fontopias. But berhaps my ears are an unusual shape. And I could always jam the fontopias in a little deeper (at a small expense of comfort) to get a deeper bass and a fuller sound. Not so with these guys. The sound is thin and tinny with no bass and weak mids. It's like someone is playing your music over the radio, taping it, then playing it back to you through these headphones.For the price these make a cheap, easily replaceable backup pair when you need one, or for camping/traveling. But if you value the sound of your music long term please do not make these its primary mode of transmission, it's just not worth it. For a little more money the musicians, and your ears, deserve better.

title: My Favorite Earbuds, But This Time...
body: Final Edit: Yea... So They're Actually TrashI didn't want to believe that my favorite budget earbuds have changed for the worse, but I've had these replacement earbuds for about a month now, treating them with the same care I have given them in the past, and they are already shorting out. It looks like I will have to find a new brand of earbuds, because I can no longer count on the quality of these ones. I gave 2 stars instead of 1 out of respect for the good sound quality when they work.--Edit: 5 Stars Again!--I received my replacement pair of headphones, and they are just as good as I remember. The pair I received before must have been defective. I am satisfied!--Original 3 Star Review--These earbuds are my favorite brand of earbuds. The sound quality is great, and the durability is more than decent for the price point. They usually last me around a year before I start to have any problems with them, and for the past 4 years or so I have repeatedly returned to buy a new pair. This time though, my earbuds arrived defective. Despite coming straight out the box, they were already shorting out. Slight movement of the cord caused the sound to cut in and out. The packaging is a bit different this time, so perhaps it is a different product, because this has never happened to me before. I am currently in the process of replacing these headphones, so I will give an update when I receive my replacement pair. I am extremely disappointed this time, but I am willing to try again. Hopefully this isn't a trend.

title: Great if you want earbuds for 2 weeks
body: I've actually had a few pairs of these. For some reason, even though they kept breaking, I was convinced that I was getting the most for my money. However, after I finally tried a pair of earbuds that weren't $10 and realized I could get the same amount of sound AND have them longer then 2 weeks, I was stunned.When you first get these earbuds, you'll be so excited. They come in a variety of colors, the sound is great, they fit in your ears well, and they have those soft little silicone things that make listening so much more comfortable. However, about a week later, you realize you've lost one of those little buggers and don't even try to go looking for it because I can assure it is gone forever. You may just want to keep a small stash somewhere.If this not-so-small problem doesn't immediately make you turn in run, maybe the poorly-covered, flimsy wires will. Hopefully you don't have any pets, because their relentless, playful chewing will just bring the inevitable death of these earbuds. Even if you are INCREDIBLY careful, there's just no way you'll be able to avoid destroying one of the wires, causing sound to cease playing from one of the earbuds.So basically, if you're planning on getting a quality pair of earbuds (I would recommend Brookstones) in a very short amount of time but you just need a stand-in pair, then these are a great solution. Hopefully this helped you out, because I sure wish someone had told me that. Thanks for your time :)

title: Great price, Average sound
body: I've been looking for a back-up pair of earbuds for my current Skullcandy Ink'd pair. I play music professionally and listen mostly at home and on the train to work. Below are my Top 3 earbud recommendations...The Skullcandy Ink'd are the best earbuds I have ever had and I've tried about 20 different ones. Wide open highs, solid punchy lows that extend very low. They also have the best sound isolation of any earbud I have tried. So traveling on the train lets me keep the volume lower, saving my ears, yet keeping the external sounds out. Give them a couple weeks to break in, and your ears will love you for it ! I could not use the microphone model. Would not play in my Smartphone or computers.The Skullcandy JIB are in fairness slightly cheaper than the Ink'd, but still have that great Skullcandy crispness in the highs, solid lows, and very good isolation from external noises. At $7.61 these are steal. Grab them in any color you want.The Sony MDR-EX51LP are a good all around earbud. Smooth highs and solid lows. I have had these for years and they have held up very well.The Panasonic RPHE120K earbuds got lots of great reviews, but do not measure up to the sound of either of the Skullcandy's. The highs are smooth and lows are there, but they sound muddy compared to the Skullcandy's. The Panasonics come with 3 sizes of earbuds which is a nice plus.I've also tried several JVC earbuds which are excellent.Good luck.

title: Poor audio quality for music
body: I got these because I expect IEMs to break irrespective of price or brand, and I think that's a waste of money. I prefer on-ear and especially over-ear headphones for sound quality and own 3 fairly decent headphone models. Wearing my open back headphones in bed bothers my wife.I've owned about 3 or 4 different IEM models, one of which had decent sound quality until it lost a channel. Can't remember the brand. I had the pair replaced under warranty, and it lost the same channel again within a day.Of the different models I've owned, these are by the worst in sound quality. It's odd to me that some people actually like the SQ. The 120S makes OEM iPhone under-average-sounding earbuds seem quite good. Nearly no bass, range or headroom no matter how hard I shove them into my ear canals. Music sounds tinny and unpleasant. The comparison to Apple earbuds isn't really fair given the low cost of this set, but it's the only way I could think of to rate the audio.The IEMs don't stay in place, so sound goes from bad to worse. For podcast, etc., they may be adequate, but if you want to sit back and experience your music, look elsewhere.Gave them 2 stars because they're still working after a week or two of use, which is more than I can say for more expensive IEMs I've owned. I don't know what's worse: under $10 for bad sound quality, or over $100 for decent sound quality for only a month or two. It's probably a 2.5 stars for what it is.

title: First pair lasted me 6 years, second was faulty, and Panasonic's customer service is subpar.
body: I purchased a pair of these back in 2016, and it was the best $6 investment in a solid pair of earbuds. The sound quality is decent, the earbuds are comfortable, they served me very well.Just a month ago, the rubber ear piece finally ripped after probably a year or so of it starting to tear. And since I threw away the original packaging years ago, I had no rubber replacements.So I opted to buy a replacement pair.They worked just as well, except this second pair must just be a dud because after exactly a month of use, the right earbud has stopped producing sound. And I honestly haven't moved them around much or taken them in and out of devices. They pretty much just stay by my laptop on my desk.And of course, the return window closed yesterday, so I can't get a replacement or refund.Tried to talk to Panasonic directly, and I would've had to mail them in for inspection to determine they were actually broken, the shipping for which I would be required to cover.For $9, definitely not worth my time.At the end of the day, I'm just disappointed. I loved my first pair, and am sad to have ended up with a bad second pair. And I wish Panasonic had a little more customer care in terms of working through the issue. Idk why they don't have a better warranty for their products. Even if they were under $10, it's still frustrating.That's my experience. Hopefully this helps in your decision process.

title: Doesn't cut the mustard for runners.
body: I bought these headphones to replace a pair of trusty MEE Sport-Fi M6 headphones that I had used for running for about the past 4-5 years. I loved the MEE's, but they are quite a bit more pricey and based on the great reviews (even by runners) I thought I'd try the more budget minded panasonics. That was a mistake.The sound quality of these headphones is perfectly adequate. But for running, at least for me, they don't work. I tried all three sizes of rubber ear buds that came with the headphones but none of them made the headphones stay in place while out for even a short run.I do want to qualify this by saying I run with my iPhone in my hand. And the opposite ear of whatever hand I held the iPhone with while running kept falling out. Maybe for people who use a sport band to keep their hands free this isn't such a problem.The MEE's have two things that make them much better suited for runners. First is the flexible memory wire that goes over the ears. Second is the clip on the cord that allows you to attach it to prevent it from moving too much. These are small details but make a huge difference in my opinion.If you are buying these headphones to simply listen to music, movies, or podcasts at home or while traveling then they will probably work just fine. If you are buying them for exercise I'd strongly recommended the MEE's, which I will be buying again.

title: Poor Sound Quality for Music Lovers and Faulty Wire
body: [[ASIN:B003EM8008 Panasonic ErgoFit In-Ear Earbud Headphones RP-HJE120-K (Black) Dynamic Crystal Clear Sound, Ergonomic Comfort-Fit]]Listed as a #1 Bestseller with such high ratings, I had no second thoughts about ordering. Now I wish I did... I don't understand the high ratings for this product and should have known better by its low pricing. (Expensive doesn't always equate to better, but in this instance it may.) On first use with my mp3 player (which is in great condition), the ear buds produced a lot of static. I thought maybe it was because of the first time use although I'd never experienced this problem with other brands. I connected it to two more devices - same problem. It seems to go away for a bit if you keep moving the wire around. Unacceptable. The sound is clear indeed, but only in a very general way. The ear buds add no texture to your sound whatsoever. Even as I changed the settings on my device (bass, treble, etc.), the sound remained the same. It may be acceptable to someone who just listens to podcasts or similar programming. But for true music lovers who understand sound, this is not it.One positive - As advertised, they DO stay in your ear quite well and come with three additional size rubber ear pads to fit your ear if necessary.I expected more from Panasonic. Sony ear buds sound better - not the best, but better. These buds are going back.

title: Poor Wiring, Decline in Quality
body: I got this pair of headphones a few years ago and loved them. For the price, the sound quality was great and the mid-wire button is a big convenience. Unfortunately, had an accident and ended up breaking the wire and that pair was lost.The trouble began when I ordered a replacement pair after ruining my first pair. The first replacement pair arrived and immediately out of the box the sound was cutting in and out before quickly stopping entirely in one ear. Additionally, the button didn’t work at all and I narrowed the problem to the strain relief as I could cause some variation by getting it to just the right spots. I decided to return the pair for a replacement.Second pair arrived and worked well initially but then I quickly encountered very similar issues. Not to the same degree as the first, but I’ve noticed the strain relief is quite touchy on this pair too. It’s degraded to the point that if it so much as gets bumped, sound will cut out in one or both ears.It’s very unfortunate as the first pair I had was great and I was and am quite happy with the sound quality when it’s working. My guess is that the way it’s packaged forces the wire/relief to bend in a way that causes damage to the wires internally but I don’t know for sure. Either way it’s an unfortunate decline in quality and I won’t order these headphones again.

title: These used to be some solid earbuds(2016-2019), but not so much anymore (2020)
body: I've been buying pairs of these earbuds off and on for the better part of 3 years now, both off of Amazon and through brick and mortar stores. These used to be my go-to set of earbuds that would last around 2-3 months of the roughness of school, getting snagged on backpacks, doors, roughly removed from the phone etc. They could take the abuse pretty well, and when they failed I would have a backup pair to use until I got another set eventually.However I recently found myself a pair of earbuds from a different brand (audio technica) that lasted me a good long while and I hadn't bought a pair of Panasonic buds for a bit.Having just recently bought a pair, something has changed lately. The sound quality is still solid, but after just a day the jack into my phone has gotten a bit spotty. It's not just me either, 2 of my friends who used to routinely buy these earbuds have also moved on due to lack of quality control lately.Unfortunately, they really feel like they'll die on me soon. The price was low enough they're not worth returning or replacing. I'll probably just move to a different low-cost brand that can satisfy my day to day needs.Hopefully they fix their quality control soon, I'm not the only one who has noticed a turn for the worse as of late. Keep that in mind if you're looking to pick a pair up.

title: Cheap--you get what you pay for.
body: I purchased these after a positive review by a cnet editor, who claimed they are cheap and deliver good sound. But alas...These headphones are a last resort, in-the-pinch sort. I won't give them one star since they work (i.e. deliver sound). But the sound is very tinny and distorts very easily if the music volume is at or above street-listening level (as opposed to library-listening level), as well as if your EQ is set to deliver any small amount of bass. Another thing I didn't like: when you move your head while wearing these, or if the very thin cords move against your clothing (e.g. walking or jogging), the cord rattling sound is very loudly transferred to your ears; so much so that it was very distracting.I'm not picky about style so the minimalist design of these Panasonics suits me fine. Another plus for me is that the cord splits and two equal lengths lead to each ear piece, as opposed to a short cord for one side and a longer cord for the other side after the split. But these are very minor pluses. This Panasonic line offers great selection in colors and unbeatable prices. The set comes with three sizes of comfortable ear fittings.Despite the few redemptive qualities, these headphones lack what I consider the most important attribute of any headphones; good sound quality.

title: Not compatible if you have a case on your phone or if you desire comfort
body: I had hopes for these earphones, but not too high since they are cheap. Reading the reviews and product description gave me some excitement that I'd have a good cheap pair of earphones to wear working outside and for a backup when I'm not using my Bose SoundTrue earhones. With that being said, this is in no way a comparison to Bose. I just got these earphones today, and I'm pretty disappointed. First of all these earphones are not for you if your phone case is even slightly thick. I have what I believe and what is billed as a slim phone case for my iPhone 6 Plus, however the jack has to be in just the right position to be able to use these earphones while the case is on my phone. Not ideal…Also, these earphones are not exactly what I would call comfortable. While I do have problems wearing most types of in earphones, and earbuds won't stay in my ear, I tried all three sizes of the provided tips and none of them worked. In fact, they hurt my ears.As far as sound quality goes, I did listen long enough to be able to review that as well. I listened to a few different songs in different genres to be able to get an idea of the treble, mid, and bass. I have to say that sound quality is better than what you would expect for earphones of this price. So they do have that going for them.

title: Initially a Good Set of Headphones, BUT Has Sound Transfer Problems with Short-Lived Life
body: Many people have a problem with a "tinny" sound and low-base response. You have to get a very tight fit of each headphone in your ear canal to obtain a quality sound. There are three sizes of ear pads in the package to get the tight fit. Often people have to use a different size ear pad for each ear. At this point, you should get a great sound for the price of these headphones.That being said, there are some significant downsides to these headphones. Sound transfer from the thin insulated wires to the headphones due to rubbing on any clothing, skin, or object is very bothersome. I use my headphones daily for listening to my iPod as well as every time I exercise (cycling or aerobics) and do yard work. I have owned these headphones since May 2014. Since then, all of the headphone's wiring has become stiffer making the sound transfer to the headphones worse and more bothersome.Bottom line, if you want an inexpensive "okay" pair of headphones that will last about six months and sound transfer from the headphone's wire from outside objects does not perturb you, this is the pair of headphones to get. These headphones fit the 7th generation 160GB Classic iPod; I do not know whether they will properly fit iPhones, iPads, iPod Touch, or other smart phones.

title: 5 stars? you've got to be kidding! What a waste of money.
body: At least the price on these was next to nothing.I should have bought lottery tickets. At least I would have fun imagining what I would do with my winnings until the results came in and brought me back to earth.Do not believe these 4 and 5 star reviews for this item. These sound like $5 earbuds should sound. Period.These may be great for kids that are always losing or breaking their earbuds, but for someone who knows anything about music, you will be disappointed with them.You can imagine the quality of sound from these earbuds by comparing the quality of sound you get out of a $20 portable desktop CD player to a $300 portable boom box. Exactly. No bass whatsoever, lack of fidelity. Lots of upper mid-range frequency stuff. If your favorite instrument is electric rock guitar, you might like these. They would probably work ok to listen to audio-books.I fell for the hype in other reviews against my better judgement, thinking that maybe Panasonic has developed some new technology to produce quality sound for a low price. WRONG. Don't you do the same.This item only deserves 1 star. I give it 2 because I tend to regard 1-star reviews written with "sour grapes," and I wan't more people to read this and not get "suckered" like I was.

title: Only Buy if You Listen Exclusively While Surrounded by Deaf People
body: I bought three pairs of these because I was tired of my earbuds breaking over the course of a couple months of use, no matter how much I paid for them. I listen at higher volumes while I do landscaping and perhaps that's the source of my problem, but I'm sure I'm not the only one who goes through earbuds more frequently than they would like. So I figured I'd get a few pairs of these and save some money, at least. The reviews made them seem like remarkably high quality for the low price. The first pair lasted two months, the second and third pairs lasted about a month each. I was satisfied with the sound quality and the durability was not awful. Each time they sputtered out the same way, one earbud would suddenly be extremely quiet and the other would cease to function properly soon after. I was content, until I let my classmate listen to a song. At the lowest volume setting, I could make out nearly every word from four feet away. I thought better of ordering another pair. While these earbuds will seem okay to you for 6$, they'll seem like a nuisance to all those around you. I feel terrible that I didn't realize this sooner and wanted to share the information with you in order to bring the overwhelmingly positive reviews down to earth.

title: Cheap, feels cheap, but gets the job done
body: For ~$11(mic model), I didn't expect a lot. When I initially received it, I thought I mistakenly took out the $2 headphones that my school passes out for free-- the wire is skinny and looks like such a cheap plastic. However, the sound quality and plastic buds are the shining stars. They also have 2 pairs of extra earbud tips for your sizing preference. The thing that drives me insane is that you can hear the rubbing of the wire any time you move. It's definitely not for exercising.I'm at day 1 of use and it is fairly good so far(aside from the sound conduction from physical contact with the wire). Hopefully, it stands up to the test of time and I can use it for at least half a year. But with the quality of the wiring, I'm pretty skeptical.Update after 4 days of use: For people who like having the earbud tips inside the canal, this set of earbuds may not be for you. There is this ridge, created by a seam, on the earbud piece that rubs against your ear pretty easily. I didn't notice it the first two days, but I started to find that my ear was becoming irritated. For reference, I'm using the medium sized tips.Update after a few months: The microphonics is terrible, but it's still holding up.

title: Too tinny and you can hear the hard plastic cord knock on the earphone casing when you move
body: I have used many types of earbuds and this ranks right down there with the electronics you find at your local Family Dollar discount store. The bass response of okay, but you have to really push the earbuds into your ear to get the low frequency bass that high quality earbuds provide.My main complaint is how tinny it sounds. Shocker right? What was I expecting from a 10 dollar earphones. I had to enable my computer's sound card driver EQ to compensate for the tinny sound this earphones has. After messing with the EQ sliders for a while, I finally found a setting that I can bear. But guess what? With the increased dB that the EQ provided, the poor earphones now has distortion and clipping with my EQ enabled.When you tug on the earphones while the earbuds are in your ear, you can hear the cord move. This is exacerbated by the fact that the cord is a hard plastic. Even when you move your head without touching the cord, you can hear the cord hit the hard plastic casing of the earphones. Higher quality earbuds solve this by using a soft plastic sheathing on the cord.I should have listen to my doubts with I bought this earphones, but the high number of 5 star reviews convinced me to get them.

title: Better than most but bad mic for singing
body: Sound quality is good. Mic (used for sing on karaoke app) sounds very compressed, like singing into a tin can. Not useable for serious recording - which may beg the question, "How serious do you expect an earbud mic to be?"But the mic quality of old Apple earbuds were easily on par with that of my low/mid-range dedicated USB Snowball Yeti mic, so I got spoiled.Would I have used them for recording an album? No, I still used a professional interface. But these earbuds, in contrast, are basically unusable for karaoke, unfortunately.Packaging was decent, sound seems pretty good, no popping after 3 uses, and they are reasonably comfortable - better than most. I haven't noticed the "phantom earbud tingle" some leave in your ears after a day of constant use, but then I haven't put them to rigorous tests to date due to aforementioned issues.I do prefer them to earplugs in a pinch due to effective sound cancelling & comfort. Jury's out on durability. Will update if they fail within a year. Sending back such a small thing in Covid times is too much of a nuisance and frankly they could be much worse; this is my 5th attempt to replace the old ones & so I'm resigned to settle, at this point.

title: Disappointed to say the least
body: I have to say that I was very excited about these headphones. I read the reviews on Amazon and was very happy to find a pair of headphones with "great" sound quality (according to others) and that were overall a very good pair of headphones--durable, comfortable, etc. (especially for the cost). Well, I had my headphones for 3 weeks and the wire snapped with little effort (I didn't yank on it or anything of the like). It was so flimsy. The wire was always getting tangled, which was super annoying. I also don't agree with other people when they say the headphones are comfortable. Even when using the smallest size I was uncomfortable, and when left in for a long time I got a horrible headache. When I was exercising and using these earbuds, I could hear the sound of the wire moving against my shirt and it ruined the sound. On a slightly positive note, I definitely thought the sound quality was better than the typical Apple headphones (what wouldn't sound better than those?!) but I wasn't impressed with the general sound quality either--it seemed a bit tinny (and I'm no audiophile). Overall, I would rather pay a bit more money for a more durable pair of headphones that have a higher sound quality....very disappointing!

title: Sounds Good -- But No Slider to Keep Wires from Tangling
body: Very comfortable (take note, Apple) and the sound is decent enough (I'm not that discriminating though), but why no slider ring on the wire where it branches in the shape of a Y to keep the earbuds from tangling? (And they do.) Can't believe they missed this. Deal killer. I would not buy these again for this reason, but otherwise they're fine (and actually quite comfortable -- i have even fallen asleep with them in listening to audio books at night).One nice touch was that they come with three different sized pairs of rubber covers for different sized ears, and one will surely be comfortable for you, plus the rubber bends and flexes and doesn't hurt your ears like hard plastic can.One little slider ring to keep them from tangling and they would have been hard to complain about, but now I'm already looking for something to replace them with.Also, the ear buds are sided, and I don't know how, but every time I went to put one in it was the wrong one. You'd think the law of averages would have made it about 50% of the time, but it was almost 100%. That got old fast, though it did make them comfortable to fall asleep with while listening to a book on my iPhone from Audible.

title: You get what you pay for
body: First, the pros of this product.The comfort is really unbelievable! Probably the best fitting pair of headphones I've ever owned - I've spent more than a pretty penny searching for a good pair.The highs come through very clear and pleasant - I'm getting some nice noises out of songs that I've never noticed before because they were drowned out!Now, the cons.The cording is very obviously cheap. Admittedly these are only $5 and you really couldn't get better cording for that price but if you're going to be doing something vigorous (like running, for example) the cording won't last you long at all.The deal breaker: the bass. The bass is literally GONE. I cannot hear any bass at all unless I have the volume set to 90%+ a volume that is obviously not good for your hearing. If listening to music at a normal volume your music will have a pathetic amount of bass and your music simply won't be the same. I simply cannot stand to listen to music without a major component. I'm not one to want the bass "blasting" but I definitely want it to be present.You would get better performance out of a pair of ear buds from a gas station.

title: Mediocre, even for the price
body: At first: These are comfy and sound as good as any earbuds I’ve ever owned. They do go rather deep into one’s ear, which is probably good for noise cancelling (I’m just not used to it.)My two drawbacks are the thinness of the cord (worryingly thin.... I foresee tangles and fraying) and the plug. Unlike the straight plug/jack on Apple headphones, this has one of those bent jacks that look very old-fashioned and are just asking to get bumped out of your phone somehow.That being said, I am sure they will do the job I’m asking of them. At this price, if they last well for at least a year I’m sure I’ve gotten more than a bargain.Update: After having these for a week or two, I've decided I don't like them. I usually spend this much on ear buds and they hold up better; the left ear bud fades in and out as the wire moves. I also don't like how the microphone button does not have a volume control.Another con for me was that archaically big wire jack. It's in the way if you use these with your phone, so actually using your phone while having head phones in is difficult.Their one good feature is the comfort, but for me it isn't worth it.

title: Some design flaws...
body: Even though I rarely use earbuds and am extremely gentle with them, I find myself buying them about twice a year. These are replacing a set of Sony earbuds in a similar price range. Like the Sony pairs, they will not last long. The sound quality and overall functionality are also similar. These buds lack grommets where the wire enters and it's almost certain to be a failure point. Even the strain of pulling them out of the excessive packaging risks a short. The cable holds a coil very well and will likely never lay flat - having a cord constantly tickling my neck or cheek makes these much less comfortable than the cheap Sony devices. And lastly, the plastic cups that you stick into your ears are made from a gummy material that attracts small particles like dust and lint. I would not recommend using these devices for stow and go scenarios unless you carry them in a Ziploc or something. Otherwise, you're going to be introducing a LOT of foreign materials into your ear. Although the driver is ovoid, the little cups have round openings; swapping them out is tremendously difficult. The stock cup on one earbud I received had a tear in it and had to be replaced before use.

title: Headphones have changed
body: This was my second time ordering these because my first pair stopped working after some time. The first pair was great in sound quality didn’t have any problems with it until one side stopped working (typical) and I wasn’t expecting it to last long anyways. This second pair that just came I’ve used for a few days and it is not like the first one. The cords of the second pair they sent me are thinner compared to the first pair. When I touch and look at the two, it’s obvious which pair is more solid. My main and most ANNOYING issue is the second pairs microphone. The microphone is now to the left compared to the right but that’s not even the problem, just noting the difference. The microphone is triggering all types of commands on my device (pausing, fast forwarding, skipping songs, triggering voice control). At first I thought the problem was with my headphone jack on my phone but I’ve tried 2 other headphones and didn’t have any problem with them. With a little research I came to the conclusion that it’s an issue with the circuit in the microphone on the headphones. I just wish I could’ve gotten the first pair exactly the same again.Lasted a little over a month.

title: Feel "low quality" and uncomfortable fit.
body: I bought these on the recommendation of the wirecutter.com but I had a few issues with them. I can't speak to the quality of the sound as I'm not an audiophile and I use my headphones mainly as a headset and to listen to podcasts.The two issues I had with these headphones are that they felt "cheap". The plastic on the headphone themselves felt very thin and light, just low quality. They also had rough edges on some of the seams where the plastic was fused together, the same goes for the microphone/button section. The button itself didn't feel particularly "clicky" either.The other issue I had with these headphones was that they just didn't fit comfortably in my ears. They're designed with an angle from the actual tip that goes into your ear and the plastic that the tip is attached to that holds the driver. I could never get a comfortable fit. The piece that extended down from the driver that holds the cord would always rub uncomfortably against my tragus.These headphones may actually sound much better than any other headphones at this price point but I couldn't get past the discomfort and cheap feel of the headphones. YMMV.

title: Mediocre
body: I bought several pairs of earbuds for everyday use, all in this price range. These Panasonic buds are among my least-favorite. Every ear is different, so YMMV, but these just don't seem to fit mine well at all. This seems to be an issue with the angle, not with the interchangeable in-ear pads (as I have tried the various sizes and none solve my issues). I have another pair of angled earbuds that fit beautifully and are quite comfortable; these fall far short. The small-size pads fall out of my ears and utterly fail to block out ambient sounds; but the medium-size pads press uncomfortably if I wear them for more than about 20 minutes at a time, and worse yet, they still don't come close to blocking out ambient noise -- mostly because they fit my ears so poorly, leaving open space for noise to get in.I chose these in part because I had an old pair (like, 4 years old) of Panasonic earbuds that are still comfortable and working well, and I hoped these would live up to that standard and -- with the nifty angled-in approach -- even exceed it. Alas, they don't. These are going to the bottom of the "random electronics stuff" drawer, to be used only in desperation.

title: Terrible experience.
body: I had owned a $10 Sony headphones before one of the ear phones blew out on me after one year. I ordered this product for $6 as replacement after reading the good reviews of it. It's unfortunate that I didn't have the same experience. Off the bat after opening the package, I noticed that the sound quality wasn't as good as my previous Sony headphones. The lower bass and treble didn't sound as good as my Sony's, and it sounded to be a more mellow sound than a crisp sound like my Sony's. I just accepted that fact, and justified it with the price.After 1.5 weeks of continuous use (listening to music from my phone, watching movies on my laptop, etc.), one of the headphones blew out! I was surprised! I haven't even traveled with it, and it has been lying on my table for the whole time, so I ruled out damage right of the bat. Obviously the quality isn't there in these headphones, as they blew out very quickly.I gave it 2-stars because it worked fine for 1.5 weeks, and because of its cheap price.I have not idea why this has good reviews...Here's a warning to all buyers:You get what you paid for. Don't go for this product.

title: Just OK
body: I purchased a pair of these based on the fact that this item had so many reviews and the majority was positive.I listen to a lot of music and Ive owned my share of different in-ear-headphones, from Bose to klipsch and other brands in between. In the end they all break from wear and tear just because they get used so much.After reading all the reviews I decided to go with these and see if a cheaper pair would give me the same sound quality and endurance as some of the more expensive pairs I've owned.When I got these in the mail the only other pair I had left was the stock headphones that came with the Iphone 5. I started listening to different songs to see how good the sound quality was and was severely disappointed. They are not bad but overall the sound isn't anywhere near where it should be. The lows are not very warm and highs to pitchy. It all sounds very cheap. Even the stock iPhone headphones sounded way better than these.I'm giving them 2 stars because for the price you really cant complain too much but they are just not for me or anyone who truly wants to listen to their music the way it supposed to sound.

title: EDIT: Broke after 3 months!
body: Edit: These broke only after 3 months of using them, very disappointing. Amazing sound quality but terrible build quality as predicted.Oh my god are these amazing!My old pair of over-head Skullcandy's just broke and I needed a budget pair of earbuds or headphones.I decided to take the chance with these. When they arrived I tried them on and imminently noticed that EVERYTHING sounded better, as if these were a pair of really expensive earbuds. A world better than my old pair of headphones!I am not joking when I say that these have the same quality as expensive headphones, because they do. I tried on a pair of $80 over-ear headphones and they sounded the same as the Panasonic's!Pros: Great mids and highs! Very, very clear bass. Also gets pretty low too for those bass heads out there. (Me) Very comfy to wear, I could use these for hours! Everything sounds better with these on!Cons: Cheap cord that I feel could break off at any moment, just be careful.Great product, great price, you will NOT find anything better at this price! 100% worth the buy!

title: Get the 125's instead
body: When a headphone is the number one best seller on Amazon, your expectations are pretty high. But when you then see that they only cost about $7, your expectations probably go down quite a bit. Should they? Well, no. These Panasonic RP-HJE120's are among the best budget headphones you can get, though when compared to their slightly more expensive sibling, the [[ASIN:B00E4LGVUO RP-TCM125]]'s, their shortcomings are forced into the spotlight. In most aspects, they are very similar, but when it comes to sound, the 125's are far superior.The Good :)+ Incredibly comfortable, courtesy of Panasonic's Ergo-Fit design+ Surprisingly pleasant sound+ Powerful bass+ Good noise isolation+ Lots of colors!+ Small, low-profile design+ Dirt cheapThe Bad :(- Imbalanced, muddy sound- Lower-quality, tangle-prone cable- Lots of cable noise, wind noise, etc.The Quirky :/~ If worn improperly, they will sound horribleStill wondering why I recommend the 125's over the 120's? Read on to find out! -- Sound Quality --Headphones are, first and foremost, a means of producing sound; thus, the quality of said sound is one of the most important aspects of a headphone. At this price point, expectations are already pretty low, so these will have no problem impressing listeners with powerful bass and immersive sound. At first listen, most people will think these sound fantastic, but when compared with a more precise pair, namely the [[ASIN:B000065BPB Sennheiser HD 280 Pro]] over-ear studio headphones, the 120's shortcomings become increasingly noticeable. This pair, in addition to enhancing the bass, also scales back the treble, making for a skewed, unnatural, muddy sound signature. When I first tried these out, I thought they were fantastic, because the best headphones I had ever heard were my [[ASIN:B008DWJ81E SOL Republic Tracks]], which have a very similarly skewed signature. Treble instruments and vocals sound reasonably realistic, but are overpowered by the massive bass response from these headphones. If you love powerful bass from the likes of SOL Republic and Beats, these may be a good choice. But for everyone else, your money is better spent on the RP-TCM125's, which still offer impactful bass response that is more prominent than an unexciting studio pair, but also have clear, crisp treble response, making for a mostly balanced and definitely enjoyable sound signature. Plus, they come with a 1-button remote and mic and are still very cheap at just over $10 on Amazon (at the writing of this review). -- It's worth noting that the rest of the review also applies to the RP-TCM125's, so if you've already read my review of that pair, you'll miss nothing by skipping to the conclusion. -- Comfort and Design --Moving on to the fit, one of the major strengths of this pair, these sport Panasonic's Ergo-Fit design, which is just a fancy way of saying that the in-ear part, instead of being circular (as most in-ear headphones are), is elliptical. It's one of those "well, duh" innovations; it's a super simple change but it makes a world of difference. Since humans' outer ear canal is taller than it is wide, an elliptical design makes it fit a lot better than a circular design without the need to get a custom-molded earpiece. Until I tried these headphones, all in-ears I had tried required excessive force to put in, were uncomfortable while they were in, and were constantly trying to escape my ears. By contrast, Ergo-Fit headphones are easy to put in, are incredibly comfortable while they're in, and are generally quite good at staying in their place.It's worth mentioning here that the "In-Ear" in the product name is important; if these are not literally *in* your ears, they'll sound horrendous. The sound is designed to be the way it is when it has the benefit of a seal so it only has to fill the tiny cavity in your ear with sound. Without that seal, you'll still be able to hear stuff, but it will be tinny and completely devoid of any bass or low-mid response. If it doesn't feel like you're wearing earplugs, you're doing it wrong.By virtue of the in-ear design, they block out a good deal of outside noise and keep your tunes in your own ears. Sound leakage into the outside world is minimal; don't expect anyone to hear your tunes unless you listen at incredibly loud, unsafe levels. It's also nice that they come in a variety of colors, and all the colors I've owned or otherwise seen in person (orange, red, purple, blue, green, black, and white) look good. They're also incredibly convenient by virtue of their small size and incredibly low price!Keep in mind, however, internal sounds (chewing, etc.) are amplified dramatically. So, if you want something you can listen to while eating, you should probably get a pair of on-ear or over-ear headphones. And if you use them for running, it may be worth it to invest in a higher-end pair that is designed with a more secure fit. -- Build --Of course, "dirt cheap" usually means they cut back in some area. Well, if anything, the build would be mildly disappointing. The cable isn't very robust and, at times, can be very prone to tangling. Thankfully, major tangles are few and far between. Some other areas of weakness are cable noise and wind noise, but these have more to do with in-ear headphones in general than these ones specifically. -- Conclusion --I still stand by my initial impression that they are worth a lot more than their price tag suggests. But when it comes to sound, bassheads are likely to thoroughly enjoy these, while everybody else will be better served with the 125's.You can read my review of the RP-TCM125's here: http://www.amazon.com/review/R1JQ2JEIVVBQMB/ref=cm_cr_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUO

title: Get the JVC Gumy headphones instead!
body: **TL/DR BELOW****UPDATED REVIEW BELOW**I just have to say WOW, I had to spread the word on these guys. I am super impressed with these earbuds. If you told me that I could have a pair of earbuds that sounded great, did not distort any sound, were comfortable, looked decent, and they were only 8 dollars, I WOULD SLAP YOU AND CALL YOU A LIAR!! I would be severely mistaken however (and arrested), as I am dead wrong.I had read reviews on a couple of different websites that these were the El Dorado of earbuds under 50 dollars. Some were so brave as to exclaim that these were the best earbuds under 100 dollars. I then found 33,000 reviews on Amazon with the overwhelming majority saying that these are THE best cheap earbud to get. These brash claims caught my attention, and as a gadget guru and headphone enthusiast, I had no choice but to succumb to the lavish price of 8 dollars.For those interested in a breakdown: these come with three sets of eartips, which I had to play with because I could not find the right size at first. Making sure you have the right size ear tips in is CRUCIAL, because it will form a seal around your ear and you will be able to isolate and block external noise better, hear the music louder and clearer, and the bass will be more prominent. I have had these for a week so far and have used them for a multitude of things. I’ve watched movies, listened to music while mowing the lawn (these were sufficient at for drowning out most noise), worked out with them, and used them for playing games. The best things I would recommend them for are for just casually listening to music and watching movies. This is my general test on how good headphones are: Play Rage Against the Machine and see if I can get pumped up on the bass, then play I Saw Her Standing There by The Beatles and see if I can hear George Harrison twanging away on his guitar over Paul McCartney’s overpowering bass. These definitely beat a lot of over the ear headphones that I own. The sound is balanced and I could hear everything almost exactly like the original audio recording. They also had a reasonable audio level and got as loud as I needed them before I hit the highest setting.I would not recommend these for working out, as they just kept falling out of my ears and distracting me while I was running.I also would say that these are not an acceptable headphone for FPS or multiplayer gaming, as you really need an over the ear set to amp up all the noise in the game and help give you directional listening. I could not pinpoint footsteps / etc. with these earbuds as well as with my Audio Technica and Polk Headsets. Also, you need a decent mic for voice chat and this model does not have a mic. Generally though, these are worth it for everything else mentioned.So, here I am. Telling you to get these. Get them now. Don’t hesitate.**TL/DR: 8 Dollar headphones, why you not buy?****PRODUCT UPDATE 08/14/16** 5 stars dropped to 3What am I doing up at 1247am on a Sunday? One may answer that I am at a club having a great time, or watching a movie with my fiance, or having a sleepover with my family, but the answer to that question is none of the above. I am awake at this hour as your sworn headphone avenger, to tell you not to buy these headphones. You should buy these headphones instead: [[ASIN:B005K925UO JVC HAFX5B Gumy Plus Inner Ear Headphones -Black]]Why? Because I was a bit over zealous on my initial review. At the time of writing the original review, I had not found a suitable alternative for a cheap and reasonably decent sounding pair of headphones. But - just like all politicians, at some point when you make a public statement you have to back track when you're mouth was moving faster than your brain.I bought the JVC Gumy headphones from Best Buy a few weeks ago and I was intending to test them side by side with the Panasonic Ergofits. I noticed some immediate improvements that the JVCs had over the Panasonics. One was that the gummy seals for your ear are a lot more comfortable. secure, and provide a much better seal than the Panasonics did. The next improvement was the mic and control button(which the upgraded ergofit has, but you shouldn't go without it). The most important feature that the JVCs have over the Panasonic is its performance. When I listened to One More Time by Daft Punk on both headphones, the Panasonics were very level all around and there was nothing spectacular that stood out. When I used the JVCs, my ears exploded with delight from the much better bass response it had. I have been using the JVCs for everything since then and I haven't looked back.

title: Price is right, sound is OK, kind of dull, lackluster, had bigger expectations.
body: I ordered these because I unintentionally ripped out the wires from the 3.5mm plug on my set of Skullcandy S2INCZ-033.Honestly I thought the Skullcandy sounded significantly better, and were louder too. Those totally and instantly impressed me when I first got them, comparing them to the ones which came with my Sony NWZS545BLK . I used these stock Sony plugs in the interim while these Panasonics were shipping, and while the Panas sound better, it is not the significant better sound the skull candys impressed upon me.These don't sound bad, but they do not have the low thumping bass or the crisp trebles of the Skull Candys. It is like a multiuband eq with all the mid ranges up and all the highs and lows attenuated.They were delivered 2 days after I broke the Skullcandy's. They sound like there is a thin attenuating barrier between them and my ear, and certain guitar riffs or female voices to not get that penetration that tickles the inside of my skull and could force this mysterious water like substance from my eyes that my former buds could.My Music ranges from Mozart to Operation Ivy to Parov Stelar, and these buds just can't make me tear up, mosh, or shake it like an 'x'ing person on a dancefloor. The SC's could. The lack of volume is probably good for my long term hearing, but sometimes I need that almost painful level to coax the emotion, and these do not have that little extra Oomph.I always preferred the 'no equalizer' with the SC's with most music, but this setting just sounds incredibly dull with these headphones, but none of the preset eq settings can tickle the inside of my skull, nor do they seem to react the same way to changes as did as with the SC's, nor can I tune in the thumping bass or crisp trebles I was used to on the manual 5 band EQ.Not obvious in the photo of these Panasonics is that the rubber buds are offset to the body of the plug and are labeled L and R. I think this design is superior and certainly fits nicer in my ear than the skull candy's which could be inserted into either ear. I never thought the SC's fit poorly, I just noticed these fit better and tugs on the cord will not draw them from my ear as easily. I think the Skull Candy's reduced ambient noise to a greater degree as well, and this might have saved the life of My landlord's skreeching parakeet, may it die a slow painful annoying death soon. Freaking useless animal which fills me with rage. I daydream of killing it with a sledge hammer.The wires themselves are thinner than the SC's and do not have the sliding constrictor to hold the 2 individual leads closer together around my neck, or tight together for wrapping them up for storage, a feature which i liked a lot on the SC's. These thinner wires to not transfer as much rubbing noises to the headphones as the SC's however. The 3.5mm jack has a 90 degree bend on it. Not sure how I feel about that. I think it will stress the plug in the MP3 player itself more, and that is how my last 3 units failed internally.For under 7$ I'm not complaining, much, but the reviews had me expecting a slightly improved sound over my previous headphones, and I'm not finding that true in any degree, and I will be looking for an improvement over both in my next pair in the 20 to 35$ range. I want to see what this Sennheiser buzz is all about.

title: Not bad, but a long shot shy of great.
body: I'm puzzled by the overwhelming praise of these. Sure they're very inexpensive, but they're also really cheap. I suppose at this price point, the majority of the 5 star reviewers are unlikely to be used to the sound and quality of much better products. I guess I should admit I've used a variety of much more expensive ones including Etymotic, Shure and Ultimate Ears. But I still think there is some sort of weird group-think dynamic happening here. So I offer up my much more critical review.I guess some are impressed by lots of bass, but the bass from these is honestly muddy and there's too just much of it. There's no tightness or clarity in the low end- it's like the 80hz bass knob is cranked to the max.
