As for the rest of the frequency range, they are fully acceptable, and I'd go so far as to say they're good, especially for the $.But overall, aside from one or two sets of ear buds I've tried which were totally unacceptable, these pale in comparison just about everything else I've used. My old favorite budget buds are an old pair of discontinued Sony MDR EX70's I believe those were in the mid $30 range a few years back. The Sony's have strong bass, but it is clean. They've lasted me over 10 years. I recently picked up a pair of MEElectronics M6-CL-MEE (as I write this $16.99, I believe I paid closer to $12.99) which blow these out of the water in nearly every way- much cleaner and much more balanced top to bottom. They also offer much better construction, come with a zippered carrying case with a compartment for the extra ear buds, have a clothing clip and a sliding binder to keep them tangle free, and tight on the back of your head (they wrap over the ear to keep them in place, IEM / sport style). The cord on the MEE's is much better quality and more tangle resistant. The cord on these Panasonic's is about as cheap as I've seen- it reminds me of the disposable ones you get on airplanes. These do fit fairly well and are easier to insert than the MEE's, but the hard plastic angled body is a bit uncomfortable against my ears. The smaller Sony buds are so soft and comfortable that I can sleep on my side with them in and barely feel them. It'd be silly compare these to any of the $100 plus models I have, except to say that none of them is perfect either (two are a little light on bass, one is fantastic sounding but I can't wear them for more than an hour).Some have said these are the best of the super cheap buds, and I can accept that. If you tear up or lose your buds frequently, these might be the ticket. But if you are looking for good sound, quality, and can spend a little more, you'd probably be better off.Given the moderately decent sound at such a cheap price point, I'd probably give them one more star if they had a better cord that didn't tangle so easily, the packaging (welded blister-pack which was quite difficult to cut open) didn't suck so bad. Also changing rubber tips stinks on these- much harder than any other bud I've used.

title: Good quality to price ratio... Only because it's so cheap
body: To give some background, I am a college student who uses headphones while doing brainless tasks (vacuuming, making flashcards, etc.). I do not consider myself an audiophile, primarily because I look for more "fun" sounding headphones than neutral and accurate headphones. In my past experience I had the Grado SR-80i (which I returned), Creative Aurvana Live!, and the Klipsch S4i.I was looking for a warmer sounding headphones, with a slight emphasis on bass/treble (like the Creative Aurvana Live!). These headphones seemed like they had the sound profile I was looking for at a fantastic price.I bought the orange ones from Panasonic's website. I was pleased with the large color selection and the ridiculously low price (7 bucks with free shipping!).The fit is very nice (it is, after all, called "Ergofit"). I am pretty average in every physical regard (foot size, height, ear size, etc.), so I think I can speak for most people. Panasonic provides small, medium, and large tips, so most everybody should be able to get a nice fit.With a nice fit, there is a lot of sound isolation. If you like to be aware of your surroundings while you're listening to music, I would suggest earbuds that don't have silicone tips.The build quality is what I expected for 7 dollars. With a thin rubber cord and no extra features, these headphones are comparable to any other pair of budget ear buds. The headphones get tangled very easily, and I honestly don't know how I should store these. You can expect to have a mess anytime you coil/crumple it into a pocket. The ear pieces themselves feel quite nice to me, primarily because of its ergonomic design.In terms of sound, these are surprisingly good. I say "surprisingly" only because they're seven bucks. Ignoring its low price, these will sound okay to most people. I personally don't like them too much. These headphones generally have the sound profile I was looking for, but it's not very refined (expected of budget headphones). The sound is prominent and vibrant, but that's only because it has a lot of bass/treble (too much for my tastes). The bass/treble is a bit loose for me, and these headphones muddle the music I listen to (EDM, house, remixes, etc.). This is a problem especially for music that have vocals and electronic instrumentals simultaneously. The songs become really muddled, and I wish there was a way to adjust the EQ.From glowing reviews from Cnet (4/5 stars) and Amazon (almost 5/5 stars), I expected these headphones to be on par with the Klipsch SR4i. Perhaps I was too ambitious. They are only good for the price, and I wouldn't recommend these as primary headphones. I'm overall satisfied with these headphones, but I feel a little let down by the online reviews. These are, at best, back up headphones.

title: The Best of Both Worlds
body: The Best of Both WorldsFor comparison purposes, I bought these earbuds along with the Panasonic RPHJE355K buds. I figured whichever pair was the better set would be my everyday set and the other set would be my spare set or I could give them to one of my sons.I didn't like either pair but I found a solution that has me satisfied. I tried the RPHJE120K's first.These are not 5 star earbuds, and not quite 4 stars but they are definitely a good value. They probably are better than whatever stock earbuds came with your device. I could see myself using these everyday and being satisfied, however when I tried the 355's I knew I could not live with the 120's as my everyday set.My primary test song was "December" by Collective Soul. It has a wide dynamic range, good mix of instruments and several instances of bass drum punch and cymbals with which to put the earbuds through their high and low frequency paces. I also fiddled with the equalizer settings on my mobile device. (Google music player on Moto Razr Maxx HD.) FYI, for most music listening I leave the equalizer off but used it to test these earbuds.Using the medium sized earpiece these buds have decent bass and adequate treble. As others have reported, using the large size earpiece the bass really comes out (and I like good bass in an earbud.) However, while they had good amounts of bass, especially with the large ear tips, I would characterize the bass as being slightly muddy. I would also say that the high end notes are not fully realized and lack the clarity of the 355's. Overall I would say these earbuds are decent but lack crispness and clarity.The 355's were almost the opposite. They had very crisp and clear notes but inadequate bass. I would characterize these buds as being bright in sound color and lacking in bass. I'm tempted to use the word "tinny" but the high notes were full and reproduced well, if a bit overemphasized. Using the large sized earpieces improved the bass but only marginally. I'm happy to report the bass was clear, not muddy like the 120's, but there wasn't enough of it. Still not satisfied with either set.Then came the eureka moment--why not put the large ear tips from the 120's onto the 355's? I did that and found my everyday set of earbuds! Good, clear bass and bright, crisp (but not overly emphasized) high notes. The best of both worlds.So why not buy the 355's and a set of replacement earbud tips in size large? Well, the replacement tips are around $5.00 so for another 2 bucks you could have the 120's, the large tips that come with it and a backup set of earbuds.In summary, I would give the 120's 3 1/2 stars and the 355's 4 stars. Using the 355's with the large tips from the 120's I give my hybrid set 5 stars.

title: WARNING: the plug may not work with your phone case
body: WARNING: the plug may not work with your phone casepros: okay, i am not an audiophile. i've owned mostly cheap earbuds in the past. i did have a pair of senheisser's i bought for 20 bucks here on amazon that were pretty decent. the sound on these seems pretty much as good as any i've heard. the default set of ear "pads" were a little large - they are not as soft and pliable as others i've used. i switched to the smaller size and now they fit in my ears fine. i agree with the reviews that stated that if you are having problems with tinny sound, they are probably seated incorrectly in your ear. i noticed a big difference when i switched to the smaller size. initially i was concerned that because they are soft but not AS soft as other buds i've owned, they wouldn't be comfortable, but now that i'm wearing the smaller size i think they are more comfortable, because they are not constantly putting pressure on the inside of my ears, which over many hours (i wear them constantly) was giving me a headache. These don't do that. I also tend to go to sleep with them in my ears, to block out noise on my block, and unlike others i've owned these are often still seated when i wake up.they work very well blocking external noise.CONS: the biggest disappointment in these buds is the size of the plug. since it's not pictured i forgot to check. the plug is large and blocky, and i had to remove the case on my iphone 5c to plug the buds in. so unless your case has a HUGE opening over the earphone jack, consider that you will either have to modify it if you can, or remove it to use these earbuds.another small point to consider, which is not really a con, is that these earbuds are designed with a flat area of plastic outside the buds that extends over part of the immediately surrounding earlobe. this element of the design is helpful in getting the right and left sides correct, since the buds have to match right or left ears to fit. but, if you are in a hurry to get them seated as i sometimes am, or if you are fumbling with them in the dark as i often am, it can be a little frustrating. the benefit is, for multimedia especially, it can be more enjoyable to have the orientation correct. when i'm watching a movie and something is moving across the screen while creating sound, it's nice to get the "surround sound" effect accurately. for music i'm not sure it really matters.

title: Great sound for the price, but longevity is an issue.
body: (Updated at bottom )Well, here's my dime and sixpence on these superlative eight dollar earbuds, which I've put to the test compared to other more expensive phones, a couple MUCH more expensive, but there goes what you term 'experience' .... They're more precise in the midrange than the Koss, which I highly admire, despite sounding overall rather 'analog' . Bass is very prominent and articulate, and if you've a portable without bass enhancement such as the popular but overrated bestseller , current-poor Sony SRF-M37W, they'll do you fine, a pleasant match. Highs dry, very present for fun, satisfactory. Equalizer curve on my vintage Sony 818 mp3 player looks planar with a 'lip' at the high end. LOTS of power handling at 200mw max; Google 200mw max input to see what the contenders are in power handling. Sensitivity at 96db/, but sounds feeble? It isn't. Frequency response 'only' 20 to 20 ? It's all there, way beyond your hearing capacity. Panasonic have done a fine job of tweaking these buds for actual human ears, not for anechoic chambers - they're fun to listen to and you find yourself pushing the volume up and up. Are they better than SHURE SE530 ? Different league, way better ; Sennheiser HD280 pro, 64ohm ? Yes , period. Listen to Philip Glass's very serious 'Anthem, pt 2', at full whack, and you might recognize some of the brutal truth of Klipsch towers there. Okay, I'm going over the top for a pair of eight dollar earbuds. But that's just how startling they are for the price.Hope this helps, for the money you would have spent on a pair of Klipsch s4 buy a couple as spares, don't know about durability as of yet, but just in case. On sonic character , they sound more or less like the Klipsch S4 , bright, clear, exciting, but which came to bits after a couple of months of admitted fun, but when shipped from Brazil to Klipsch at considerable exportation expense never heard from them, and will never buy from them again either.Update , 4 Nov 2016Right-side bud crapped out after 4 months use , which is pretty miserable for the careful usage which the buds were submitted to , and hence the product has lost two of its stars. I'm into my second pair , which is worth another two stars. Since I initially got three pairs, if the third pair craps out also prematurely Panasonic will be owing me a star , and a letter of apology !

title: Great budget headphones with a finicky jack.
body: Looked to buy some cheap but good sounding ear buds when my previous pair stopped working (some beats in-ear headphones I got from my brother - overpriced for what they were). I only use ear buds for a couple of hours a day so I don't need anything super fancy, just something with decent sound at a low price. In the sound department, these earphones deliver, with a catch. I wasn't blown away by the sound by any means, but they sound as good if not better than the beats I had been using. I was especially surprised with the accuracy and fullness of the bass. They really do sound good, not amazing, but good... better than expected anyway. They also fit comfortably in my ear, which is a plus.So why 3 stars? The problem lies with the headphone jack itself. I'm not exactly sure what the problem is, but I get rich, full sound out of these ear buds if and only if the jack is positioned in that magical position that allows for that sound. The slightest wiggle or turn of the jack and the lower end completely drops out... no bass whatsoever. It's easy for me to understand all of the negative reviews mentioning a tinny sound with no bass. That's exactly what I hear when the jack isn't positioned just so. My suspicion is that other reviewers received headphones with a defective jack or had a device that didn't work well with the jack. I had better luck plugging them into my laptop than my phone. The real test, however, will be how they function with my computer at work, since that is the only time I'll really be using them. Only time will tell.I believe they will work just fine for my needs (listening while at a desk at work). However, any movement, even walking, might bump the jack out of that magical position. There's also the likelihood that certain devices may work better with the jack than others. I've seen other reviewers mention that they use these while running, which I certainly cannot foresee working well with my device, but yours may be different. I have a different brand that I use while working out, so these won't be used during any sort of activity where I'm moving around.The verdict? They really do sound great, especially considering the $6 price tag. I think it's definitely worth rolling the dice on these if you plan on using them while stationary.

title: Counterfeits?
body: I've noticed some other reviewers writing warnings about counterfeit products, due to them receiving products with varying levels of quality. I too have experienced this. I ordered my first two pairs of earbuds in November 2018 and April 2019, both from the Amazon.com Services Inc. vendor, and they were of excellent quality.I then ordered another pair in August from a third party vendor (a very highly rated vendor, mind you). But the quality of this third pair was far lower than the first two. I brought this to the attention of Amazon and was given a replacement pair. The replacement pair (from same vendor) was also very low quality. Contacting Amazon again, they offered me a full refund and told me to just keep the earbuds.About a month later in September, I decided to try one more time (I really love these earbuds). Having realized that the "counterfeit" earbuds came from a third-party vendor, I decided to order again from Amazon.com Services Inc. This was the vendor that I originally ordered from. This time, the pair I received were excellent quality.Now, I'm not completely certain that the lower quality pair were counterfeits. It could easily be that Panasonic contracts many different manufacturing plants to make these earbuds. Some of these plants might have different quality control standards, and this third party vendor (which was very highly rated) might've gotten a bad batch. I did notice a slight difference on the packaging between the earbuds. On the back of the packaging, all the way down at the bottom, there is a four character code. The code for the good pair is 8I1H. The code for the bad pair is 8G3H. My guess is that these codes are either identifying the batch number, or the plant that they came from.That is all the information that I have. I hope this can help others figure out what is going on. My advice to anyone considering buying these earbuds is to buy two or three pairs from two or three different vendors, compare the different qualities (It will be obvious which pairs are "real" and which ones are "counterfeits"), and return the lower quality pair(s). They are fantastic earbuds if you can get a good pair.

title: Whoa! Slow down.
body: If you have found your way here the same way I did, through the Wirecutter review of earphones under $30 claiming that these headphones are the best sounding headphones in the price range, then please, control your expectations.I am an avid fan of a wide range of podcasts and I practically collect inexpensive earbuds because, not only do I wear them a lot, I damage and lose them a lot. I have more expensive, better headphones, but for day-to-day use a pair of headphones like these Panasonics are right in the price range of what I use for most of my listening.I read the Wirecutter's review of these and bought a pair to see what I have been missing. The answer; nothing. They are a generally acceptable pair of cheap earbuds with a microphone. I compared them to a couple of other similarly inexpensive earbuds and found that they are not only not better, in some regards they were worse.As someone who rarely (never) reviews products on Amazon, I felt it was necessary to try to bring some perspective to the discussion. These headphones are fine. For the price, they are a good choice. Wirecutter's review made them sound like they are far more than they are regarding their sound"The Panasonic won because they sounded the best. Hands down. They were everyone’s top choice in terms of sound fidelity. They have a nice overall balance with airy, mellow highs and present-but-not-dominating bass. They sound just as good listening to acoustic guitar as they do hip hop and rock. Nothing pierces, nothing muddies: every frequency plays well with the others. And as you’ll see with other contenders later, that’s not often the case in this price range."When considered alongside their lackluster build quality (from the Wirecutter "their plasticky design won’t be winning any awards for beauty or construction") I can say that I will not be purchasing another pair of these. I have been through lots of other sub-$30... actually, sub-$20 as is my rule for day-to-day wear headphones... that sound as good, if not better, and have a better build quality.

title: Not bad
body: I go to the gym, cycle, run, etc - about 5-6 days a week, so I go through headphones fairly often, or they end up lost or damaged. I am a little spoiled as the TJ Maxx near me sells Skully Candy head phones for 7.99 that I have been very happy with. But it's located in a very traffic heavy part of town I have not gotten too recently (nor do I want to around the holidays). So these were close in price and a name brand, so I figured I would give them a shot.I first put them in an they sounded kind of trebley and tinny, and I instantly new what the issue was (another review hit it right on the head in a longer explanation). The biggest size of ear buds these come with don't seal my ear canal. With the sizes shipped, I could hear way to much of what was going on around me at the gym and not enough bass. That seal you get when when you put them in the ear is when you get more full sound. I swapped just the buds with another set and they actually are not at all bad.They have a little odd disc shape thing that sits in your ear to help keep them in, and the work well.For my ears, anyway, the weren't going anywhere - which ended up being a good thing since the buds were too small Then again, with buds that fit properly, I do not usually have a problem with them falling out anyway.The only other thing I didn't like is they have the connector at a right angle, and it's quite bulky and square shaped. It just barely fits into the area allowed for by my iPhone case. So if you have a larger more protective case- as many active people using iPhones might- this could be an issue for you (and I think they actually would be a pain for the arm band case I use for running outdoors).I guess if I had been paying 15-20 dollars for head phones, I would be more satisfied with these for this price. I have definitely paid more for worse. With the proper fit, they really aren't at all bad. But given the ear buds not fitting me and to a less extent, the jack being large and right angled, I don't think I would order these again. Hence the 3 stars.

title: Its nothing spectacular. It works. Fits securely in the ear.....but not that comfortable to me.
body: I bought this to replace an iPhone 5 earbud that I accidentally pinched in a car door. I had high expectations with all the rave reviews I've read on Amazon. Now that I've had a chance to use this Panasonic earphone I've come to the conclusion that most of the 5-star ratings is mainly due to its price right around $7. At this price, I don't know if you can do better than this. Of course, if you have more money to spend then you can find much, much, much better quality than this. The sound quality is definitely a notch or two lower than the IPhone 5/5s earbuds. In fact, the Panasonic is not in the same league. But then again, this is only $7. So buyers, lower your expectations on this headphone.The earbuds fit inside my ears more securely than the iPhone earbuds. However, after a while it starts to bother my ears. I can wear the iPhone earbuds all night (in fact I fall asleep with them on). But the Panasonic earbuds cause pain in my ears after about 30 minutes. Of course, this may be different for other folks' ears. Yes, the Panasonic comes with 3 different size earbud attachments. Chances are that one of the three earbuds will be comfortable to 70%, 80%, or maybe 90% of the general public. But for me, its not comfortable. Definitely fits securely in the ear, but not comfortable.Also, I can hear movement coming from the earbud cable and earpiece. What I mean by this is, when I have these earbuds on and I'm doing some task such as walking, I can hear the cable bouncing around and the earpiece moving slightly inside my ears. Its very annoying. Again, I don't have this problem with my iPhone earbuds. I think I will dish out more money and go back to the iPhone earbuds.

title: Nice sound quality and fit, but....
body: Okay I like these earphones. I have had bad luck with other earphones giving up (usually one side goes silent) in shockingly short time periods. These have lasted now for months. The sound quality is very good and the fit is superb. I especially like the eccentric shape so you will always know which is right and left even in the dark. But...What makes me give this just three stars seems a bit trivial, but the aggravation that occurs every time I use these makes me not interested in buying them again, should they eventually fail. What bugs me is the stiffness of the wire. Months after unwrapping them and using them several times a day, the stiff plastic insulation material still retains the bends and kinks from packaging. What this does (the wire's stiffness I mean) is make them become a tangled mess no matter how careful you are in putting them in your pocket. I like to listen to audio books for brief amounts of time during the day at work when i can, so I don't have a lot of time to waste. Pull these out for ten minutes of listening pleasure and the things are a mess of tangled wire, forcing me to spend several minutes trying to get them untangled. This is so frustrating!One small plastic slider on the wire, like other earphones have, would solve this problem. Why these do not have it is a really mystery to me.EDIT: Okay it has been two years, and i feel a bit better about these ear buds. They have lasted for a long time, longer than any earbuds I have ever had. The fit is very comfortable. The tangling cord issue, while upset me at first, I just leaned to live with. So there you go, 3 star review now a 4 star. I would and probably will buy them again.

title: Good functionality, while it lasts
body: I bought this primarily to use for calls from my cell phone (old motorola running gingerbread). I'll review it for a few features that I think people look for in such ear phones1) Sound Quality: Quite good for the money. I'm no audiophile, and I wasn't look for excellent highs or mids. For the little music that I listen to from my phone (classic rock and pop), the sound quality was highly satisfactory.2) Comfort: Excellent. They would stay in my ears and not fall off. The fit quite snugly and provide decent noise isolation without being uncomfortable.3) Call quality: Mic and speakers were highly satisfactory. An incoming call will also ring in the ear phones (if music was playing, it gets paused) and can be answered by pressing the one button near the microphone. Pressing this button also ends the call, starts/stops music when not on a call. I'm guessing this feature can be better supported by the software on the phone ( my phone did not treat long presses any differently from a short press or a double press).4) durability: Low. I've had them for about 9 months now and I was quite happy with the product for half a year or so. Then, there were sporadic noises and disturbances that would require me to pinch the soldering where the wire joins the stereo pin. Now, its almost fully dead. That is, the mic and the earphones seem electrically isolated from the pin that connects to the phone... I might get it to work for a few milliseconds if I fidget the wires around, but I'll have to toss these.Overall, this product set me back by ~$14 and lasted for 9 months. I'm not too happy/sad with it. I'm trying other products to replace this though.

title: Decent inexpensive Earbuds
body: I will compare these Panasonic RP-TCM125 earbuds to my Philips PRO6105 earbuds that are available for the same price on Amazon ($19).These Panasonic earbuds have decent sound and are comfortable to wear. The sound quality is on par with the Philips earbuds, but the Philips are a little bit louder. Both have extended frequency range with 10Hz-24KHz for the Panasonic and 7Hz-40KHz for the Philips. The extended frequency range doesn't do much for my old and abused ears, but younger users may hear the difference.The mic/switch is on the right ear for the Panasonic and left ear for the Philips. The switch on the Panasonic feels cheap whereas the Phillips switch feels like a much better quality design.The plug for the Panasonic is a right angle and the Phillips is a straight plug. The right angle plug of the Panasonic earbuds didn't interfere with my thick cellphone case, so that is a good thing because I have had issues with other right angle plugs in the past. The plug on the Panasonic is nickle plate whereas the Philips have a gold plate plug. The wires on the Panasonic are vinyl coated and have the typical molded plastic strain relief. The wires on the Philips are more like rubber and have a rubber strain relief... the Philips wires quickly lost their memory shape after removing from the package, the Panasonic wires needed to be massaged by reverse bending the wires to get them to lay flat.Overall for the price of these Panasonic's being the same as the Philips, I would pass on these and get the Philips PRO6105 earbuds. The Panasonic earbuds sound good, but the manufacturing quality is not as nice as the Philips.

title: Inexpensive, long cord, sounds pretty good.
body: The earbuds are angled and are directional. They fit in the wrong ear, but feel funny of that happens. I put a small dab of White-Out on the left cord so I know which is which when I pick them up to use.The cord is very long. I can have my phone or iPod in my pants pocket and use these. But I usually clip an iPod Nano or Sony SBH-20 Bluetooth adapter to my shirt and run the wires under the shirt and around the back of my collar. I'll tuck the buds in my shirt at work and pull one or both out for music or phone calls. Even after tying a loop at the Y to relieve stress (that's where most of my earbuds die, at the Y connector) I still have the buds hanging low under my shirt, taking a few more seconds to fish them out than some others I have had. At least the cables are not rubbery and get caught on things like some Sennheisers I've had.The 3.5mm jack's plastic is squared off instead of round, and I found they don't plug into the bottom of my Nano 4th gen if it's plugged into the dock connector, though I found few aftermarket buds that will. Also, no microphone, but I use a $30 Sony SBH20 bluetooth adapter that has a mic, headphone jack and play controls for calls and music on my phone with any earbuds I want.They are fine for podcasts and phone calls, and OK for music, with more bass than clarity. They do a decent job of blocking out ambient noise. As a cheap pair to get you through the day or as a backup to toss in a gear bag, they will do the job. Good value, especially compared to cheap Skullcandy buds, but 3 stars for the cables being too long for me and just OK music quality. Though I would probably buy them again.

title: Too much hype...
body: People make these earbuds sound greater than they actually are. I am generally price conscious and do my research to choose the best earbuds within a certain price bracket. I'm no audiophile and don't own any Sennheisers or whatever, but I can compare them to other budget level earbuds in the $20-$40 range. The last two pairs I ordered were made by Denon and I couldn't be happier with the sound quality, based on the price I paid. These are priced very low and have great reviews, but unfortunately, I can't agree with the majority on this...Sound: The bass level is acceptable, not overpowering, not distorted, but good, I think. The mids/voices are fairly clear, but sound a but underpowered. The highs are nearly non existent with these buds; the only way to describe it is that it sounds like i'm listening to my music behind a glass window. The sound is okay, but the lacks a "crispness" overall, which is fitting for an $8 pair of earphones.Fitment: The odd shape of the earphone actually makes for a very tight, comfortable fit. I have trouble keeping some earbuds in my ear while running, but these will probably stay nice and snug for the duration of a run, with minimal adjusting. I haven't tried them for that yet, but it's the impression that I get.Overall: If you are looking at something in this price range, you are probably looking at a backup pair of earphones that you're willing to let get lost or damaged. These would fit the bill nicely for a backup pair, but the sound quality is a bit of a turnoff. Unless price is your only driving factor, I suggest looking elsewhere for a fair priced bud with decent sound.

title: OK earphones at this low price
body: These earbuds are nothing special. Their best attribute is that they are cheap. The sound from them is nothing special. In comparison to my standard white IPod buds these seem to emphasize the higher frequencies. There seems to be nothing happening in the bass range with these. I don't think they are efficient enough to have much bass at all. To be fair, my IPod buds overly emphasize bass so they are not that accurate either. I also compared these to a pricy Sennheiser set that I've owned for a couple of years and let's just say there is a reason why the Sennheisers cost more.Surely these Panasonic buds are very nice to use if you think they might get damaged somehow in use as it will not break your heart to have to replace them. These might be especially useful if you only listen to talk radio or have high frequency hearing loss. If you really like to listen to the most of your music and you must wear earbuds then these are not for you.I also have to note that these are packaged in a difficult to open blister pack that required several passes with utility shears to break into. I hate to see all the plastic that has to be discarded just to get this product out of the package.Cosmetically, they are OK. They don't hurt my ears and are comfortable to me. They come with a couple of other size ear pieces if you are hard to fit. The wires take a set when folded so they are not very well-behaved and don't coil nicely and lie flat when you put them away. These will be a little bird's nest of tangled wires when you set them aside.

title: Good for the cost. Far from great.
body: Decent earbuds for $9. Note, these are earBUDS. Thus your experience with them depends wholly upon the fit of the silicon buds in your own ears. These happen to fit me very well. I have small ears, if the housing were even 1mm bigger, they would be painful to wear.Pros:- Mids come out fairly clearly.- Lows are clear, too clear. (hip hop market?)- The cord doesn't cause booming noises like a braided cord would.- the mic works well and is well positioned.- they don't fall out of my ears (but this really only applies to me).- angled connector.Cons:- the cord likes to tie itself in knots when I'm not looking.- the freq band is not flat.- Lows are too prominent. It's annoying for some tracks, and definitely annoying for movies.- Highs are completely muffled and non existent. I use these strictly for gym/running, so I can use my droid's equalizer to correct this fairly effectively (but this does kill my phone battery). I can't watch movies etc. with these headphones, the voices are unclear.- Single button problems for Android users. The issues you will encounter depends on the droid OS version. I'm using Jelly Bean, and so the OS reserves certain functions (like click and hold) which cannot be remapped without root access. If you have rooted your droid, go nuts, you can map anything.- I have the blue ones, and I like the colour :)In sum, I got what I paid for; reasonably well performing $9 earbuds that may or may not stand the test of time.

title: Decent for the price
body: Decent earbuds that I've used for about a year but, after a recent upgrade, I rarely use them anymore. The stock sound (out of the box) isn't great but can be greatly improved by some equing. Just for reference I use a rockboxed Clip+ as my source.Accessories: As expected with a budget IEM, there's not much here. 3 sets of silicon tips and the buds with wire.Comfort: Overall, they're pretty comfortable and provide decent isolation with the right tip. I upgraded to the Sony Hybrids and got a slightly better fit which translated into better sound and isolation.Sound: The low end or bass tends to be boomy and bleeds over into the mids making these buds sound muddled. I've seen other reviews complaining about the lack of bass and this really surprised me as these are definitely not bass light. A quick look at the frequency response graphs at Innerfidelity or Goldenears backs this up. Mids are recessed and vocals are difficult to make out especially when mixed in with complex music. The highs are decent but can be a little shrill with some recordings. After equing (dropping the bass, increasing the mids and dealing with hump in the highs) the sound improved significantly. The bass was tighter and more complex, vocals stood out more and the sometimes shrill highs disappeared. The overall clarity of the sound improved. After equing, did they match my more expensive IEMS (GR07 BE)? NO (not even close) but they did sound a lot better.Overall: Definitely worth $5-6 and even more so when equed.

title: I like a snug fit for my earphones.
body: First of all, beware, even though this item shows up under a search for "in-ear headphones with microphone", it does not have a microphone, it is earphones only!Secondly, I personally don't like the way these fit into the ear. I admit I'm very picky about this, and I spend most weeks using in-ear headphones for an average of 6 hours/day, so it's one of the most important pieces of technology in my gadget purse.I've been on a mission to find just the right fit, and I've gone through several (really, over 10) pairs over the last 5 years or so.The best in-ear headphones I had was Beats, both in terms of sound quality and the earbuds fit (it came with about 8 choices and I particularly liked the medium-sized ones). However, I spent $100 on them and they absolutely fell apart after about 2 months of use. I find that unnacceptable for a product of that value, and did not buy new ones.Logitech UE 600vi was good sound and fit for me, and seemed like a good buy at $30ish, but the foam buds kept falling off on both pairs I owned.I then bought a Jarv NMotion (bluetooth) which was a terrible fit in my ears (not a snug fit, which is what I like) and has a very, very bright blue light blinking near the mic button the entire time that it's on.So I'm still on the lookout for the perfect in-ear headphones. I just ordered a symphonized, and will update this post once I've worn them for a few weeks. I hope this review was helpful to you!

title: Meh
body: I'm glad I only paid $6 for these. Meh. I'm not an audiophile, but I do want to hear the depth of range as intended by the artists I'm listening to. They are great for running or some other activity, where the true sound is relatively unimportant because the vibration from the foot pounding and other ambient noise outdoors or in the gym. But sitting at my desk for 8 hours in a quiet office, I want to HEAR the music and iconic crunch from a Gibson Les Paul or the twang of a Fender Telecaster, which I cannot discern from these headphones, they have good volume and sound with some music, but overall they are flat. I wouldn't say tinny or no bass at all, like some reviewers, but they are definitely lacking in depth.I didn't expect much from a $6 pair of headphones, but with the star rating I guess I expected more. I love good heavy guitars, with drop tuning, palm mutes and face-melting solos to a badass bluesy slide on a Gretsch hollow body, and even find myself mixing it up with bass-heavy rap tunes, these are an epic fail. These were not designed for that at all, these are for the masses listening pop auto-tune stuff and for working out a few hours a day for distraction. I would definitely recommend them as 5 star for that use... I want to LISTEN to all the nuances of my music and the fact that I can't hear what I know is there drives me crazy. I'll save them for the gym and running outside, but the search continues for a new pair of reasonably-priced headphones for the office.

title: Can't beat the price
body: For five bucks these are great. My favorite part about these earbuds is the design. They fit in your ear like ear plugs so they won't fall out. These are perfect for working out because even while running and sweating these will not fall out.The sound quality is decent. There is enough bass, and an all around good sound. Also, due to the fact that they fit like earplugs, they could almost be considered noise cancelling as well. However, and I'm not sure if this is due to how they fit in the ear, the sound is a bit muffled. Not only does it sound like there is a barrier between the speakers and my ear, but I've noticed that I have to turn my ipod up louder than with my other phones. In the end, these phones leave something to be desired as far as sound quality. But, as mentioned, for the price they're amazing!If you are looking for some phones that sound decent and won't fall out, these are perfect. They come with 3 different size speaker covers. I use the smallest without discomfort. The medium size fit well but would leave my ears sore after about 10 mins, and the large size would not fit at all. If you're picky on sound and want the best, these aren't it.One more thing to mention, all vibrations are amplified in your ear due to how they fit. So while running if the cord is rubbing on your shirt, you'll hear it. You'll also hear your breathing quite loudly. The same affect if you had ear plugs in. Just thought I'd mention it.

title: Not bad, in the right environment
body: These were bought because of the high ratings here, but I'm not as impressed as some people were. The sound is reasonable, not the best I've heard from cheap earbuds, but I'm most unhappy with them insofar as the amount of external sound they let in; perhaps they just don't fit my ear well, but--well, I guess they don't. I've bought cheap JVC earbuds that I liked a lot more than these.I won't buy these again when the die or get lost.UPDATE Mar 2, 2014: I've had more time to use these earbuds and have modified my opinion of them. The sound from them seems, to me, to be essentially flat, with no real emphasis on any part of the audio spectrum, which is desirable (for quality sound), or perhaps with a slight boost at the extremes, the bass and treble (perhaps a de-emphasis of the mid range). The bass is not muddy, the mid's not overpowering (as mid's tend to be in cheap, crummy, earbuds), and the highs are also clean and detailed (keep in mind this is compared to other earbuds I've used, *not* high-end speaker systems and headphones!) But these fine tonal qualities only present themselves in a quite place.As such, my original complaint stands, insofar as too much outside sound allowed in. Someone had mentioned that this is due to poor-fitting, but, sorry, that's not the case. These are great earbuds in a quite environ, but not nearly as good, or useful, in a normal-noise or loud area, i.e., an airplane or some workplaces, like mine.

title: I think the reviews are too generous due to the low price
body: I had the SkullCandy Ink'd S2INBT-FZ for over a year and one side recently stopped working. So I decided to try these due to the good reviews, but I think the reviews were too generous.There's nothing better than these at this $6 price. So if price is your concern, you should buy these. But if you're looking for a great quality headphone for under $20, I would skip these and go with the SkullCandy Ink'd.The bass on the RPHJE120K is a little weak and the treble is a bit low also. I listen to Reggae, Hip-Hop, Alternative Rock, and sometimes Hawaiian folk music. So for me, the best sound is clear voices with a slightly deep bass that doesn't overpower the music or singing. The RPHJE120K headphones couldn't quite balance this.I would have given the RPHJE120K 4 stars, but another thing that annoys me is the elbow-jack (the "L" shaped connector). I am active in the gym, bodybuilding with heavy weights and I also do a lot of jump-roping. So my headphones take a lot of abuse. The Skull Candy INK'd was a straight jack, so if the wires got yanked, it would pop the jack straight out of my phone leaving the phone intact.In my experience the elbow jack headphones are dangerous. If they get yanked, it takes the whole device with it. I would rather spend $20 on new headphones than $200+ on a new phone or music device.Also, I use headphones primarily on a Samsung Galaxy S2 with a cover and the elbow jack doesn't fit too well with it.

title: You get what you pay for
body: I bought these for $5. It's no surprise that they sound exactly the way that you would expect -- cheap.I've used $10-20 Skullcandy, JVC Gumy, Sony, and now, these earphones. Sony had the best audio quality, JVC had decent audio quality, and Skullcandy had the poorest audio quality. I got these, because I needed a new pair of earphones and the overwhelmingly positive reviews helped influence my decision.Sadly, these Panasonic earphones have audio quality that's on par with Skullcandy. It's like listening to music from a tin can and like listening to mp3s with a bitrate below 128kbps. But again, you get what you pay for. Plus, chances are, if you care a lot about audio quality, you wouldn't be getting these in the first place. I was really hoping for Sony quality, so that was a little disappointing. JVC Gumy earphones would be a good choice if it weren't for its faulty design -- allowing ear gunk to collect inside of the earphones, which was not only gross but would eventually ruin the sound quality; the lifespan of the earphones would be around 3-6 months depending on how often you used them. (I've owned 2 or 3 pairs of them, since they were so comfortable.) They have Gumy Plus now, though, which I have yet to try.I also own Panasonic monitor headphones (RP-HTX7-G1), which I highly recommend for anyone who's looking for headphones around $30.At least, these earphones are comfortable to wear.

title: Love these earbuds, but my newest pair is defective.
body: These are the best earbuds I've ever used. Affordable, long-lasting, and sound great. As you can see from my reviews, I love these and I've bought many pairs over the past 6-7 years. I just had to replace mine because the right earbud was going out a little bit, so I purchased the Metallic Red pair. I've had them for a few days and I did not notice until today that these are defective and play audio in the wrong ears. For example, the right earbud will play the left channel, the left earbud will play the right channel. I will be returning these and replacing them. What used to be 5 stars is now dropped to 3 stars.My first review: These have to be my favorite earbuds I’ve ever used. Been using them for a couple years and all of the pairs I had lasted a super long time. My first pair lasted 9 months but I lost them and had to buy a new pair, and those lasted from December 2017 all the way to the end of this past September. Just got my new pair last week and love them. These earbuds last a very long and the quality is great. They fit well in my ears and they are affordable.Another review from 2020: These are the best earbuds I've ever used. Affordable, long-lasting, and sound great. This is the fourth pair I've had. I got my last pair in October 2018 and didn't need to replace them until now (February 2020!!) when they finally started to break. That's over a year! I've never had earbuds last this long!

title: Great sound and feel, as expected build quality is a little lacking.
body: I really like the way these feel, and the sound is actually up on par with ear buds in the $20- $30 range so they are a great bang for your buck, but there are some things they could do better. I wish they had inline controls and didn't have an L shaped connector, but I knew these things before buying them, so I can't knock them for that.The cord is a little shorter than I would like, as I tug on my phone when it's in my pocket and I move my head while walking around (I'm 5' 7" so I'm not even that tall.I really like the way these fell in my ears, they always feel like they are firmly in my ears and never going to fall out, even when they get tugged on a little as I mentioned before.The build quality is my only real complaint, after using these for a few months the sound started to go out. For a week or so I could mess with the cable where it goes into the L-Shaped connector and if I got it just right everything sounded fine again, however now they just don't work. No matter how I mess with the cable the sound in on ear bud won't come through, or won't stay when I let go of the L-Shaped connector. Oh well, you get what you pay for, can't say they weren't cheap.Overall if they lasted about a year I would just buy a new set every year, but I don't want to be buying a new set every 3 - 4 months, so I will pass on these in the future.

title: Cheap, comfy and okay sound quality...
body: I am NOT an audiophile and don't have a very developed ear to discern quality headphones v. poor quality headphones. I mainly bought these babies because 1) I am cheap, 2) I will probably loose them/destroy them by not caring for them properly, 3) Comfort. After some time spent looking, I chose these based on the amount of reviews/price.I'm using them as I write this review, making this the second or third time I've used them since my purchase so they're still pretty new. As I listen now, I detect a faint crackling on the right side that is breaking the concentration I am trying to cultivate. Because these headphones have been at the bottom of my purse for the last day or so, the fact that one side is crackling may totally be my fault since obviously I haven't been careful in my treatment of them. To be fair, I'm rating them 3 stars because they are comfortable and I don't feel that pressure in my ear canal that I typically do when wearing standard ear buds for an extended period of time (like an hour). I can live with the crackling for awhile but will probably end up throwing them out in a month after I get my money’s worth. I would recommend them to someone like me who is apathetic about headphones and won't be terribly crushed if they get lost. If you are big on sound quality and all that, you may want to bypass these guys and keep on searching.

title: Ya Man.
body: I bought these to replace some House of Marley Midnight Ravers ear buds. I also bought something cheaper because the Universe doesn't seem to want me to have the Marleys. I've had four pair of the Marleys: 1 stolen, 1 to replace the stolen ones, 1 warranty replacement for the ones that replaced the stolen ones (a wire problem), 1 to replace the warrantied ones because I backed up over them with my truck, and 1 to replace the flattened ones, which were lost the day I drove off into the sunset with them sitting out of sight on the hood of said truck. They were lost along with my latest Sansa Clip+ purchase (of which I've had at least six - same Universe). Some jogger got lucky. Anyway, I bought these because they have such good reviews and are less than half the cost of the Ravers. The Panasonic's are OK; slightly better than the ones Sansa packs with their Clip+ players, but not as good as the Marleys. The bass in the Marleys is better and they fit my ear better, plus they look cooler - style is everything. I've not tried Skull Candies. They've been tempting because of the reviews, but I tend to stick to things I know and like - e.g., amaibi and not sea urchin. Buy them again? Probably not; my next pair will likely be another set of Marleys, and hopefully Stephen Hawking will do something about my Universe. Three stars, no bullet.

title: Kind Of Disappointed I Brought This Headset
body: I've read nothing but glowing reviews regarding this Sony headset at Amazon. So I've decided to purchase it because I need a replacement when my Panasonic headphone makes its demise after a long year's use. Big time mistake of me to order this item. The stereo sound is heavenly and powerful but unfortunately not a sliver or hint of any bass reception can be heard while I'm listening it on my Apple Classic Ipod. It has a "tinny" effect thoughout and it's very disappointing not to hear Charlie Watt's incredible drumming talents from the song ''Monkey Man" on the Rolling Stone's album Let It Bleed. I believe this headset is not recommended exclusively for Apple Ipods. Maybe it's best suited for more smaller ipods. Despite this setback, it's not such a bad headset. I really like the plugs because as small as they appear, they stay intact almost like an airtight seal inside your ears without the worries of sudden popouts from jogging walking and sweating. The extended cord is generous in length and again the stereo sounds are trully outstanding. I'd like to give this headset at least a four stars rating if it weren't for the lack of bass frequencies. But I've decided to keep it for my private audios on my computer. I'd recommend this listening device to our Amazon buyers but be warned : bass sounds are altogether excluded.

title: Can't return these. They do not easily fit iPhone 6 with case. Muddled frequencies
body: Pro's:* Noise cancellation works very well* InexpensiveCons:* Much too heavily emphasized bass, distracting and gives me a headache* Mids/Hi's very "busy", lots of added noise, frequencies muddled together* Can't turn the music up, too much extra noise, not enough music* Have to wear them deep in your ears, is painful for me after a whileNotes:FYI, for some reason Amazon will not let me return these. So be sure you're buying exactly what you want.By default, these earphones do not fit my iPhone 6s when it has its Caseology case on. The plug of these earphones is huge. I ended up purchasing a 3.5mm male to female slim extension cable for $6 to be able to use these earphones.The cables on these earphones tangle very easily. They do not come with a carrying case so is hard to pack them away in my laptop bag. Have never had a set of earphones tangle so much.The sound on these is okay, not epic like some others have stated, I'd say the sound average, nothing spectacular. In fact these give me a headache after prolonged use.If the above qualms are not things that would bother you, these earphones are a decent value. For me personally, I think I'll be replacing them with a higher quality pair soon.

title: Buzzing is distracting, color is really nice
body: I have a pair of Bluetooth earbuds for my phone, but when watching things on my computer, I still use the old school plug-in headphones. I’ve been using this pair of headphones for months, and since I usually use them at night when the house is quiet, I set my volume at less than 1/4 of the volume level bar. Yesterday, the kids were making some noise around me when I was trying to study, so I upped the volume to 1/2 of the volume level bar. I am no audiophile by any means, but the buzzing from the bass was so strong that it was uncomfortable to listen to. The volume wasn’t even loud at all, and I tried taking the earphones out slightly so they wouldn’t be so close to my ear, but I still heard the buzzing. It came from both the left and the right sides, and was so distracting that I couldn’t focus on my work. I understand that this isn’t an expensive product, but it is still from a reputable brand, and as such, I would expect better quality. What I received also seems like a legitimate Panasonic product, as it came in the hard plastic and not a cloth pouch like some others received. My pros to this product are the very affordable price and the beautiful color (the metallic violet is gorgeous). If you always have your volume on low, then these may work for you, but otherwise I wouldn’t recommend them.

title: Why so many five-star reviews? Four, maybe, but five?
body: I'm not obsessive about good headphone sound, and I expect far less of earbuds, and even less of cheap earbuds. But, I fell for all the praise that appears in these reviews, and -- alas -- these $6 earbuds perform fairly well for the money, but do not represent an extraordinary deal. If the user chooses the tightest fitting silicone bud caps that ship with the buds, you will hear fairly balanced output with acceptable bass and treble -- but only if you shove 'em deeply into the ear. That's a bit uncomfortable. Customers who dislike that feeling will wear them more loosely, and will hear midrange output only, shrill highs and barely any bass.They would e fine for audiobook listening, in any case.As with all things -- and except for the sporadic deals that appear on amazon that are truly worthwhile -- you get what you pay for. And these little buds -- well -- work only when they seal the ear canal in a pretty unhealthy way.. They are neither physically tough nor sonically outstanding, but for the money, they're more than adequate. I award three stars because they are extremely fragile and will probably not stand up to prolonged use, and because there are slightly more expensive buds that don't require deep ear placement to achieve a similar frequency range.

title: Worth Upgrading for the Extra Money
body: For the price, these headphones will get the job done. I bought them because they were cheap and had good reviews. Looking back, I don't know that I agree with al of the reviews on here and on CNET. I find that the sound quality is fine. I'm not really a sound snob so it would be very hard to impress or disappoint me there. I dislike the headphones for their functionality.The cord is super thin. It is constantly being tangled and it doesn't have any weight behind it. The weight isn't enough to hold the cord down against any curvatures or twists and it can get annoying when you end up with cord in your face. I much prefer my iFrogz headphones.Another disappointment is the length of the cord. It isn't really something I looked into. However, I have trouble threading the cord under my jacket and up into my pocket.The headphones do have the microphone, which is a great bonus at this price level. The feature works well enough. My opinion on these headphones is if you want a cheap pair to not worry about, then these are probably your best bet. If you want a decent pair without spending too much money, then money up the extra five dollars and buy a nicer pair. I think the CNET review on these is fairly fluffed and they are overrated.

title: Photo not accurate for shape -- sound quality and fit still good for price
body: These are different than I expected, looking at the photos and description. The angle of the photo makes it seem like they are the kind where the rubber part goes over the entire speaker/bud but it doesn't, there's like a little nib that pops out and that's where the rubber part is, and the bud is separate.This is what they actually look like: [...]Not what I was looking for, but they seem to work alright so far, though it's only been an hour or so.I did notice a weird clicking sound every so often in the left ear, but I think it has to do with the fact that I can hear my own movements and breathing. O_O My old ones didn't do that, but these also seem to cancel out a bit more sound then they did.My old ones came with my 3DS pack, they didn't have much bass but they also didn't make me hear weird noises when I move around or any of the other issues. I'd still be using them if they were chewed off and taken by mice.All in all: They are decent quality, especially for the price I'm sure. Has some weird little quirks I'm not used to but they fit well and come with other sizes, sound quality is also pretty good. Just be sure they are the shape you want first before ordering. (view the photo I linked instead of the deceiving one they posted)

title: Ok quality for $8-9, worse build than 2017 version
body: I bought a pair of these headphones (black, no mic) in early 2017 and loved them--great value for $10 and quite sturdy. Went out in the rain with them, dropped them, sat on them, etc, no problems. Sadly I lost my pair after about 2 1/2 years, so I bought a replacement pair in August 2019.Unfortunately, the build quality on these headphones has gone significantly downhill. The fit is still good, but the materials and build are noticeably worse. The noise canceling is average; if I'm in a subway station I have to turn my music up past halfway to block out external sound (before I only had to turn it up a quarter of the way), which isn't good for your hearing long term. The soft black plastic cover of the earbud itself is also a little tougher. The wires seem a bit flimsier and less sturdy, and are not strongly attached to the earbud (i.e., dust and grime can get in the little clear bit where the wire leads into the earbud). The good thing is that they do still fit well and don't fall out easily-- I have smaller ears. I'm not sure if I just got a bad pair or something, but this is the pair I got, so.I wouldn't pay more than about $8-9 for the 2019 version of these headphones, but if you need a cheap average pair, you can do worse than these.

title: Decent headphones for workouts
body: This is my second pair of these headphones, and the first lasted a couple of years. They're sturdy, lightweight, and the sound quality is great. I have trouble with a lot of earbuds because I use them when jogging and most tend to constantly pop out of my ears while jogging. These ones stay snugly in place and don't slip out even when I'm sweating or it's raining.The only con is that the connection isn't great. If I accidentally touch the input jack the wrong way, the sound gets static-y. This happened with my last pair too, but it wasn't a lasting thing or a big deal. Also, the left earbud stopped working well after a while with the last pair. The sound still came through it, it was just way quieter in the left than the right.Edit: I've had these about 5 months now and this pair got the same problem as the last pair, where the left side stopped working well. Sound is still coming through it, it's just really quiet compared to the right side. I would give 3.5 stars if possible instead of 4 because they don't last, and the same problem happened with both pairs. When they're working, they're great, but they're cheap and don't last long. I got another brand to try out and they seem better so far.

title: Just OK - Scratchy and Oblong earpiece
body: These are just ok earbuds. I needed an inexpensive pair for work in case they got stolen from my workstation. They have good sound and the sound doesn't travel so that others nearby can hear, but I can hear scratching when I move about the cord rubs against something.One thing you cannot tell from the picture is that the piece that the ear pad goes over and goes into the ear is oblong and not round. I already have a hard time finding small enough ear buds so these definitely do not work. I put the smallest ear pad on (which is a tad difficult because the pads are round and the plastic ear bud is oblong) and it hasn't been a half hour and my ear already hurts. Given that the pad is round and plastic is oblong, the pad fits snugly and is not likely to come off (an issue I've had with other ear buds).I do like that the piece that fits inside the ear is moved forward on the part that goes into the ear. This shows in the picture provided.Only three stars because I've purchased other inexpensive ear buds that don't have the scratching when the cords rubs against things. Also because of the oblong shape. Maybe if I had researched more, I would have known this prior to ordering.

title: Sound quality is fine, less than optimal comfort for my small ears
body: The sound quality is fine although admittedly I am not an audiophile. But both podcast/voice only and music sound good through these headphones. I like the orange color (I got it to go with my Fire cover which is also orange, and now I am quite coordinated!). My only issue is the comfort. These do come with the three sizes of soft rubber for the ear bud part that goes in your ear, but even on the smallest one they hurt my ears a bit, which I have not had a problem with from similar headphones - I am a small person, 5' tall adult female, but normally the medium or small parts work for me with other headphones. These particularly hurt when wearing them while lying down, which I think is due to the posts having the unusual offset design - it is hard to find a comfortable position for these if I have my head on a pillow that puts any pressure against my ear. Also, the plastic posts that the soft rubber fits onto are oblong rather than round, so it was somewhat difficult to swap out the default medium size for the smaller size. Not impossible, just more difficult and time consuming than I am used to. So overall these work fine, but due to the comfort issues I will get a different kind next time.

title: Use as backup
body: Positives - Only true positive is that these are very comfortable. Clarity is good, no muffling of voices, but.....Negative: The volume levels too low for my taste as compared to other buds. Minus a star. My other older earphones sound better than these and they don't even go all the way in my ear. I know these things are cheap, but I thought the other reviews were just, seems like false reviews. I got one thing to ask, "WHERE'S THE BASS?" Minus 2 stars off the rip. I never have my player turned up and I like clarity driven bass and I don't like the fact that I have to crank my player high to get to the levels of pleasure with NO BASS!Their must be several reviewers on here that were either paid or have not listened to a pair of high quality earphones before, because if anyone actually appreciates music then these things SU..... To be polite, these things actually are not good to listen to anything due to the low volume levels, I won't even mention the lack of Bass again.I purchased two highly rated ear buds, the cheap iLuv iEP322BLK City Lights are way better, PERIOD!!I will use these buds as a temporary backup if the Luvs get damaged

title: Meeeh ... Hips don't lie
body: I tried these earbuds with the largest silicon ear pieces and they still did not make a good fit. The sound is a bit better than other $7 earbuds, but they still make music listening a chore due to their clack of clarity and sharpness. Like Shakira said, "Hips don't lie." When I'm wearing my good earbuds, my feet start to tap, my hips want to shake, and I am standing close to the musicians. When I put on these earbuds, the music is still there, but has lost it's passion and my feet don't feel like moving This is why I gave it only 3 stars. This could be the best $7 earbud available, but it takes the joy out of good music.. You can invest in a decent set of earbuds for under $40 such as the Logitech Ultimate 500. If you have a few more dollars, consider the Klipsich S4 for $59, but you will have to hurry. They have been discontinued. A slight notch up from the Klipsich, you may want to consider the Meelectronics A151, but be forewarned, this last one had the shortest lifespan of the three. Also keep in mind that all earbuds rely on thin wires and the expensive earbuds can die just as fast as the cheap ones. Consider yourself lucky if one of them lasts even a year.

title: Sound quality when not running is very good with strong base
body: I bought these with the main purpose to use for running. Sound quality when not running is very good with strong base. While running however I have 2 issues. Even with trying all 3 ear plug sizes provided, I find the ear buds to be less then secure. I find that with the motion associated with running, the buds work themselves out. I've worked in industry requiring hearing protection use for years so know how to insert ear plugs properly but even following that procedure, only sure way to keep these in is to cover with a head band after getting these inserted. The head band serves to isolate the wires attached to the buds from jarring and therefore the buds stay secure.Issue 2 is that as I run and the wire below where the wire splits for each ear piece bounces against my body, a sound seems to to transfer into the buds. Sounds similar to if you tapped your finger on the back of the bud as it was in your ear. This is not a deal breaker for me but might be for others. I'm pretty tolerant of the distortion because I'm only using music as a bit of distraction as I run. If I was really interested in listening however, I might not use these.For the extremely low cost of these, they'll do

title: Almost great but too harsh sounding
body: Not quite the wonder earbuds that other reviewers think.I bought these as a replacement for the stock earbuds that came with my iPhone 6s. I ran pink noise through them for about 10 hours to break them in before using. I'm not sure if cheap earbuds need breaking in but did so all the same.Sound quality: Reasonable. Bass is there but not overpowering, exactly as it should be. Mid range seems to be lacking a little. High end seems to be a little overpowering. It results in an overly harsh sound that gives me a headache if I listen to music for more than 20 minutes. The stock iPhone earbuds have a more balanced and listenable sound.For phone calls the voice sound quality is good. The microphone is better than the stock earbuds. These earbuds (are supposed to) sit very snugly in your ears which has a sound isolation effect. This can be a bit weird when talking on the phone. With stock iPhone earbuds I can hear myself clearly. With these it is like talking with my fingers in my ears. I end up talking a bit louder because I cannot hear myself. It takes a bit of getting used to.Overall, they are ok for $15 but not quite as good as other reviews would suggest.

title: Fits great, but sound isn't the best
body: Since this product prides itself on its actual fit, I have to say Panasonic got that part right. I've always had difficulty finding earphones that fit me and don't fall out (I'm looking at you, Apple's one-size-fits-all *NOT* earphones). Right after I took these out of the package and tried them on, I knew I had found a pair that wouldn't fall out even at some rigorous movement. And yes, I jumped around and stuff to test that out.However, where these aren't as great is sound quality. The actual speakers are very small, I don't know if that contributes to the rather flimsy sound. These are the kind of earphones where, at a decent volume and with both buds in your ears, you'd still be able to make conversation. Compare that with the Gumy set I got at the same time for about the same price -- while those did not fit as well, they were noise canceling and surround sound. The music listening experience was totally better.I've decided to make these my work earphones, since I can tune some sound out but still hear my coworkers if we need to talk. I would've wished for better sound quality so I can use them for working out, but these will do for a different purpose.

title: Fantastic Value, But Cable Discrepancies
body: I have purchased four sets of these earbuds now. Three of those are the standard type, the fourth has the button and microphone to use with a phone. The quality is good, they're rugged, and the sound is really good. While the fit is good, they do protrude a little from my ears, making them not as comfortable to use when laying down on one side.The first two standard sets I received had a nice tangle-free cable. Then I ordered and received the phone-style set, and its cable was not as flexible and was much more prone to tangling. At first I attributed this to a difference between the phone-style and the non-phone-style, but then I ordered a third non-phone-style, and it showed up with the tangly cord. The attached photo shows the difference.This may seem like a minor problem, but in reality it is kind of a pain. It is harder to wrap up the earbuds and put them away without them becoming all tangled. Also, if you use this with an FM Walkman style radio like I do, the radio uses the headphones for an antenna. If the cord is twisted and tangled rather than hanging free, reception definitely suffers. I am deducting a couple stars for this discrepancy.

title: Just okay overall, good for the price they are
body: I've been using the earbuds that came with my Samsung s4 for a long time now, and recently those have been malfunctioning on me. So after reading the reviews for these headphones I bought them because they were well priced and seemed to be a good deal. When I tested them out they initially sounded odd, not very high quality. I knew from the reviews that you have to sort of adjust them, you can't just put them in and expect them to work for you. I tried all the sizes of the earpieces (small medium large) and the medium worked best for me. I had to mess around a lot with the positioning in my ear to get a better sound, and even then it wasn't amazing. This is probably because I'm used to the very good sound of my Samsung headphones, and I would recommend these for a ''cheap, but good for it's price'' backup pair if your main pair breaks. The sound is not exactly tinny which is good but they don't sound as I'd hoped. If you were in my position and liked your phone's earbuds, I would suggest seeing if you can get a replacement pair. I will keep these decent headphones for backup, but in the meantime I will probably be looking for Samsung earbud replacements in stores.

title: I am an audiophile.
body: Contrary to a lot of the reviews, I actually found these buds to have some decent bass reproduction for their product class. Comfort-wise, they are certainly a bit different due to their ergonomic shape, and while it wasn't necessarily my favorite shape or feel, I was able to wear them without being bothered too much by it. But here are the 2 deal breakers for me:(1) The treble causes figurative ear bleeding. And if I were to use them regularly, I suspect they might cause literal ear bleeding. There was something about the treble that just hurts my ears like crazy. It was extremely raw.(2) At the same time, everything sounds distant/tin canny. I felt like I was listening to the headphones of the person next to me. I mean, you just don't feel like you're within or surrounded by the music.Obviously you're not going to get quality sound from something in this price bracket, let alone this style of device. But I WAS hoping for something decent for portable purposes. I'm throwing these out in favor of apple earbuds for the time being. And for my real listening I will continue using my Audio Technica m50 set, which I highly recommend.

title: Good sound, without balance or durability
body: I bought these after reading the countless positive reviews on the sound and deciding they were worth buying for the price.I opened them, and the packaging was expected. Nothing special. But that's what you get when you buy ~$10 earbuds.Now on to the headphones themselves.When I tried them out for the first time, I wasn't expecting much. Let me tell you, I was pleasantly surprised. These sounded absolutely incredible for the price. I would honestly compare the sound to that of a $50+ pair of Sennheisers.The bass is excellent, but very dependent on the left bud. The right bud sounded very lackluster, but the left was great. This seemed very odd to me, and after a while it became very obvious and quite annoying.Now onto the comfort.I'll keep this portion short. They are very comfortable and I didn't have any problems with them after 3 straight hours of listening.Durability: This is the worst quality of the buds. They lasted me about 3 weeks until one of the buds split in half and we're nearly unusable.So overall, these have excellent sound for the price, but lack balance and durability.

title: Janitor stole my buds.  Now I'm looking for a good replacement.
body: The janitor recently stole the pair of Skullcandies I usually left at work. I wanted a good music listening pair to replace them. I recently bought several "cheapish" ear buds, one 20 dollar set, and a set of JVC flat headphones recently. I couldn't decide based on reviews alone, so tried several different options. I know it was silly. I should have just spent it all on one "nice" pair, but I thought it would be fun, and it actually was. Besides, why give the Janitor such a big target. ;p I also discovered different roles for the buds I bought. They nearly all have a place in my collection. :] Win win.I'm no audiophile, or sound professional. Evenso, I bet I'm like a lot of you. I want nice sounding music to accompany my day while quieting the environment. I hope these comparisons will be helpful so you don't have to buy a bundle of buds, and can maybe zero in on a set or two that will serve your tastes best. I enjoyed comparing and contrasting, and actually noting quit a bit of difference.The buds/phones I tried areiLuv iEP322ORG [orange] in-ear Ultra Bass ~6 dollarsPanasonic RPHJE120K [black] in-ear ~6 dollarsYamaha EPH-20Bu [supposedly blue but actually purple] in- ear ~20 dollarsJVC 10HAFC earphones ~3 dollarsJVC "Flats" on ear headphones black ~12Skullycandy "Ink'd" in ear buds ~15 [already owned, but in for comparison]Sentry Wind-Ups in-ear buds ~5 dollarsiLuv:I chose the orange iLuv's because it was the cheapest color on Amazon. I enjoyed their sound overall. They are the most bass heavy of the group. However, they leave the highs and mids a little neglected. They also reduce ambient sound decently, so you can focus on what you are listening to. So while these are not the most balanced, your teen will probably love the sound. They are also on the "loud" side. Even at minimum device volume these came in the loudest. As such, I will probably put these in with one of my DS systems' carrying cases. It will be great for those, and be on hand so I won't have to scrounge around for a pair, when I want to hear the game and not my wife's TV programs. These also won't be my work or walking pair, because they don't look very adult [again, your teen will probably love them], and they produce the second most "cord noise" in the group from the cords rubbing on your shoulders or clothes while you are walking or exercising. If I had nothing else to compare to, I think I'd have been very happy with this set.Panasonic:The Panasonics were probably the quietest of the group. Sound is relatively balanced, with no real extremes. Bass seems light for an in ear bud. They are now my go to bed buds. I can put my device on minimum volume and easily go to sleep. The iLuvs and Skullcandy were too loud to be comfortable on both my phone media player and iPad mini's lowest setting for sleeping. The Panasonics are also small enough, that they weren't uncomfortable for side sleeping. These also produced the least cord noise from movement, so should be good to walk, exercise in. Noise reduction was decent and reduced my wife's snoring several decibels. This might not be so great for outdoor exercise, where you may want to hear your environment for safety. I may even get a second set or carry mine back and forth so I can walk at lunch break in these, since I have a protected area to walk [no cars, etc.]. The lack of cord noise really makes the difference here.Yamaha:I was curious about what I would get by going a little upmarket. Granted it's not much of a jump at "only" 20 dollars. The Yamahas were probably most similar in sound to the Panasonics or JVC flats. They had a good all around balance and were more full than the Panasonics. Bass is not prominent, nor is it missing. They sound best with Classical music in my experience. On the down side, they were advertised as "blue", but Yamaha's idea of blue is very clearly Purple. It's not a major issue, but be aware, if you choose this color. Cord noise seemed on the loud side, compared to the JVC buds and Panasonics. I probably would not want to do anything active in them. However, if I could "only" have one bud, this might be it.JVC HAF10C earbuds:For only around 3 dollars you shouldn't normally expect too much. Agree? Well, I already had a blue pair [ 4 dollars at retail] so knew what to expect. It's a testament I bought another. This pair was black, and I far prefer the gorgeous blue plastic set, but would not pay a couple extra dollars on Amazon for that choice. I know it shouldn't matter, but I REALLY like blues.
