Anyhow, the most unique feature of this set is the clamshell case you get. It allows you to wind your cord and protect the buds. I wish higher quality buds would do this. I'd pay a couple extra bucks for it. The case does not work well for most in-ear buds, I would guess. I am able to get the above Yamahas in one case [why I ordered an extra set], but it is not a perfect fit. Larger buds probably won't fit. The buds themsevles have a durable hard plastic casing and should be quite durable. Sound is actually nice, for non-in ear buds. For the price I doubt you'd find better sound. They are also on the quiet side, and not being directly in ear, you could probably give these to your kids without the threat of hearing damage you might get from louder and in ear buds. I use these to walk, talk radio, and some music. Unlike most in-ear, I can wear comfortably all day if I loosely cradle them on ear. While outdoors, they allow you to hear the environment, including wind, which is a downside. Fortunately, they do not generate the cord noise that is such a pet peeve of mine. Great all around value. I don't know that the value can be beat for the quality of plugs, and the very well designed case. Being so cheap, I also don't have to fret too much if the Janitor decides to steal this pair also.JVC "Flats":These are on ear headphones, so probably not fair to compare to ear buds. Still they were part of my purchase, so I am including for contrast. At only 12 dollars, I was very surprised how crisp and clean these sounded. They delivered a balanced spectrum, without sacrificing bass as I'd expected. They do not isolate noise as well as in-ear, but you do get some reduction without having to turn your music WAY up. For straight music listening or watching a video, I might choose these most of the time at home. They do not seem to generate cord noise due to movement, but are bulkier than what I want to walk in. They also fold "flat" making them a nice travel pair for people who don't like in-ear buds. I keep mine in an inexpensive ipad mini neoprene case, for example. Would be easy to take on a plane. I have a big head [really], despite their diminuitive size, they adjusted to fit very comfortably. I can wear for long sessions, without bother. Can't go wrong with these.Skullcandy "Ink'd":This may not be a fair comparison either. I think these had the greatest "retail" price when they were new, but I bought them on sale at Ross stores for about 15 dollars. These are a few years old, so might be an older make than what is available now. However, I am still surprised by how good these sound to me. Of all the ear buds in my collection these are probably my favorite for music. It figures, after buying several, the one I had still sounded best to me. It has plenty of bass, without sacrificing the overall quality like the iLuvs seem to. However, it is still one of the loudest of my collection, and is uncomfortable to sleep with on my devices. My radio is more sensitive and can tune more quietly, but I rarely use that to sleep to. This set also probably produces the most cord noise of any ear buds meaning they won't walk the beat with me. It also features metallic red and white buds, white cord, and the companies trade mark as a red skull. While I think they look "cool" the skull and colors all look a little childish. They are not as garish as the iLuvs, but they are certainly second in my comparison. That said, there are more sedate colorings available, however they all have the contrasting skull logo in some fashion or other. As such these will mostly be used privately.Sentry Wind-ups:These were my least favorite. I was drawn in by the promise of a nifty carry case again. I might have kept just for that, if they'd have worked. However, the cord winder was rather difficult to accuate, at least for my biggish hands. Also my pair of buds may have been defective. The right bud produced only half the volume of the left. I took back to Wal-mart asap. At only 5 dollars, I didn't expect much, but I did expect balance across left and right. I would presume that was a single item issue, but I did not want to get a second pair to find out. Sound was more hollow than most I tried, not even measuring up to the 3 dollar JVC's.There it is. I hope you've these comments helpful in finding your pair.

title: Are you like me and you are on that life-long quest to ...
body: Are you like me and you are on that life-long quest to find earbuds that are cheap but have good quality sound?If you are, then these are a good buy.About me: I'm not an audiophile; but I do want quality sound when it comes to earbuds. I also don't want to break the bank either. That being said, I have never listened to a pair of earbuds that were over $30 (I'd say the most expensive ones I've listened to were a pair of skullcandy's). So forgive me if my review is a little bit subjective.I go through earbuds a lot (some have cracked, gotten lost, or sound just stopped coming from one side); So i'm always on the search for earbuds that are cheap but also have good sound quality.--->Pros:--They have pretty good sound quality.My current pair of earbuds right now are the "Brooklyn Headphone Company High Performance In-Ear Headphones with Built-In Microphone - Retail Packaging - Black." I compared these panasonic earbuds with my brooklyn in-ear headphones (when i say compare i mean i listened to a song, then interchanged between the two headphones); i noticed that the brooklyn earbuds sound a bit louder then the panasonic earbuds. The brooklyn earbuds also have a lot more bass. These panasonic ones have good sound quality; the treble is great, but there are earbuds out there that sound bette than these. Of course, these panasonic earbuds were better than my apple earbuds. The panasonic earbuds were a little bit louder, and the bass is a little better if not the same.--The noise cancelation is pretty good (?).I've actually never really tried on an expensive pair of earbuds that have noise-cancellation, so I don't really know what can be considered good/bad/fantastic/crappy noise-cancellation.However, if i were to rate the noise-cancellation on a scale of 1-5 (1 being the worst where you can here everything outside, and 5 being you can NOT hear anything outside), I would give these a 3.7. Why a 3.7? Because with absolutely no music/volume, you can hear outside noises somewhat well. With music about halfway, you cannot hear much in a moderately loud setting. With volume at 75% or louder, you can't hear ANYTHING outside of your earbuds.--Noise Isolation is good (?).I haven't tested noise isolation much. The only test i've done is cranking the volume to about 70% and pushing my earbuds against a surface (like my legs) so as to sort of reenact having earbuds in my earcanals. After doing this, i noticed that the music does not leak out that much. You can hear it, but its very very low.--Stays in ear well.Ran and walked with them on. They stay on well. No complaints.--Durability of Earbuds (not cable) is great.I've stepped on these multiple times, and these have still managed to not crack. The plastic on the earbud is pretty hard.--->Cons:--They hurt my ears for the first couple weeks.It took some getting used to, but my first couple of listening sessions with these on made my ears feel sore. But i guess my ears got used to them, cause now I listen to them without pain.--The cord/cable feels very thin/flimsy.Now these are still working, and I've had them for about 9 months now. I'm actually very surprised that no wires in the cable have ripped/teared causing sound to stop coming to one side. But still, i feel like its only a matter of time that sound stops coming out of one ear due to a wire being torn inside the flimsy cable.-->Tip for Durability:Now I may be wrong on this, but I believe that the lifespan of earbuds shorten when you pull a lot on the cable; it causes the 2 thin wires inside of the cable to get disconnected or torn. So when you have earbuds that have a very thin or weak cable, your earbuds might not last long if you tug or pull lot on the cable of your earbudsGeneral tip for all earbuds: When you wrap the earbuds around your ipod/mp3, provide some slack. What I mean by that is just don't pull on the cable while wrapping it around your ipod/mp3 and have the cable tighly wrapped. This will obviously cause the inner wires to get torn or stressed. Instead, wrap the cable a gentlier with a loose fit.TL;DRThese are a good pair of earbuds for a good price. If you go through earbuds a lot and you're looking for a good cheap pair, or if you're looking for just an extra pair to have, I recommend these. BUT, (i hate to promote products on another product's review page, but i owe it to my fellow cheapskates out there) if you're looking for a pair of earbuds with better sound quality with a mic for the same price as these... Go ahead and look at the "Brooklyn Headphone Company High Performance In-Ear Headphones with Built-In Microphone - Retail Packaging - Black." CNET even had them in an article for "best earbuds for under $10" or something like that.Regards,-Alex

title: Good for the price
body: I ordered a pair of those because I went looking for a backup pair of earbuds and saw the low price and great reviews. These are awesome for the money.There seems to be a tendency to pile on with "me too" reviews, so I wasn't sure what I'd think of these things - but at the price I thought I couldn't lose. As a frequent traveller I also thought they'd be a good option for air travel where I might forget them on an airplane (where, if I am not using a personal device, doesn't have great audio quality anyway).I compared these to an expensive set of Grado headphones, a mid grade ~$100 pair of Klipsch earbuds, and my desktop monitor system (which is a standalone uDAC2, "Fat Man" integrated amp with tube preamp, and KEF iQ1 speakers). I used the DAC's headphone output for the earbuds, used the Fat Man headphone output for my power hungry Grados, and obviously used the Fat Man speaker level output for my KEF bookshelf speakers.My point isn't to needlessly compare the above items (as I doubt many would cross shop them), but simply to illustrate that I directly compared several sources to form my opinion of the Panasonics.- The Panasonics have "big" bass (at least in my ear canal). I'd like the bass to be toned down slightly, but I'd rather have the current bass level than a shrill tinny product if I were forced to pick extremes. The bass bloom might actually be preferable under some circumstances (loud jet, cutting grass - which I do with ear protection over my earbuds, working out, etc).- The ability of these to passively block ambient noise is average and acceptable.- They have a warmish midtone that is probably pleasing depending on the device these are coupled with.I do wonder if my setup had a bit more power available than these were designed for. Sometimes a good clean audio setup leads one to crank the volume higher than they otherwise would do.I may have just been "cleanly" overloading these drivers (which might not be practical with some portable devices and is never a good idea regardless of what upstream equipment you have). At lower levels (and therefore any level that I'm likely to listen to for more than 2 minutes they sounded very good).- Although I think the warmish midtone is a decent feature of this product in some cases, if you find a track where there's a hint of gritty midrange warmth/distortion you can really hear things lose some definition. I'll give 2 example cases:(1) Taking U2's "The First Time" (a somewhat obscure track I admit), there's an outro sequence where keyboards get progressively louder and overload most driver/amp combos that I've heard the song played on (maybe because the generally low level of the song inclines one to listen to the first 80% of it louder on the dial). It wasn't until I got a fairly high end rig that I noticed the keyboard "clipping" I was used to wasn't part of the recording (or was 3% of the magnitude that I had previously thought).(2) Listen to the last minute of Johnny Cash's "Hurt" cover. It's an amazing song, but the general grittiness of how the vocals are presented during this bit combines with the characteristics of these Panasonics to show how the warm characteristic isn't always a good thing. Other factors (such as the rasp of Cash's voice here) probably come into play, but it's a case where you can appreciate the characteristic of the Panasonics when comparing them to other products.- High frequencies are perfectly pleasant. They may lack a little bit in absolute detail but I'm happy to listen to them and don't have much other criticism to offer. If I can't get a "perfect" (neutral) earbud I'll take a less fatiguing presentation of high frequencies any day.- This is a personal criterion, but these are probably the most comfortable earbuds I've ever worn. I have smaller ears and an average ear canal size, but I couldn't imagine a better fit for my personal needs. I mentioned being an avid traveller, so the comfort of these will probably matter more to me than relatively minor audio distinction between these and $50+ earbuds when I'm on my next 10hr flight.These aren't an end-all to earbud listening (but you'd be foolish for having made that assumption despite the overwhelmingly positive review tally). However, at this price just buy them and try for yourself. If they don't suit your needs give them to someone you know (as they come with extra ear buds you'll be able to find someone to use them).If I were forced (as an audio enthusiast) to spend the rest of my life doing all headphone/earbud listening on only these earbuds I'd probably be "mildly annoyed" (and largely because I know what else is out there). That's not bad praise for something this cheap!

title: Immediately Impressed...THE value pair of buds to buy.
body: I've used a TON of different pairs of earbuds/earphones ranging in price and quality all the way from Samsung and Apple's abysmal pack-ins up to some of my all-time faves, the Etymotic ER-4P and Shure SE530. I've run my phones amplified, un-amplified, through my Dragonfly DAC, straight from the laptop's line out, from multiple phones and media players. Long story short, I do possess the ability to provide an educated opinion on whether a pair of buds should be worth buying and which shouldn't be.And you know what the real shocker is? Hand over fist, I'd recommend these Panasonics over just about any pair of earbuds under 50 bucks. There are a few exceptions in the budget arena, such as the Xiaomi Piston II and Forte Life S which excel in their own rights, but as an all-rounder these really can't be beat. They're ridiculously comfortable, seal very quickly (though they could stand to come with another pair of slightly larger silicone tips for listeners with Viking-sized ears...very few people will need this option), offer a very pleasant sound signature, and (maybe most important) has a price point that makes it a serious contender in the budget arena.As far as the sound signature is concerned, I feel safe in saying they were tailored to the average consumer. First, there's definitely plenty of bass. Much like the Forte Life S, it's hard to look at the size of these things and expect them to dig down towards the sub-bass regions. Granted, it's not going to give you the sensation of strapping a subwoofer to your skull, but they shouldn't leave you lacking, either. Next, there's a healthy amount of midrange and listeners of the majority of rock subgenres should feel very happy. Electric guitars have a definitely advantage here and there's a lot of meat in tracks like Crossfade's "Washing the World Away" or Dark New Day's "Brother." Finally, at the top, you get a smooth yet (in my humble opinion) premature rolloff. Perhaps in an attempt to tame the typical sibilance of most consumer-level headphones, they thought an early rolloff would be an advantage and most listeners certainly won't complain. There are those, however, who will want a pair with more "sparkle," in which case I'd suggest doubling your investment and going with either the Xiaomi or Forte.As far as fit and comfort are concerned, I have zero complaints. If there ever was a straight drop-in solution, Panasonic has things on lock. I don't find myself needing to perform intricate maneuvers like pulling my ear lobes back (a la Etymotic) or twist-locking (Yurbuds is notorious for this). Instead, I get an immediate seal without feeling like I'm putting undue pressure at any point. I can see these providing hours upon hours of comfortable use.Final notes:The remote and built-in mic work as intended, though only has a singular button for accepting/ending calls vice separate volume controls (most likely to assist with cross-compatibility between Apple and Android devices). I can't speak much on durability as these have only been unboxed for a few hours, but will do my best to update after I run them through the gamut that is your typical submarine underway period. Cord length feels more than adequate without feeling like I have far more than I need. For your person of 5'10-6' tall, you'll be fine keeping these plugged in and storing your media device in your pants pocket. The 45-degree termination for the headphone jack should help provide the necessary strain relief and the jack itself is skinny enough that it shouldn't have an issue with phone cases that don't offer a healthy opening for headphones.TL;DR: These Panasonics should be a MUST OWN for anyone concerned with good sound. Are they a replacement for my Shures or Etymotics? In a way, yes. I can pay 20 bucks for these and replace as necessary without worry in a more rugged environment. Still, if pristine sound quality or a truly flat signature is your concern, these probably aren't the way to go and you're probably not looking at the $20 price bracket. I feel comfortable in saying that you'd really have to more than double your budget to attain that goal.

title: They've worked like a charm
body: I purchased these headphones approximately 1 1/2 years ago and they still work. When I originally bought them I needed them as all-purpose headphones which I could bring to school, use in the weight room, and use while doing work around the house. For a large portion of the time I've had them that's exactly what I've used them for, and they've worked like a charm. It is important to note, however, that these headphones are all-plastic, so they will take some abuse but will get damaged if the situation gets too rough. (I'll touch on that a bit more later).For the price, the sound quality of these in-ears is fantastic. When I let a friend use them once, he immediately responded with "whoa." For $10 earbuds the sound is very impressive and probably suitable for most people. These headphones have a balanced sound with enough clarity to deliver the basic framework of whatever music you're listening to, plus a little bit more. If you're mostly a passive listener and want cheap earbuds you won't have to worry about if they get lost or stolen, these are a great buy.When I eventually upgraded to more expensive earbuds, these ones were able still to hold a candle to the competition, although with further comparison the Panasonic's are slightly "blattier" and "fuzzier" than higher-quality, more expensive options. On their own, though, these earbuds sound significantly better than others in their price range, even rivaling some costing around $25.I used these earbuds as my primary listening medium for about nine months and they withstood a lot of abuse. Every day I would take them to school, usually stuffing them in the smaller pocket of my backpack and carrying them wherever I went. For a long time, they stood strong. However, I pulled the buds out of my ears one day and the right side wasn't all together. In fact, the right speaker had split off from the rest of the earbud's housing. However, the wire to the speaker was still intact and after I gently snapped the two pieces back together, both earbuds worked just the same. Unfortunately, I was long passed the ninety (90) day warranty for the headphones and there was nothing I could do to have them fixed. From then on, I have had to be careful when pulling the right bud from my ear, but besides that, nothing else has broken.Now, these earbuds have long been replaced by more expensive ones with higher sound quality. Currently, I use them as my main running earbuds: these headphones are good enough to get me in the groove, but I wouldn't be too upset if they broke. So far, they have managed fairly well despite their earlier injuries and despite being sweated on a great deal. They still work just fine and sound exactly how they did when I first got them.Pros:-Cheap-Sound GREAT for the price-Reasonably durable-Ergo fit (fits very well in your ears & helps you figure out which side is which)Cons:-90 day warranty-Plastic housing-Thin shielding on the main cordThe Panasonic's also come with a mic and a pause/play button on the cord. It's also important to mention that these headphones are in-ears and fit pretty snugly in your ear, which I think helps block out sound and helps to keep the bud in place. For some people this could be uncomfortable and be a big negative for them.Overall, the Panasonic RPTCM125's are great-sounding headphones for the price, which you can take around with you almost anywhere. They will withstand a lot of abuse but, sadly, are only covered under warranty for the first three months. Recommended for the listener who wants a pair of powerful headphones for cheap.Update 7/10/2015:Changed my rating to better match my experience with the product.

title: Cheap Cost and Cheerful Sound
body: For the price, these are surprisingly good. I tested them on a Nexus 5 phone using Pandora One on a mix of music that does NOT include "rap" or other nonsense so I can't report how well they handle the "elephant ****ing in an echo chamber while someone screams dirty words" that that music is.OK, editorial aside, these provide a surprisingly good sound Not great but for the approx. $10 price you aren't going to get Sennheisers or AKG but quite respectable for casual listening to the kind of compressed music you get out of a phone or MP3 player.The sound is pleasingly neutral with everything rendered with acceptable clarity and no overly bright or dull sections within the frequency range (based on casual listening) There aren't going to pound out the bass but they do provide a relaxing listening environment for general use and they don't sound like tinfoil diaphragms in a drain pipe as some el cheapo headphones I've tested have done.They are apparently "breaking in" a bit and the sound has improved noticeably after a few hours. The bass is quite good for my taste without the currently popular excess many find fashionable.(Bought a $2 set of phones once just to see how bad they were..and they were *bad* with a capital "B".)The ear buds as supplied fit me just fine but there are two other sizes included. (Watch for them when you open the pack, they want to fall out and roll under things and play hide and seek with you.)There's a "pause/phone answering" button on the (as I wore them) right ear piece cord that works just fine with my phone. The cords are the usual "I'll tangle if you blink" sort of ultra clingy rubbery material. Which is one of the reasons for the four star scoring.The retail package looks like the standard "You're going to be bleeding before you get *me* open, Pal" type of blister pack but if you look carefully and persist, there is a small tab on one side of the plastic that, with some leverage and decent finger nails, will pry the package open without needing tools or stitches. But as I mentioned, watch for the extra rubber ear thingies to bolt for freedom when the package finally opens up.The warranty is the other reason for four stars: Darn thing is a joke. 90 days for parts and NO warranty on labor. C'mon Panasonic, you can do better that that and you usually do.Once you are past Amazon's 30 day return window, just toss these if they die, postage will cost you a third the price of new ones and with no warranty on "labor", who knows what Panasonic will try to charge you for service.Even good companies are getting ridiculous about warranties and trying to give you the old rubber peach when you're not looking. (But that's where a good reviewer comes in. I read this stuff so you don't have to.)Ongoing update: I have been using these regularly now several times a week for bus rides and down time in movie theaters and I am getting to like them more and more.The mid size ear plugs work fine for me and do a very nice job of isolating outside noises such as the endless blather of people on cell phones or the idiotic commercials you get bombarded with waiting for a movie to start.They do not block all sounds but they do a very good job and the difference is immediately apparent upon removing them. I would most definitely NOT wear these while walking around (but you go right ahead, the world is getting overcrowded and this is a great way to weed out the less bright segment of the population) as they'd do too good a job blocking traffic noises or someone coming up behind you with a decided lack of your best interests in mind.

title: Can't be beat for $6 or even $12, unless you can get the Sony MDR-EX100AP for less than $15.
body: Just received these along with the Sony MDR-EX100AP from Amazon: http://www.amazon.com/Sony-MDR-EX100AP--Ear-Headset-Smartphone/dp/B00BN0N03U/ref=sr_1_1_m?s=aht&ie=UTF8&qid=1434157881&sr=1-1&keywords=mdr-ex100apThis is a mixed review since I got both at the same time. Various music sources of various quality on the same computer with the same software and equalizer settings for all comparisons. I punch up the base, mids and the highs to +2.5-3.2 on an 10 bar setting eq and go about midway at +2db on the inbetweens. This is about as good as one can do with typical audio cards. The settings emphasize the overall range and punch in the bases with mids. This usually causes grief to slower responding headphones and clashes with high end distortions, especially when listening to scratchy vinyl recordings.1st song, Dixie Chicks Travelin' Soldier - I love this song, great dynamic range, instrumentals and harmonies. You get a base punch that is either missed or overruns the instrumentals with lower priced headphones. The Panasonic's played this very cleanly as it did with ever song, base was good and the resolution on the instruments was clean. I liked the sound of the Panasonic. It played the instruments very well, banjo, steel guitar, drums, base - all were discernible. It lacked the hard punch I like out of my base. Base was good, not great. Mids and highs were as good as anything I've used. The Sony MDR's were all of this and better. With the Sony's the base punched out from both sides and hit you in the middle of the head in full concert sound. There was an amazing difference between the two headsets. The Panasonic's played very well, but they were more sterile, kind of like just setting the eq to the middle setting for all frequencies (bars) and expecting something spectacular. The Panasonic's delivered very good to excellent sound and resolution, but the Panasonic's would not compete with a more expensive very good set. I changed the cups and reseated the Panasonic's and there was no noticeable improvement. Played with the eq settings and ran through the list again, still no real punch. Good Base, no where near the Sonys.This was the theme for the Panasonic's through Two Cello's - Smooth Criminal: Beethoven - Sonata 29 OPUS 106, B flat and Violin Concerto; and SuperTramp Crime of the Century. The opening of the Violin Concerto has a couple of kettle drums starting out very softly and the Panasonic's did not grab them until around the second measure. The Sony MDR's hit them from the get go and it just got better. I like all of these music pieces, know them very well and all of these pieces really pit Base and Treble against each other with the Middle frequencies messing things up. These music pieces break poor performing headphones and the Panasonic's held up very well though not great when compared to the $16 Sony MDR's.That being said, they are spectacular for $6 (get the green for <$5) and play better than most other brands I've purchased for up to $21, just not the Sony MDR-EX100AP. The Sony's are unbelievable, the Panasonic's are very good and you really can't go wrong with them at this price point and playback quality. I'd give them a 3 if they were $12.Both sets blocked sound fairly well. I played with the cup sizes and ear sealing. They worked the same and as expected were not truly noise cancelling. I was after noise cancelling (ok I'm cheap) and thought I'd try these sets. I did not expect to get true noise cancelling since not fully covering the ear, but I wanted something better than my travel set to use with the FiiO mini I ordered along with the earphones. Got about what I expected out of both sets.

title: Fantastic sound, tricky fit, low efficiency.
body: They sound EVERY BIT as good as my Sennheiser HD-280s. [[ASIN:B000065BPB Sennheiser HD-280 PRO Headphones]] (WITHOUT doing a "rapid switching" comparison.) Good enough for you to hear details you would not with other headphones. They can play loud and clear. But do not let that fool you into damaging your ears. (Oh God, that rhymed.) Very, very good, clear bass, but not for "bass-heads". (20 Hz low end cutoff, according to the specs.)For "super-bass" you go to V-Moda. V-Moda has (had?) something they dubbed "Bass-Freq" headplugs. THEIR DOWNSIDE: somewhat muddled sound.[[ASIN:B000FUICLY V-MODA Bass Freq In-Ear Stereo Headphone (Rocker Red) (Discontinued by Manufacturer)]] Though you can see the word "discontinued", the link should show you other "super-bass" (plug-type) headphones that are available.The level of noise isolation of the Panasonics is quite noticeable, but, of course, nowhere near the level of protective ear plugs. Even though, their noise isolation may have something to do with their capability to reveal details that other headphones do not show.I used to always use the medium rubber ear (fit) plugs with other headphones of this type. With these, the medium plugs made every head movement produce horrible rubbing sounds. Now I use the small plugs, and bury then deeply in my ear canals.Not too comfortable, but quite bearable. Do keep in mind that I have a high tolerance for discomfort when it comes to headphones if the sound is good. I would rather have uncomfortable headphones that sound great, than comfortable ones that sound bad. If you get to fit them the way they fit me, your ears will feel quite full and pressured.ALWAYS (at least once a day) clean your ear canals with a Q-tip before putting them on, or you will get them filthy, and clogged (to the point of sound loss), in a few days. And to hell with the warnings, just be careful, and use a turning, NOT "hammering" motion.This, of course, also depends on the amount of ear wax you naturally produce. My ears are wax factories. And I meet them with an obsessive level of cleaning.I do not want anyone to get hurt cleaning their ears. So here are some instructions:- Firmly grasp the cotton swab (a.k.a. Q-Tip) between your thumb and forefinger by the stick, but near the cotton. That way you cannot insert it too deeply into your ear canal.- Do a turning/scraping (NOT twirling) movement inside your ear canal.- You can obviously change the depth of insertion by grabbing the swab closer or farther from the tip.- If you (dare) go really deep you will have to switch to a twirling motion.- Personally, I have cleaned my ears all the way to the tympanic membrane using a twirling motion, without any problems at all. But I have very steady hands, and a devil-take-the-hindmost attitude that you may not share with me.And again: YOU HAVE TO keep your ear canal squeaky clean if you are going to use these headphones with any expectation of them staying clean, and not getting clogged with ear wax. At least if you are going to be as merciless as I am in terms of how hard (and deep) you stick them in your ears.Efficiency: 96db/W. Too low if you have one of those vanishingly small MP3 players. No problem at all with full-sized equipment.Bottom line: if you care about sound quality above all, it is probably quite impossible to find something that sounds better without spending a GREAT LOT more.These would make fantastic computer (laptop) headphones.

title: I like, but do not *love* these headphones. (UPDATE! Compared w/ Sony MDR-EX100AP/L)
body: I like, but do not *love* these headphones.--Price--I think I got these for $10 or $11 and for that price, they're quite good.--Build Quality--Not bad at all, really. I wear them to the gym, and they've held up well in that context... FAR better than the Alpatronix ES100 headphones that I had before these - and which fell apart under similar use. I will note that these things are every bit as small as they appear in the photos (although three earbud sizes are supplied), but please don't take this to mean that they seem poorly made. To the contrary, they're diminutive, but seem well put-together... again, FAR better than my old Alpatronix.--Sound (Output) Quality--Okay, here's where these little guys kind of stumble a bit. They're perfectly competent for spoken word (audiobooks, podcasts, etc.), but they *struggle* with bass-heavy electronic music, more so than the aforementioned Alpatronix ES100 headphones, even. That's not to say that they're awful (they're not... hence the four-star rating), but at a loud-but-not-deafening volume, you will get some popping and crackling when the bass gets going. Adjusting them in the ear canal helps *a bit*, but the distortion is still there. Simply put, audiophiles need not apply.--Sound (Mic.) Quality--I've been using these things during work videoconferences, and clarity-wise, they to be somewhere between my old Samsung smartphone headphones, and the Alpatronix. To put it into perspective, my coworkers said I sounded good with the Alpatronix ES100 headphones, but they HATED when I used my Samsung headphones and complained incessantly about them. With these Panasonics, one guy complained one time. So in general, they seem to do pretty well.--Summary--* Tiny, but excellent build quality.* Mediocre sound output for electronic music listeners.* Better sound output for spoken word audio, such as podcasts and audiobooks.* Good, but not outstanding mic. clarity.* Solid for the price, but not the "OMG AMAZING" experience reported by some.***UPDATE - May 26, 2015***Although these headphones are still going strong, I decided to try out another really highly-rated of sub-$20 headphones, because I wasn't really satisfied with the sound output of these things, especially given the type of music I listen to (see above). That said, I ended up going with the Sony MDR-EX100AP/L (Link: http://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B00BN0N03U/ref=cm_cr_ryp_prd_ttl_sol_0 ) which I picked up for $15.80 - which as of today, is about $5 more expensive than this set of Panasonics.The result? If you listen to electronic music (trance, house, techno, etc.), GO WITH THE SONY linked above. It's not that these Panasonics are *bad* in any way; it's that the Sonys... for just $5 more... put out far fuller sound, especially when it comes to bass-heavy music (but really, the mids and highs are better with the Sonys as well). Now... in terms of mic/phone performance, the Sonys and Panasonics are pretty much neck-and-neck, with maybe a VERY slim advantage to the Sonys, and if you are listening to spoken-word audio, you might be better served (and $5 richer) by sticking with these Panasonics, but for everyone who intends to use these for music, and who doesn't mind spending just $5 more, I suggest the Sony item linked above.

title: Don't buy if you want high quality sound
body: Summary: These are horrible headphones and I would suggest you look elsewhere.After reading the many great reviews on here, I decided to give these headphones a try, since I was traveling and wanted something simple.I tried to like these earphones. I even tried the various sized rubber inserts. I adjusted the volume on the various devices I used it on. Overall despite the brand name, these sound like $2 headphones from China. The issues I had were:1. I had to really crank up the volume to get any kind of decent sound. The drivers just aren't very sensitive.2. Too much midrange and not enough highs and lows. My guess is that the design of these headphones forces sound through a tiny hole that get routed into the ear canal. Sound that have the resonant wavelength for this size hole would be amplified, while highs and lows would be attenuated.3. The rubber inserts were uncomfortable. The design forces the inserts deep inside your ear canal. This makes the inside of your ears itch, so I don't think I can wear these for hours on end. I would prefer the older foam style that fits on the outside of your ear canal.4. Audio just sound faint and distant. I never get the feeling that I am right in the action.In conclusion. These are not Sennheiser so don't expect quality. They remind me of some JVC headphones I got once on an impulse buy because they were cheap. I have owned many fine Panasonic products so I was initially quite surprised at the low quality (In contrast, my 10 year old 720p Panasonic TV still looks better than most newer TVs). The only use I can imagine for these headphones is if you want something to fall asleep to, like comedy or talk radio but not music, and don't want to risk breaking good headphones (due to finding them under you the next morning) then these might suffice. I have broken many good headphones this way, so I won't mind when these Panasonics headphones break. (I got the white ones, in case it makes any difference)update: I have had this for 5 months now. I use it almost every day for video editing (mostly voice, sometimes intro music) and falling sleep to internet radio. It has started to grow on me. (maybe I have forgotten what good headphones sound like). I rate the sound quality to be adequate to good. I haven't really intentionally abusing it -- just use it normally. Sometimes I find it crushed under me, between me and the bed in the morning. So far it has been quite sturdy. So upping to 4 stars from original 2. The one thing that I still do not like is that it goes deeper into the ear than traditionally earbuds. So sometimes my inner ears gets itchy after prolong use. Also, it blocks out more sound that traditional earbuds, due to the compressible rubber inserts, which are quite soft and generally pretty comfortable. This seems by design for noise isolation purposes. So sometimes I have to remove the headphones to have a conversation, or to check to see if the phone is really ringing. Knocking one star for these quirks. Otherwise pretty good headphones for the price.Will update again if/when it breaks.

title: Good value - acceptable sound quality
body: *****UPDATE: 1 year later they are still working great.I wear them for hours at a desk and they don't fall out. The light weight coupled with them being compact make them the best earbuds I have owned for staying in my ears.As mentioned below the sound quality wont blow you away buts its pretty good for what they cost. I'm satisfied******If your expectations are to get high end sound quality from $8 earbuds AND you have experienced high quality audio you will most likely be disappointed in these earbuds.If you're trying to save a few bucks and set your expectations appropriately I think you will like them.Sound Quality:This is the most important aspect. I couldn't find a description of what these earbuds sound like before buying them, so allow me to do my best to describe what they are and are not (in my subjective opinion).Bass: Semi weak but present.If you know what a small subwoofer in an overly large sealed enclosure that is under powered sounds like, I would say these have that sound. The bass is there but not tight or authoritive, just present.Kick drum/mid bass is weak and washed out.Mid: Smooth at low to medium volume levels. Lacks some clarity but adequate.The best quality about the sound is the lack of harshness, especially around the 1kHz-3kHz frequency where many drivers without some kind of sound shaping can be harsh. Similar to poly cone smooth without any harsh peaks in vocals *at reasonable volume levels*Vocals are smooth. Extreme accuracy and presence is lacking.Highs: Soft, lacks sparkle, clarity and sizzle.Playing with an EQ at 16kHz maxing and cutting to minimum had zero affect.Ribbon tweeter soft without the clarity or presence.Distortion: Acceptable at low to medium volume level.High distortion at high volume.To summarize the sound:*Soft low end and high end *Smooth midrange without any harshness - slightly lacks clarity and presence.4/5 stars because:-They cost $8-I feel the majority of people will listen to them at normal listening levels where distortion is kept at acceptable levels.-The sound offers a lack of harshness (especially in the midrange) and the softening of the high end will not be fatiguing.-They strike a good balance in their category offering acceptable sound quality and acceptable distortion for a great value price.Cons:-Noise isolation is not good. I can hear everything going on around me.-The oval shape of the 'sound port' requires a bigger than normal ear-tip for a proper seal but feels a bit tight on the top and bottom leading to less comfort *for me*-Short cable. Unable to reach the phone in my pocket when wearing them *i'm tall*If you have more money to spend AND you're looking for strong bass, sparkle in the highs or a higher build quality I would pass on these.A better alternative for a little more money is the Sony MDRXB50AP which I consider to be the value leader.

title: ...Wow, I'm impressed. *EDIT Dec. 6, 2012*
body: EDIT: While I will admit these headphones are good for the price, the comfort of these are now really getting to me. Even after swapping the rubber tip sizes around, the oval shape of the part that enters your ears just make my ears hurt after more than 30 minutes of use.Plus, on songs that really emphasize bass, it is noticeably lacking compared to my other headphones (JBuds J4, JVC Xtreme Xplosives). That said, it's more than adequate for most songs, and you can actually hear it... my ears may have been saturated by the xtreme xplosives bass, since it is my main pair of headphones.Lastly, these are quiet compared to my other 2 headphones. Not an issue at all, but swapping earphones to hear how they compare, this becomes quite noticeable.Overall, 3.9/5.-----Long story short, get these. You will not be disappointed, and these will outdo headphones that cost more. Maybe not Bose more, but is very, very good. And cheap.In reality, I would give 4.5 out of 5 because the thing isn't comfortable in my ears no matter what I try.-----The long story:During black friday deals week, I came across a deal for the jbud j4s for $20. I decided to get it, given how I was (very heavy emphasis on was) impressed by the j2s and j3s I had. To get the free shipping, I decided to get these given the great reviews.By this point, my j2s and j3s had died. I had been using JVC xtreme xplosives to replace them. While I thought the j3s I had were perfect, the xtreme xplosives reminded me of the bass I had been missing.Over time, I've grown accustomed to wanting both. The j3s had clear mids and highs, but performed poorly in the bass (again, at the time, I thought it was adequate). The xtreme xplosives sacrificed some mids quality for lots of bass.Now, on arrival of my new sets of headphones (earlier today), I was eager to try the j4s I ordered. And... to my disappointment, it sounded very, very muddy. I could tell the balance between the bass and mids/highs was much better in terms of volume, but everything sounded a bit less than stellar. I'm currently burning them in, as is recommended to do.For fun, I decided to open these and see how far $6 went. And... holy ****.*insert a bunch more curse words and disbelief here*The bass is just about where I want it. The mids and highs are similar to what I recall hearing from my j3s.I'm tempted to order more pairs now so I won't have to worry about broken headphones for awhile.That is one thing about these headphones though: they do feel like they will break. And as they sit in my ears now, they don't feel as comfortable as my other available headphones. Unlike the other headphones I have, the bit the rubber tips go around is an oval shape instead of a round hole.For the price though, those are easy to overlook. If you need some amazing headphones for cheap, buy these. You will not be disappointed.

title: Nice basic earbuds
body: These are good basic earbuds. They do not have an inline button thing. They seem to be pretty good quality construction. The sound quality is also pretty good, not mind blowing, but very good. They seem to be a excellent value for their price. I got them because they are Panasonic brand & under $8. I got the kind without an inline button, because I hate inline buttons. I'm glad that Panasonic is still making earbuds without inline buttons. I hope that these will live up to the quality I expect from Panasonic & last a long time. One thing I noticed about the "ergo" design is that the speaker part on the earpieces are oval shaped instead of circular. I've never had any other "ergo" earbuds, so I don't know if that's unique or not. It doesn't seem to make a much difference in fit, but it can make changing the silicon pad parts slightly trickier than on a round one. The rest of the "ergo" design doesn't seem to be a big deal either. They don't seem to fit any better or worse than regular "non ergo" earbuds, but with the "ergo" design the ends don't stick out as much. They do seem to stay in place in the ear better than "non ergo" ones tho.They cost less than most regular "non ergo" earbuds, so I have no complaints about the ergo design.The last pair of earbuds I had were "fake brand" earbuds from a local store that cost $15 ("on sale") & stopped working after about a month. I don't beat up my earbuds, I just use them on my PC, I don't tie the wires in knots or pull on the plugs or stomp on the earpieces or anything like that. Yet it seemed like every time I got new earbuds they would cost more & break faster.I am sick of earbuds that have an inline button thing. I never use the button things. I don't even know why they are there, they don't seem to do anything. Yet it seems like every pair of earbuds simply must have them now. Some claim to have a microphone built in. I have no use for that, I use these to listen to my PC, not talk on my phone & my phone has its own mic anyway, why would I need one on an earbud? Some claim to have volume controls. I have never gotten any of these so called volume controls so do anything useful, I press the button, it does nothing; even if they worked I have better volume controls on my PC.My only experiences with inline buttons are negative. The last few pair of earbuds I had owned all stopped working because the tenuously soldered wires in the button parts broke. And the wires are too thin & the PCB is too tiny & fragile to try repairing. (I did intend to re-solder the broken wire, but two others - there were four in total- broke off just from me handling the PCB, the wires all looked the same & with no way to tell which wire went to which corner, I gave it up. It was not worth the hassle of dealing with a tiny part, seemingly designed to break at the slightest provocation)My conclusion is that the button things are built in as deliberate weak points of failure. Inline buttons are a blight to earbuds.

title: I have trouble comprehending that these buds cost $7
body: Well, first off, let's justify the reason why these are $7: The quality is very bare bones, I personally think they're not flimsy, but they're definitely not reinforced. Any excessive force and they will break, these were manufactured with the bare minimum in mind in regards to build quality. The wire is an old style dual cable that separates @ the "y" split, and the L-plug is an old style one that may be too big to fit in some present-day phone cases (although I have no problem plugging it into my phone or tablet case). The actual earbud itself, looks dated and (obviously) uninspired on a design level, like as if they were an afterthought from earphone tech from the late 1990's-early 2000's, but for $7 it's definitely function over form, that function being that it houses the driver & it does a good job of it, regardless of design, for $7, that's what counts. This particular model was meant to be the low-end, entry-level product for the Panasonic line of earbuds/earphones. These were probably made with no amenities in mind, and depending on the user, a pair that is expected to be thrown away after 6 months to a year maximum.Now, let's discuss why these *shouldn't* be $7. I've never heard such Sound Quality on a pair of entry level earbuds like these, like as if this is magic or sorcery. Every similarly-priced, low-end earbud I've bought in a pinch has always been severely lacking in the SQ department, but these, these are completely different. Now I'm not saying these will beat a pair of higher-end of earbud/earphone (think $50-$100 range) or professional IEMs (which, we all know can go up as high as $800), but, these things pump adequate sound, and it's amazing that they do. The soundstage on these are actually level for earbuds like these, a good amount of detail, but nothing really overpowering. The treble is good, very noticeable, the bass is tight, not too overpowering, the mids are a tiny bit muffled (I'm also assuming it depends of the quality of the codec, sometimes it's hit-and-miss for mids), but again, these are $7 earphones that manage to sound like $20-$30 mid-range earphones (where treble, mids, or bass can fall a tiny bit short, regardless), there had to be a catch somewhere (keep in mind that everyone's perception of sound is different, so you may like that, you may not). Regardless if there is a small caveat in SQ these things perform admirably for a $7 pair of everyday earphones. I'm really glad I made the purchase, and the blue color really looks snazzy too, reminds me of the beach & summer skies.Oh, one more thing, I use them while working out, and they have managed to stay in my ear (make sure to try all silicone sleeve sizes to ensure a proper fit), so, if you're looking for a pair of "gymphones" w/ good quality sound, look no further :)

title: Exceeded my less-than demanding needs
body: [*UPDATE* at the end] ~[original review]: Here's the thing: I really did not wish to be in the market for a set of earphones/earbuds or whatever these things are being called these days – but given the fact that I'd grown tired of barely being able to hear spoken-word audio through my smartphone speaker, whether it was during phone conversations, trying to listen to online programs, or hearing GPS directions while motorcycle riding, I decided it was time to invest in a "workable" set of something that could be plugged directly into my ear canal.Given that I'd had no interest in investing in audiophile-grade equipment at the requisite audiophile-grade prices, my main concern was finding something somewhere within that delicate balance of "Quality-enough" at just "Cheap-enough," and it seemed to me at the time that these just MIGHT have fit that bill: they did have the "Panasonic" name going for them, after all.But even I, who actually WAS trying to "cheap-out," was impressed with the crispness and clarity, as well as the unexpected comfort, of these buds. Again, they are not anything to go for if you're looking for Top-of-the-Line type quality or heavy-duty performance... but if all you're looking for is something simple and basic, and yet packs a surprising amount of audio quality, I can easily imagine less stellar choices. My only rating demerit being in regards to the uncomfortably thin wires which easily snag on things and get annoyingly tangled upon themselves – but hey... ya gits wutz yoo pays for, correct?I'd purchased two sets of these together – the other of which being with the added microphone feature: I've yet to test out that second set fully, but the audio output quality on that set matches this one, so there does seem to be consistency in that department.Definitely a "recommend" for someone who does not require much but who still appreciates those little pleasant unanticipated surprises that occasionally accompany such decisions._____["update" addendum]:After a series of hours'-long phone conversations using the "microphone" variety of these ear buds, I was so impressed with the results that I'm literally right now, as of the typing of these very words, in the process of ordering a SECOND "mic-equipped" set – one to be set aside strictly for "in-house" phone calls, and the other as a "travel set" in case of ever needing to make/receive phone calls outside of the house or while on the road.Still the same concerns relating to the ultra-thin wires, but still strongly recommending for those on a budget or who just want something simple, yet who BOTH are looking for something reliable and of surprisingly good quality.

title: I hardly ever rate products...
body: ....but this is worth my time.Being an avid runner and cyclist, I go through a ton of headphones. With that being said, ideally I want a relatively inexpensive product that provides both comfort and quality sound. Comfort above all; nothing bothers me more than having to readjust my headphones every other minute while running. I bought the Sennheiser OMX 680 Sports Earclip Headphones a while back because it came with an armband for my iPhone 4 and cost $50 (assuming price reflected quality)... worst purchase I've ever made in my entire life. For whatever reason, the headphones did not want to stay on my ears, the sound quality was horrible, they were uncomfortable, and the armband broke within a week. I ended up ripping them off my head and tossing them in a ditch while I was out running.Unable to exercise without music, I borrowed my roommate's Sony's for a while, which were very similar to these; they were 10x more comfortable than the 680s and 1/4 the price. I was going to buy the Sony's, but these were much cheaper and had a better rating overall. I am very satisfied with these headphones!Comfort & Ergonomics: 5/5Fit perfectly in my ear, they're comfortable and secure; based on my experience, ear-buds are best for exercising. Behind-the-neck headphones tend to interfere with my helmet (cycling), and ear-clips tend to interfere with my sunglasses. The buds are slightly angled to optimize both comfort and sound quality, and come in 3 different sizes.Sound Quality: 4/5Good when considering how inexpensive they are. The sound is crisp and the bass is distinguishable, yet not too overwhelming. The are also somewhat noise-cancelling.Durability: 3/5I can't really comment much on durability for I have not had the headphones that long. I have had them for about 2 weeks now, and though they're holding up fine, they're still relatively new. Since my hearing is not that great, I tend to have the volume turned WAY up, which I know is not good for the headphones (as well as my own ears) but, fortunately, it has yet to affect the quality. The same goes for sweat.Price: 5/5They're only $8! If you don't like them, you lose them, or they break... no big deal! And might I add, they shipped within 3 days. Standard shipping.Overall: 4/5If you're looking for a good pair of headphones for your computer, or for your audiobooks, or for movies on a long plane flight, then you may want to invest in something a little nicer like Bose's QuietComfort 15 headphones; one of the best purchases I've ever made! But if you're looking for something affordable, portable, and comfortable, I would most DEFINITELY recommend these!

title: I Understand the Rave
body: I have never purchased earbuds.I have always used the earbuds that came with my phone until they broke. The earbud that I have to compare this one to is the AKG tuned earbuds from Samsung that came with my Samsung Galaxy S8 long time ago. Well, after a couple months of use, the left side of the Samsung earbud stopped working, but I continued using the other side until the rubber around the earbud started tearing and became a mess. I have still been using it until today, when I received these Panasonic ErgoFit earbuds. I purchased these because it's the most affordable earbuds from a reputable brand that I could find (with almost 100k 4.5 star review average!) since phone companies stopped including earbuds with their phones to garner more profit from separate sales and expose us to even more radiation with the wireless earbuds for the sake of continuously advancing new luxury technology, fashion, and convenience. ANYWAY...Let me tell you straight away that the first thing I did when I received this product is check the back for any Chinese or foreign language text (after reading another review here about receiving a counterfeit product with pictures to prove) and did not see any of that. Also mine happens to be made in Vietnam rather than China if that means anything. I was not impressed at first sight. The earbuds are TINY compared to the Samsung earbuds I've been used to. The wire is also super THIN and fragile, I'd say, and needs to be taken care of to make sure they don't snap or break.I tried putting the earbuds on and was pleasantly surprised at how comfortably they fit. They definitely feel like they go in my ear farther than the Samsung earbuds did. I plugged it up and started listening to them and understood why so many people posted great reviews about them. I am not an audiophile(? audio expert), but I know I loved the sound from the Samsung earbuds and was hoping for something similar- and they definitely deliver in my opinion. The bass is a little more pronounced in these earbuds than the Samsung ones (maybe because they go inside your ear more? idk) and overall sound quality is satisfactory.The only reason for knocking off 1 star is due to the fragility of this product. The AKG tuned Samsung earbuds had thicker/sturdier wires and braided cable to make it even more quality/durable. A shame that it didn't last as long as I had hoped for, but now I will enjoy these ones and hope that they will last a long time.P.S. I purchased the NO MIC version and it does NOT come with volume controls or anything fancy like that. Just earbuds and wire to connect.

title: Overall, Very Good.
body: I got these primarily because $5 headphones tend to break too easily and sound extremely tinny and empty. Metal was borderline painful with such thin sound quality, and that being my preferred genre, that just wouldn't do. Being a student, I had to work within a budget, so at about $10, these were wonderful. They produce a sound with the body I would expect of pricier headphones. There is enough bass and treble that I can actually begin to lose myself in the sound, even while running to a class five minutes late. The sound quality, with the small exceptions I'll discuss below, is pretty great for the cost. I prefer them to the Apple ear buds which came with my iPhone 5s both for how well they stay in my ears and how good they sound. They drown out the people I live with, keep me entertained and thoroughly immersed in music when I feel like listening to music, and are sturdy enough to survive a very mobile college life, so they generally serve my needs.I probably would not buy them in orange again. This is more personal preference, but they are awfully gaudy. They let my friends know I can't hear them if we pass between buildings, which I guess is a nice warning system so they know to gesture for me to take out an earbud if they want to chat, but if I need to replace them, I'll probably buy something more subtle.On a more serious note, the design could be improved. The white part is offset towards the wearer's back from the part that's inserted into the ear and can be uncomfortable if your ear is shaped a little differently. This seems to help the ear bud stay in the ear, so perhaps it would be better if it were made of something a little softer. The hard plastic can cause some soreness in the cartilage if worn too long. As such, they're not the pair I'd choose for a midnight Netflix binge. Additionally, I've been noticing an odd whistling at certain times, particularly when there's a breeze or any substantial wind. It goes away as soon as I take off the ear bud, so I know it's some interaction between the air current and the ear bud. This is somewhat annoying, although not a particularly awful concern depending on what you listen to. It was annoying while listening to Johnny Cash, but I couldn't hear it when listening to Slipknot, so it's mostly a matter of what your particular needs might be.In short, I'd recommend them, but if you live in a very windy place, maybe consider something else if you prefer quieter music. Metal (nu-metal and thrash especially) is great, rock can vary, but acoustic might be better heard through something else.

title: Pretty good for what you're paying!
body: First off, don't think of these are replacements for $150+ Etymotics. If you do, you're setting yourself up for failure.That said, these are pretty nice, especially for what you're paying for them.I generally use a set of stereo bluetooth earphones for editing my church's podcast. I can generally get through two editing sessions (about four hours) if I'm lucky. I decided I needed a backup because the speakers on my Fujitsu laptop are horrendous at best.I also decided I didn't want to spend a ton of money on another set of earbuds that seem to get damaged at the drop of a hat. I tried a pair I got at the Dollar Tree Store, and they ended up in the trash about five minutes after buying them. So after reading enough online reviews I came upon these Panasonic earbuds.As sound goes, they definitely lean towards the bass end of the spectrum. Bass isn't overly booming fortunately. Unfortunately, the quality seems to be at the expense of the highs. I tried multiple styles of music to see how they sounded, and they were generally clear, albeit voices seem every so slightly muffled.Since I bought these primarily for editing sound that was a concern. But, for $7 w/shipping included, I'm perfectly happy with what I received.These come with three different sized silicone tips, with the medium size mounted as a default. With other brands I've had to switch out to the large ones. But, not with these. In fact, they fit very smuggly inside my ear canal; blocking out lots of outside noise. (Right now I've got my computer set to 34% volume, have Dr. Who running in iTunes, and when the soundtrack is running I can barely hear the Price is Right running on my television).I might be concerned if I used these at the gym because every time the cord rustled against my shirt I could hear it, and I even heard a slight heart beat when I first put them in (something I noticed big time with my high end Eytmotics!). Plus, it's a shorter cord than some headsets I've seen.As these don't have a built in microphone I'd suggest those looking for a pair to work with their smartphone look elsewhere. I purposely didn't want the built-in mic so that was a plus for me.Totally esoteric is that they come in multiple color options. Yes, there are the traditional white and black colors. But, they also have snazzy things like Lime Green, Violet, Red, Pink and Aqua Blue!!If you're looking for an inexperience option for earbuds, especially if you're not the gentlest user, these are a pretty great choice!

title: I doubt you'll find better sub-ten-dollar headphones.
body: Before I purchased these Panasonic RPHJE120 ear buds, I had Sony MDREX51 in-ear headphones for years. Three pair. They were comfortable, simple, but like all things under heavy use, fell apart or died. Yes, all three. A friend gave me a pair of iBeats in-ear headphones, and I'm glad I didn't waste my money on those--terrible little things. In short, I needed new headphones. Thanks to the magic of the Internet, I came across some positive thoughts on these cheap-but-effective Panasonic headphones. The price was right, and though public opinion is what it is, the price made me give 'em a shot.They're delightful, comfortable and sound lovely. If there were a category for stars given for price range, I'd have given them five. The sound is a bit more thick in the mid-range than the Sony MDREX51's with which I'm familiar (including the Sony MDR-EX57LP headphones I bought just in case). The Sony MDR line hasn't changed at all; that's a good news/bad news thing. They're bright and easy on the bass. Sometimes that is nice. The Panasonic RPHJE120 model, however, has a warmer sound, and a bit more rounded of a range. Your ears may vary.Pros:* Sound--warm, balanced, good range, handles power outputs and line-out surprisingly well.* Comfortable in the ear (sleep-test approved, at least in my realm).* 1/8" jack fits snugly, and in most players/phones with cases (...that I've tried, anyway...).* Price, price, price.The bad news is the build-quality. Granted, they're cheap headphones. The split-wire, "Y" design is as basic as it gets. I cannot prove they won't hold up until I've given them a shot, though. While the rubber ear "nipples" are comfortable yet quality, the plastic housing on the actual "phones" are a little thin, as are the wires. Something to keep in mind for those of you prone to dropping your devices and having headphones yanked from your ears, or those who tend to mindlessly store headphones on the floor (not that I'd ever do that... :blush:...).Cons:* Wires/plastic housing is thin--be careful. Thin wires also tangle a bit more when stored.* 1/8" stereo jack is not gold plated, can be a little scratchy in connections with certain devices.* Earbud "mounts" (the part you put the rubber "nipples" on) are oblong, and make nipple-swapping (sorry, sticking with that term) a bit tricky.Overall:If these hold up for at least six months of regular gaming/listening/traveling/sleeping use, I'm buying a crate of them.KW

title: Good earphones with forward bass, great treble, and recessed mids
body: These earphones look good, are very comfortable (so long as you use the right size eargels) and sound good, but have some flaws. I find the cord a bit short given that it's an L-shaped plug (which causes you to lose some cord length due to the curve) and the build quality is merely decent. In my experience, unless you treat these headphones with care, they'll break after a while (maybe in a year or two) unless you use them infrequently, like in a stationary location like your computer desk.Although to be fair, most earphones in the $10 range have the same problem. They're not that tough, and frankly, they're probably designed to break after a while, in order to force you to buy more. You have to buy higher end stuff like Moondrops with detachable cables (not the lower tier ones, which have non-replaceable cables) to get stuff that's designed to last.As for the sound signature: it's good, but these are definitely V-shaped consumer headphones with forward bass, and they're not really neutral. That's probably fine for most people, and the bass isn't TOO forward, but it does overwhelm the mids a little, and to a much lesser extent, so does the treble. The mids/vocals are a little behind the treble and moderately behind the bass. They're good when they're isolated, but get lost in a mix of sounds. When they're isolated though, vocals / mids are good, however.Don't get me wrong though, the overall sound quality is good. The treble is EXCELLENT, and surprisingly so. The bass is solid, and has enough presence without being overwhelming, so long as you use the right size eargels. The right size being big enough to create a seal inside your ear, but not an uncomfortable one. The default medium size is probably good for most people, but my ears a bit bigger than normal, so I needed the large ones. Creating a good seal really help with sound quality with most in-ear headphones really, especially if you care about bass, and even if you care about anything else really.All in all, would I recommend these earphones? Yes, I would. Especially if you want the L-shaped plug and don't need a mic, or specifically don't want one. Honestly, if the mids were more forward and the cord were longer, I'd give these headphones a 5/5 instead of a 4/5. Many people who aren't as picky as me probably would just consider it a 5/5 for this price range.Alternatives: if you like a straight plug, the JVC HAFX7 is worth looking into. If you're an audiophile who wants a neutral sound for cheap, maybe try the Moondrop Quarks

title: For Six Dollars, You Can't Beat These General-Purpose Buds
body: I own a number of moderately expensive bud-style headphones, including the (sadly) now discontinued Sony MDREX310LP and MDREX510LP models, as well as the Klipsch S4's. I also own a gaggle of inexpensive Sony buds that I use for nighttime use when listening to my audiobooks while falling asleep. When I found these Panasonic buds inexplicably priced at six dollars, I thought it would be worth a try to see how they perform.They actually perform surprisingly good. They are not going to compete with the expensive (or even moderately expensive) buds available today, but they serve more than adequately for music and voice. Why is that important? Well, as most people know, if you spend a large amount of money of a set of earbuds, the last thing you want to do is subject them to a brutal workout routine at a gym where they are pulled, yanked, stepped on, and covered in sweat. That means that, if you want to have an inexpensive pair of headphones for this type of use, these Panasonic's really fit the bill. They perform more than adequately, and, for their price, you can take them to the gym or some other demanding environment, and enjoy them without worrying about possible accidental damage. If they get damaged, you're only out six bucks.These buds use the offset-axis style used by some of the earlier Sony models, meaning that the bud is left- and right-oriented, and you have to slightly work them into your ear to get the proper fit. It's really not an issue once you get used to that type of design. The physical quality of the buds seem good, as well, and I don't think there should be any problem with breaking cords, etc. It's a pretty impressive package overall for such an inexpensive price. The set comes with three different sizes of buds, but due to the oval shape of the stem that holds the silicone bud onto the headphone, you'll have to spend some time working on it to get them on. (I suppose that a "plus" to this inconvenience is that it seems pretty unlikely the silicone buds will come off the stem.)One other item of note: these buds come in about eight or so colors, but not each one of these colors can be purchased separately using Amazon Prime. Some of the colors fall into the "add on" Amazon category, which means that you have to make a purchase of $25. But if you look carefully, some of the colors can be ordered separately (and using Prime, if you have that). It's a bit strange, but it worth nothing.I give this four stars for price, quality of construction, and general sound quality.

title: On the topic of cheap earbuds.
body: So I purchased these because of the ludicrous amount of reviews this product has (a little over 25k at the time of writing), and after a bit of use, I deemed these worthy of spending my money on. At $6.50, they put out great noise. I'm not an audiophile; I don't think I've ever spent more that $30 on any earphone, earbuds, or headphones. However, these definitely perform at what I consider to be good to above average. From my testing using an iPhone 5s, they draw enough power to push out good bass for EDM and dubstep. Rock music sounds pretty solid too.To note: I used skullcandy tips with these. The tips they came with are probably fine, but the skullcandy ones fit me fine as well so I just use those. You'll want to find a tip that can create a seal, as that's how you'll get the most out of these. These buds happen to come with 3 sizes of seals, small, medium, and large. I imagine mediums would probably work for most people, but you'll want to take the time to experiment with each size. The seal works great for noise isolation, which may be a bad thing should someone sneak up on me while I wear these.Now here's the thing about cheap earbuds: they usually aren't made of the best stuff. These are no exception. While the earbud parts of it are amazing, the rest leaves something to be desired. The wires are barely a step up from the earbuds you get for free on an airplane. They just feel delicate and thin.The other part that has problems is the jack. The one on here is L shaped, so it won't fit in some battery cases if you use those unless you have an extension on hand. This does not affect me personally. What does, however, is the build quality. Rotating it can cause static noises, and can occasionally just drop one side. I have walked around with no problems, but when I go to sit it can cause these problems to happen. This problem isn't so prominent on my laptop, however.For the cons, I docked a star, but I really do think these are a great pair to pick up anyways just for the sound and price. For me, these will serve as a backup, and probably as my go-to for listening to things on my laptop/desktop. I have another pair from monoprice that are braided, so I'll be using that as my everyday driver since they've put up with my abuse pretty well. They're less ergonomic, but they too put out good noise with better durability at a slightly more expensive pricepoint, but that's for another review. My previous pair broke, but I can't wait for the replacement to come so I can compare these.

title: Good, but not amazing. If you own a working pair of Apple Earpods, these aren't an upgrade.
body: I bought these based on the Wirecutter's recommendation. While I like them, I agree with the other reviewers who say that these are over-hyped. The sound is good and these are a decent purchase, but the Wirecutter review fails to mention some of the drawbacks, which I'll address below:1) The sound is good and ear pieces fit fine once you select the correct ear piece size. It ships with three options, so choose which works best for you - this took some trial and error for me. Taking the earpieces off and putting them on is frustrating, because they attach very tightly to the headphones. Ultimately, this is good because it means the tips won't fall off, but getting the right fit is a chore and took longer than I expected.2) The cord is cheaply made, and is the biggest disappointment of the product by far. For comparison, the wires are thinner than the wires of the Apple Earpods, and they tangle more easily than the wires of Apple Earpods do. The cord is the "cheapest" feeling part of the headphones.3) When in use, the microphone is on the right side, not on the left. This doesn't matter much, but it's worth noting. In my experience, this is the opposite of the way most other brands are.4) Due to the removable/interchangeable earpieces, the weighting of the earbuds is off. Because of the styling of the earpiece, the main weight of the headphones is on the outermost section that's furthest from your ear canal, and I feel like these headphones are also heavier than other similar models. Effectively speaking, it means that the earbuds will want to pull out of your ear canal, pulling in an out/down motion, even if the size of the rubber tip is the correct fit for your ear. For me, the headphones ultimately sag, with the middle of the earbud dropping by about half a centimeter. They don't actually fall out of my ears, but I can feel them sag. (And if you question the fit - yes, I've tried all of the sizes of ear tips). While this effect is always present with any type of similar in-ear headphones, the pulling effect is exacerbated because of the weight and design of this model.These headphones are still a very good deal for the price, but they're not the amazing wonder that the Wirecutter review makes them out to be. If you're like me and were hoping for a replacement for the Apple Earpods that came with my phone, don't bother buying these. They're good, but they're not THAT good, and they aren't worth buying if you already own Apple ones.

title: Consider these first
body: I'm a reformed audiophile and a committed audio bargain hunter. I rarely use earphones at home but was looking for a pair of sound-blocking earbuds for air travel. For these I was willing to spend up to $50.I took a chance on the Panasonics based on the huge number of positive reviews. Right out of the box they sounded cheap and disappointing: muddy bass and hissy highs, though they were comfortable to wear (I'm used to using ear plugs when I sleep). So I set about breaking them in.After running pink noise and 20-20000 hz sweep tones through them in a drawer for several weeks, the highs were tamed (although a bit rolled off) but the bass was still untidy. I guess that without the acoustic coupling of the earpieces to my ears the bass wasn't breaking in properly. After scolding myself for expecting too much from a pair of $7 earphones, I started to use them on my daily morning walks.After about a week of daily use, playing wav files from a Sansa Clip player, I started to hear and appreciate what everyone was talking about. These earphones aren't overwhelming in any respect, but they do what they do pretty well. On Gary Burton's "Next Generation" album, Burton's vibes had a pleasing ring, the piano and hollow-body electric guitar both sounded acceptably clean, with good attack and decay (the piano lacked a little weight, however). And the acoustic bass was still a little muddy but not annoyingly so.In short, these are a pair of earphones that I can enjoy music through for long periods, at a ridiculously low price. They do most things acceptably well, and their shortcomings (mostly attenuated deep bass and highs) aren't serious enough to spoil the musical experience. British audiophiles have a phrase for gear like this: "well judged".UPDATE: I bought a pair of discontinued JBL J22 earphones (about $15, marked down from $40) as a travel pair, and just to hear the difference between them and the Panasonics. The JBLs are somewhat better, with cleaner bass and brighter, more detailed highs (maybe too bright for some people). The JBLs give a more upfront presentation, making the Panasonics sound a little more "in the background". I also like the JBL's thicker, tangle-proof cord. But the difference wasn't as great as I expected, and the Panasonics are still a great bargain as everyday knockaround earphones. Sweat and salt water can damage earphones, so the Panasonics are my choice for exercise and trips to the beach.

title: Needs a User Guide
body: UPDATED REVIEW 1/3/23After using these only a couple of times and securely storing them with my Chromebook, which works fine with headphones, these earbuds developed some intermittent noise playing music on YouTube.
