I was all set to return them but then last ditch effort, tried smoothing the cord ... which was not twisted but a bit bent from original packing ... now they work fine again. Go figure......ORIGINAL REVIEWNeeds User ManualI'm using this version with a Chromebook computer.I've found the listening sound excellent, better than any of my other headphones. The earbuds are the right shape, are very comfortable, and do not fall out.That having been said, I have a suggestion for Panasonic: Make and include a user manual!The fact that there is no user manual makes it odd that the box has this stated warning:"Before connecting, operating, or adjusting this product, please read the instructions completely."The only insert paper is a small sheet with product specs, warranty info, and a blanket warning about hearing and volume of listening.I searched all over the Internet for User Guide, Manual, or Instruction sheet, but no more than the above was found. I think we can safely conclude there is no user manual and no instructions to read for this product and you are on your own.That was a setback when I went to test the mic online.According to the testing sites, the mic did not function. I was prepared to send it back, when I thought to try pressing the button on the mic.Well lo and behold, after pressing the button "to answer calls", the mic did show up on testing.A second press made the pickup even stronger, as evidenced by playback of the test. The second time, I could even let the mic dangle instead of holding it up.This is just a guess, but this mic button appears to have multiple functions, thus the need for a manual.Reading through some of the currently 200+ reviews that mention "mic button", people frequently mention using the button to skip songs. The product description says the button is to answer calls.But what all is the mic button supposed to do when using these buds with a computer!?Could the issue some reviewers are saying with the mic not working, or not pick up the voice well, be resolved with an instruction sheet?One star off for this omission.

title: Panasonic RPHJE120K In-Ear Headphone: Great once you learn how to wear them
body: I've had a love/hate relationship with these earbuds until I learned how to properly use them (I've had them for a few weeks now). These are my first pair of in-ear earbuds and as many have already said, they are to be worn sealed into the ear canal to get the proper fidelity. You cannot simply fit them into the opening of the ear and get any bass response at all. I knew this but didn't know how you were supposed to get them inserted properly and I struggled in the absence of any pertinent guidance. I understand that everyone's ears are shaped a little differently but what I found that worked for me was to insert the bud into the opening of my ear canal and rotate it while applying inward pressure (kind of like turning a screw) until the cable/shaft points upwards (see my pic). When the bud is situated this way it slides into my ear canal and makes the appropriate seal and I can run the cable behind my ear. No amount of inward pressure could get the proper seal with the cables hanging downward like you would think it is intended. It took me a while to figure this out. I also discovered that there is a fine line between pushing it in just enough to create the seal and pushing it in too far. Go too far and they begin to hurt rather quickly... and they hurt pretty bad. So find the spot where they seal but go no further. Again, it took me a while to figure this out. When positioned correctly I can wear them for extended periods.That being said, once fitted correctly - sealed in the ear canal - these buds provide an adequate (quite a bit actually) amount of bass and sound isolation compared to the regular headphones that simply rest in front of the ear opening. The bass was too much at first. I realized I had my EQ settings on my device set to a high bass boost to compensate for my old headphones. Once adjusted to flatten out the bass they sounded fantastic.I've never had any other high-end earbuds to compare these to but I feel that these sound great especially for the price. They do a very good job of keeping out surrounding noise. I used them on a recent flight and they did a MUCH better job keeping out the background noise of the jet than my old phones. Use caution when walking/running as they can block the sound of traffic and other hazards you would otherwise hear.Summary: These Panasonic in-ear earbuds are a great value and have exceptional sound... once you learn how to properly use them. Experiment to achieve the correct and comfortable fit.

title: Good stuff
body: A bit of context first: I don't buy expensive earphones because I loose/break them relatively frequently. I use headphones all the time. I listen to music on my phone daily and also use a set plugged into a bluetooth intercom system on a motorcycle. The latter has speakers, but I prefer using earbuds as they block out the background wind noise which help protect hearing at the same time allowing me to listen to music, make calls, talk to other riders and hear GPS instructions while I ride.For my purpose, these headphones have been great. Better sound than any other headphones I have tried in the <$20 price range and good at blocking out background wind noise while I'm riding (with a full face or MX/goggles helmet on). The relatively low profile allows me to put on my helmet without the earbuds popping out of place and they also stay in securely while running, cycling, etc. While this will depend on the individual ear, these headphones come with three different sized ear inserts, that are well made and fit securely to the base.Like with ANY earbud, fitment is key both to sound quality and comfort, so your bound to get a range of reviews. "Easy Tiger", does a great job of going into detail about proper fitment, so read his review if you are struggling to get good sound out of these. I'm very happy with the sound, but my expectations are also reasonable considering the fact that these are earbuds, not a $200 pair of over the ear headphones. I've have owned more expensive earbuds, but from my recollection (not a side by side comparison), any improvement in quality of sound is marginal.At this point, my one gripe is that the insulation on the jack is to thick for my phone case making it pop out and loose the connection. To remedy this, I carefully trimmed the insulation back about 1mm around the entire jack using a utility knife which solved the problem. There is no concern about cutting the wire by doing this as that portion of the jack is still metal. Whether or not that will weaken the connection of the wires to the jack remains to be seen. I will update if I have any troubles. Another option is to just make the case headphone cutout larger, but I have three headphones and one one case, so I went this route.I bought three of these and will by three more when the time comes. For my use, these are the best earbuds for the money that I have tried to date.

title: Price and Performance you can't beat at $7.00
body: I have MORE earphones and headphones that I would like to admit to. I'm a Serial earphone buyer. I've had everything from a $500 set of earphones down to these sub $10 set of earphones from Panasonic and Sony. My GO TO EVERYDAY earphones are still the Sony EX-71, which I've had for years. They do a lot things very well, non fatiguing. and with the right set of earpieces very comfortable. So when a set of my EX71 lost the battle with an exercise machine at the gym, I thought about scaling down a bit in price. The EX71 are about $50, not ridiculous, but I just need a good beat at the gym to keep me motivated. Lucky, I still have a few more sets of the EX71 to fall back on. Which BTW, I used to compare with the Panasonic and Sony noted in this review. I listen to mainly high energy rock at the gym, and acoustic new age when relaxing, or passing time on a plane flight going cross country. I also checked back on my GOLD standard headphones: Sony MDR-V600 - renowned by a lot of audio engineers as their GOLD standard as well. I would be remiss if I didn't mention my other GO TO earphones: V-Moda Duo (now called Remix). The only issue with the V-Moda are their kevlar wires that are indestructible but noisy. But, I really like the V-Moda overall sound.So, I bought two low priced earphones: the Panasonic RPHJE120 and the Sony EX10. What's another few earphones for my collection!Here is my impression: The Panasonic WON hands down, and at the price of $7.00 these are REALLY hard to beat. The Sony EX10 had plenty of bass, but the mids and highs lacked, and of the bass generated by the EX10's they were a bit muddy. The Panasonic HJE120 had good solid bass, went a bit deeper/cleaner in response and mids and highs were acceptable. You should note that neither of these earphones are top of the line, so if you don't expect much, you will be impressed. I see a lot of comments about the construction of the connectors, wires etc. I agree. What did you expect for less than $10? If your cat chews up the wires, it's not the earphones fault. And, lastly, you MUST MUST MUST get a good acoustic/physical seal with your ear canal with ANY earphones for them to perform well. It does take some fiddling and patience, but when you get it right, the ambient noise will be reduced and the earphones (which ever you get) will reward you with the best performance any earphones can provide.

title: A good bang for your buck
body: Let's start off by saying that I've been victim to purchasing goods based on the raving reviews surrounding them-- earbuds and other audio-related accessories are no exception. Some have been incredibly disappointing, while others have been unexpected goldmines. These fit somewhere on the "not bad/what I expected" level.As far as sound goes, the fidelity of these earphones are subpar. Bass is heavily emphasized, and this leads to mids and highs being incredibly muddled. If you're a fan of flat frequencies and/or classical music, these might put a small frown on your face. But then again, you can't really expect perfect studio quality reproduction from a pair of drivers being sold for less than $10. In fact, when compared to others of similar price, it's not too out of reason to say that these are quite good. That being said, these are by no means an alternative to better options out there.I was surprised by the build of this pair of earbuds. Sure, the wire is very mediocre and cheap, the casing is pretty much generic plastic, and the ear tips are pretty much standard faire. But, wow, color me impressed by how comfortable these are. I can definitely see myself working out with these are just using them for casual listening on road trips or whatnot. Unlike many of the higher-end in-ear monitors out there, these sit very light in your ears, and hardly feel like a pain to use. For those looking for an affordable pair of comfortable earbuds, I'd say that it's safe to stop your search here.I don't expect these to last me very long, and I'm only really using them as my "expendable earphones". If you're looking for a good, entry-level pair of earphones, please look elsewhere. A small amount of digging around will likely yield quality options set at under $50. Criticism aside, these are great for casual listening due to their relatively good sound (for the price range), high comfort levels, and their ability to block out exterior noise (I'm very impressed by the isolation that the ErgoFit provides) These also don't leak too much white noise or whatever the "hissing" noise might be during quite pauses, so that's another plus.tl;dr Great pair for what you're paying for. Comfortable. Decent sound. Good isolation. Don't settle for any less, and don't be afraid to look around for better.

title: My Lamentable Love Stories with Earbuds
body: Spouse often says I'm rough on earphones. I'm certain that I've never beat up any of them, nor have I scolded them for anything they did or did not do. In fact, when it comes to earphones, I've always acted like a dotting parent or sometimes, smitten admirer. I've always started off loving them to bits and they loved me right back for a while, then they grew up (headphone years are like dog years, you blink once and it's terrible twos, blink twice and it's rebellious teens, so the honeymoon usually did not last very long) and became obnoxious things. I have tolerated a lot of bad behaviours, but when they let the precious connection between the ear buds and the cord fraying away (and they always do), I have to put my foot down. Time to open the wallet once more.I can't count how many pairs of earphones have passed through my life that way. All with pedigree or well-known parentage -- Apple, Sony, Panasonic, Skullcandy, Dre, Plantronics, Koss, Philips, JVC, Pioneer, etc -- and all came and went without leaving a lasting impression in my memory. Except my precious Bose QC, but they fit over the ears so they don't belong in this lamentable tale.Enter this pair of Panasonic ErgoFit. Not the first pair of Panasonic earphones I've owned. Today is still a couple of days short of 6 weeks since ErgoFit and I became partners. We are constantly with each other and with beauty music flowing between us -- Agustín Barrios, Joaquín Rodrigo, Émile Waldteufel, Buster Smith, Toots Thielesman, Laurindo Almeida, etc. Chuck Prophet sometimes drops in and that's fun too. We also enjoy Cornelius Garrett, Michael Drew, Sian Phillips, Clifford Norgate and many others giving their voices to Georgette Heyer novels, over and over and over again.As mentioned above, it's not quite six weeks yet so my new relationship is still rosy. If I don't come back to alter this rating of 4-star in a few months, then it means I've found true love with Panasonic ErgoFit. Wish us luck.Edit: My faithful ErgoFit earphones were chewed up by a curious Bichon Frise named Bear during Thanksgiving week when we visited his owners, my husband's sister and her husband. Immediately after we returned home, I placed another order, and this second ErgoFit pair works just as beautifully as the first, thank you Panasonic.

title: Nearly perfect. Good sound. Indented ear buds so you can feel which ear they go in without having to look at them. Angled jack.
body: For a very cheap pair of earbuds these sound great. They have nearly every single thing that great earbuds have, which are as follows.1) Indentations/formation on the actual earbuds so you can feel which ear they go in without having to look for the L and R symbols.2) Angled input jack. Some earbuds have a straight jack input which leads to the internal wires wearing down over time after continuous plugging and unplugging.3) Good sound, obviously. Now I'm no audiophile, but I can't imagine any audiophile is even clicking on a pair of $6 headphones. I've owned plenty of Sennheiser, Sony, Bose, JVC etc midrange headphones and earbuds. Comparatively, these sound worse, but also come with a VERY steep price drop. So, for the price, these are probably the best because all earbuds die eventually, and I hate dropping 30, 50, 100 bucks on a pair of buds that sound incredible only to have them meet the same fate a year or two later if I'm lucky.Now lastly, the only real con I have; the wire. It is so thin and it's what really makes them feel like $6 earbuds. In my opinion, all the pros far outweigh the con. Not even a close battle either. I'm almost certainly going to be buying these again when the inevitable happens to my current pair, but of course I'll weigh my options when the time comes to see if anything potentially superior has rose to the top of Amazons highest reviewed list.TLDR; Nearly perfect. Good sound. Indented ear buds so you can feel which ear they go in without having to look at them. Angled audio jack. Only drawback is the cable is quite thin. Overall, great headphones and I can't find anything better for $6, or even under $10.UPDATE 12-10-2016; over a year later and the original earbuds are still kicking. I even fell off my skateboard going 20mph on a hill, slid on my side and frayed the casing for the wire, which partially exposed the wiring. That was 4 months ago, and it still hasn't stopped working lol. These things are seriously some of the sturdiest earbuds I've ever owned. I liked them so much that I bought a second pair just so I could keep one in my car and one at home. Price went up though. They were $6 (as the review states) when I bought them in 2015, now at the end of 2016 they almost doubled. Still worthy price, but a shame to see.

title: Very decent sound and comfortable too
body: I have been using a pair of Ultimate Ears TripleFi 10's as well as a pair of Shure SE530s. For kicks I decided to test a few varieties of cheap earbuds and then give them away to family members. I compared these Panasonic RPHJE120K to the JBuds Hifi Buds and the GOgroove audiOHMs (almost twice the price as the previous two).As background on the comparison, I'm playing 256kbps variable bit-rate MP3s through a Sandisk Sansa Clip (which sounds great by the way).Like the other buds in my comparison, the construction of the RPHJE120Ks is definitely what you'd expect from a sub-$10 pair of headphones. The buds come in a hard plastic package with an extra set of plastic seals. The cable is very cheap and thin and short. The ear bud itself is oddly shaped with the rubber seal protruding at an angle from the driver of 20 degrees or so.Honestly, I wasn't expecting much considering I'd been listening to my UE 10's for an hour on the drive home. I played the same song over and over, switching between the three budget pairs listed above. Then, I'd switch to a song with different dynamics and do the same.The first thing I noticed is that these sound very good for the price. I like the overall sound of the Jbuds slightly more, but I'm able to compensate for the lower amount of bass on these by switching to a different EQ setting. The other thing I noticed is that these buds are surprisingly comfortable. Like the JBuds, they sit in the ear, and then disappear, something I'm not used to with my UE 10's which sort of protrude from the ear in a very noticeable way. Maybe because the cables are so thin, I didn't notice any cable noise either.In comparison to the other budget buds, these are a very close second to the JBuds and far exceed the Gogroove AudioHMs (which are almost twice the price). What these don't compare to are my UE 10's or Shure SE530s. The difference is night and day both in construction and sound. But then, these are not meant to compare to those other ear buds, considering that they are 20 times cheaper in price.For anyone looking for a decent sounding (albeit very cheaply constructed ear bud) I'd say, check out the JBuds Hifis first, but you can't go wrong with these either.

title: Love it!
body: These are by far my favorite earbuds, I've bought these time and time again. I keep trying different companies and eventually come back to these. I use my earbuds all the time, literally every day. I throw them in my pocket and carry them with me everywhere. So basically what comes in the box is pretty standard for earbuds, the earbuds themselves, there are three different size ear bud ends, and some paperwork.Pros:-great sound-fit well in ears-stylish-comfortableCons:-don't last very long. (Mine lasted on average about 3 months, however, for $6 it's unbeatable)Let's go over the earbuds in more detail now.PackagingThe packaging was nothing special, just a standard plastic case. The ones that are a pain in the ass to open. It took me about 2 minutes just to open the box. However, it's not the box, but what's in the box that matters.Build QualityBuild quality wise, these earbuds seemed ok, the wire was fairly thin but unless you where pulling on it, it doesn't seem like the wire would give way. The 2 earpieces themselves are made of plastic, once again, it doesn't seem that the plastic would break unless you abuse the crap out of these earbuds. As for the headphone jack, I like how instead of having it go straight it turns, so if you rest your phone on the jack, it won't wear off the wire joint.ComfortThese earbuds are quite comfortable, I wore them for about 5 hours straigh before I started feeling pain on the inside of my ears. I also like how well they actually fit in your ears, they don't feel like they're hanging out of you ears, they feel like they are in your ears. So if you wanted to go for a jog with these earbuds, I don't think it would be a problem.SoundThe sound is very good. It isn't going to blow your mind, but they do sound very good for the price. I use them for listening to music all the time and I enjoy every second of it.SummaryIn the end, I would recommend these to anyone. They are good earbuds. I have purchased these earbuds 3 times now, and I will probably be back again soon.If you found this review helpful please click the helpful button. I appreciate it!

title: Very surprised
body: I recently lost a pair of apple headphones and didn't want to spend $30 on another pair of earbuds. I looked up some reviews and found these headphones were getting some good reviews so I decided to take a chance. I've been using these headphones for the past 4 hours and have been very impressed.One major problem I had with the apple headphones was they seemed to always be just to big for my ears. They would always fall out for no reason. Also, after an hour or two of use my ears would begin to ache and I would have to take the headphones out. I have had no such problems with these pair of panasonic. They have 3 different sized attachable gel pieces which allow for a better fit to your own ear. Many $30 headphones don't have this option, but these $7 ones do.One other thing I really liked about these headphones is how comfortable they are. I've been listening to podcasts and music for about 4 hours straight now and am only now starting to feel some minor ache from use. The gels really help with the comfort while also blocking out alot of unnecessary noise, which also allows me to turn down the volume of the audio which saves my ears from any hearing loss.On the downside, the wire seems very flimsy. You can hear in the headphones whenever the wire is being touched, that booming kind of effect? Also, the wires aren't connected to the plastic of the earbuds themselves, I can seem me accidentally snagging the wire on something and then it comes ripping out of the earbuds. But seriously, for only $7, what do you expect?The sound quality is pretty good. I would say slightly less crisp than the apple headphones, but very good bass response and pretty good mids, highs are kind of muddled in with the mids. These are really good for casual listening, like audiobooks, podcasts, some music. Don't expect these to sound like Bose or Shure, they are good but not that good, and again only $7 so...Overall, I am very very happy with this purchase. They work like they should, didn't cost alot, are cheaply made and don't have the best sound quality, but are only $7. If you need a pair of replacement apple headphones but don't want to break the bank, I would give these a try.

title: Very solid all around headphones
body: Usually, I am not the biggest fan of ear buds because they always fall out of my ears when I'm listening to music and doing pretty much anything remotely active. But what I love about these Panasonic Ergofits is that they come with different size pads that slip on over the speaker (the part you insert in your ear). These headphones come with 3 different size pads: small, medium and large. They arrive with the medium size pads already on the headphones, so if you just use the headphones as they come you will experience the comfort of the medium-size. I wore the headphones with medium-sized pads for a couple of days, but I found that when I went running or when I worked out at the gym the earbuds would almost always fall out. So I switched to the smaller sized pads and had absolutely no problems. I even tried the large-size pads, but I guess my ears are small because I could hardly keep the headphones in for 5 minutes, physical activity or not. With three options for different size pads/ears, I would give these headphones a comfort rating of 5-stars. Especially since other earbuds I've used with different size pads have often not had much of a difference between any two sizes of pads, the small and the medium size for Skullcandy, for instance, is almost identical.I think that durability and price are two more strong points for these headphones. I've dropped weights on them at the gym, sat on them by accident, and pulled them out of car seats and I have yet to experience any damage to the headphones. (They're hardly even scratched!) I would give these headphones a durability rating of 5-stars.While the sound quality for these headphones isn't incredible, it's definitely better than you would expect for a pair of earbuds that cost <$10. They tend to lose significant sound quality when the music has too much bass or too many highs, but even in the basic iPhone equalizing system, it's possible (though not easy) to balance the quality out a little. I give these headphones 4-stars for sound quality, and 5-stars for price efficiency.Overall rating: 4-stars (I just couldn't get past the sound quality issue, but I couldn't give them less than 4-stars because of the price point).

title: 4-year review: Good durability and customer service
body: I bought my first pair of these way back in 2017 when I was still in college. I'm no audiophile, but the sound quality was definitely adequate. Durability was generally good, although both inside mesh covers got destroyed almost immediately and needed to be removed. They lasted me through many nights of studying and a couple of degrees, until I pre-emptively bought a replacement pair along with a couple more as gifts in mid-2020. Ultimately the first pair lasted about three years before one side began to fail - I could still hear music out of it, but the volume was greatly diminished. I think three years is a pretty good lifetime for what was a $8.37 purchase, and I opened my second pair with equally high hopes.The second pair was a lot more disappointing. Like the first, the inside mesh covers failed very quickly. Also like the first, one side started to fail with low volume. But unlike the first, that side began to fail after only about a years' worth of usage.Not to be deterred, I contacted Panasonic as instructed by their Amazon page. They told me (in what seemed to be an automated email) that this product was not repaired but would be replaced entirely if it fit the warranty criteria. I would pay the cost of shipping to their repair facility in another state, and they would pay to ship it back to me. I sent out the broken headset in an envelope and waited several weeks with no correspondence from Panasonic.Nonetheless, I followed-up directly and was asked to provide proof of purchase (and to provide a purchase date). Again the line went cold for a couple weeks, until suddenly a box containing replacement headphones showed up at my doorstep. Now these actually didn't turn out to be the exact same model - my RP-HJE120s were now RP-HJE125s. But as far as I can tell, they are very similar and seem to work fine for now.In conclusion? Great durability on the first set and OK on the second. Customer service took some time and prodding to get back to me, but ultimately replaced the failed product which is exactly what I was hoping for. Thanks Panasonic, I'll be coming back again in (hopefully) a few more years.

title: Decent sound, excellent value
body: Pros:* Cheap.* Decent sound, not great but nothing terribly annoying like distorted bass or ear-piercing, overblown highs.* No microphone or controls, which are always the first thing to fail in my experience.* 90 degree plug, these seem to last much longer than straight plug, particularly if you put your phone/iPod in a front pocket and sit down.* Low profile, they don't stick out.Cons:* Cheap construction. The cables are extremely thin and tangle very easily.* Somewhat muddy mids and upper-mids. Has a little bit of that "listening underwater" sound.* Plug pops out more easily than my other headphones, a minor annoyance.* Really needs a clip to hold it in place on your shirt, especially if you run the cable under your shirt, since the spot where the wires meet will get caught on your shirt neckband and pull them downward.To be clear, these are EXCELLENT for the price. I've tried a variety of cheap earbuds active use (gym, bicycling, working in the garage) and these are by far the best. The sound is a bit muddy in the upper-mids, but they're so much better than most other low-end headphones I've tried.Regarding the missing headphone controls and microphones, they always cause me problems, so I'm glad they're gone. Sweat will get in there or they'll rub on my shirt, and suddenly Google Now is talking to me, or the phone is redialing, or the songs are skipping. They're just an annoyance and a point of failure. In my opinion, either unplug the headphones and take the call directly or get a proper headset. Obviously this is subjective.Their low profile makes them great for bicycling. They're almost entirely covered from the front by the tragus (the bit of your ear that sticks out in front). This means very little wind noise. Many other headphones I've used stick out far enough that you'll end up hearing the wind whistling past them and have to significantly increase the volume.My point of comparison for sound quality are Etymotic ER4 and Sony MDR-V6 headphones which are vastly more expensive than these Panasonics.

title: Better earbuds, but still earbuds... three models compared.
body: These are pretty good headphones... let's face it, ear buds are always going to be a compromised form of listening (as is anything compressed into an MPEG or MP3-4, etc., anything other than full bandwidth AIFF or WAV), so no earbud is really going to knock your socks off... that said, these are some of the better ones I've tried.I previously used [[ASIN:B000IG66VS JLab JBuds Hi-Fi Noise-Reducing Ear Buds]], and while they tended to sound pretty good, they suffered from a critical flaw: the rubber earpiece was not firmly attached to the plastic ear bud, so it would fall of sometimes as it was taken from my suit pocket, or even taken out of my ear. And while commuting on either a commuter train or a busy street, that's the formula for a lost earpiece. No thanks.I then went with Panasonic... [[ASIN:B003EM8008 Panasonic RPHJE120K In-Ear Headphones]] to be exact. While these were pretty good (and had plastic ear buds that stayed firmly attached), they always sounded a bit hollow, as though a steep filter was placed on any frequency below 120Hz or so. This was sometimes corrected when I moved my head, and even when I yawned of all things, meaning they weren't sitting well in my ear. Still, they worked all right, and made the commute a bit better, but there were many times I wanted to hear a real recording of what I was listening to, rather than the 'approximation' I felt the RPHJE's were producing.Which brings us to this product. They have ear buds which do not come off... check. They are not as thin sounding as the other Panasonics, check. These sound pretty good. While there is still compromise in the low frequencies, it is definitely less pronounced in these (meaning there is more bass). And while they don't replace good speakers and a CD, they do the job of playing back music for the morning commute quite well. So they are the best of the three types I have thus far used.There is a switch on one of the earbud cables for cell phone calls, but I have not used it, so I can't vouch for its quality or functionality. But as far as music playback earphones go, these are a good choice.

title: Great headphones, but better options are available if you don't need the mic
body: About a year ago, I paid $60 for Etymotic MC5 earbuds. These sound every bit as good as those ever did -- And the Etymotics didn't stand the test of time. One of the buds started making a horrible rattling sound after about a month or two. Don't know about longevity on these yet, but I'd buy them again even if they only last me 3 or 4 months.These are great headphones, although if you don't need the built-in mic, I would recommend the very similar Panasonic RPHJE120S model. They're about $8.50 and, believe it or not, I think they even sound a little bit better than these. I bought both because I tend to lose/break headphones a lot and I wanted to have a backup pair anyway. But I was also interested in comparing them. The cheaper RPHJE120S model sounds just as clear and rich, but seems to have slightly more volume and bass. They are very comparable, though.If these make your ears sore at all, use one of the smaller tips included. I'm a big-ish guy, so I assumed I'd need one of the bigger sizes, but the medium one was killing my ears. I switched to the small tip and it's perfect. It still seals the ear canal, just doesn't squeeze as hard.I only have two minor complaints about this model. One is cosmetic -- the cord looks and feels cheaper and flimsier than the comparable Apple buds. (The RPHJE120S ones have a similar cord as well.) Certainly not a dealbreaker -- especially considering the price -- and it doesn't affect the sound at all. My other complaint is that the button doesn't control volume the way the Apple ones do. Instead, it pauses and plays a song. On a Mac, it launches iTunes and then plays or pauses your song, which is kind of annoying if you're using Spotify, for example. It doesn't work the same way as the play/pause button on the keyboard in that way.I'm giving these 4 stars instead of 5 mainly because the lack of volume control/annoying button behavior and because I think the cheaper buds are actually slightly superior. But they're very, very good. If you DO need the mic, it would be hard to do much better for the price.

title: Great for the price but has occlusion issues
body: I ordered these to give to my sister after the trade-show freebie buds she was using broke. I also bought a pair for myself as a backup to the Yurbuds I had been using.The fit is excellent. They have an oval shape that fits the ear canal much better than the standard circle shaped buds. Also the offset and angled driver fits the contours of your concha and inter-tragal notch (bowl of the ear and notch at the bottom) very well.How do they sound? Pretty good. They seem to have a very flat response. Coming from the Yurbuds which spike at 4K and trail off from there, these seemed to be missing something in the midrange but they have the highs and the lows as well. (as long as you insert them deep enough in your ears.) I work at a hearing aid company so I might bring them in sometime to test their frequency response more precisely.The main thing I noticed was the Occlusion. (blocking of the ear canal) My Yurbuds did not occlude and I could hear outside noises pretty well. These do occlude and thus block external sounds to some extent as well as amplify internal sounds like swallowing, breathing, etc. Tapping the cord results in an audible sound in the ear. The occlusion is the reason I only give it 4 stars, other than that, its a great earbud for the price and I'm sure I'd have similar complaints about any other occluding style earbud.Update: I checked their frequency response in an OES (occluded ear simulator) coupler. They do have a bit of a dip around 1KHz but they're pretty strong out to 10KHz with some response (25dB lower) out to 20KHz and beyond. The graph shows both the occluded (jammed in you ears tight) and non-occluded (loosely placed in you ears) response to show how it affects the sound. This difference may account for the wide variety of reviews on its bass response however this issue is not unique to this device. All earbuds exhibit this effect. Most people should be able to find a semi-occluding fit with the included silicone buds that gives them a relatively flat response out to 10KHz.

title: The Best Budget Earphones w/Mic
body: I purchased these earbuds as just a pair to keep in the car and they turned into my daily drivers. I had a pair of nice quality, good sounding Samsung earbuds that I received as a gift. After one too many times of either forgetting the Samsung ones at home or leaving them in the office I purchased these Panasanic Ergofit earbuds as a backup.When I received these in the mail, I popped them in my ear only to be disappointed by the fit. That is until I realized a little trick of putting them in my ear slightly turned to the back and gently wiggling them until they almost "lock" in place. Once I did this I quickly found that these are some of the most comfortable earbuds I have ever used. I generally can't wear earbuds for long due to earache/headache problems, but I can wear these all day long. I have even used these while running and they have stayed in place pretty well.The audio quality is right up there as well with great volume and range with very clear sound. The ear buds do a decent job of muffling background noise just enough to help enjoy the audio, but not enough to completely block you off from the world. The play/pause(answer/hang up on calls) works well with my Note 4 and Nexus 6P.There are a few downsides to these earbuds. First, the length of the cord is pretty short, much shorter than I am used to. It made using them at the gym difficult at times. Also, the noise from a rustling cord can get very distracting and interfere with the great audio quality keep the cord from bouncing around or use them sitting or stationary for best results. The microphone has passable quality with a few people on the other end of the call mentioning background noise getting picked up by the mic, but it not being too loud or unbearable.The bass these provide is decent, but I would have liked to get a little more out of them.Overall, these are very good budget earphones that have served me well. The pros have made these my go-to earbuds and the few downsides these have aren't enough to make me want to look anywhere else.

title: Very nice but
body: I also bought these because of the Wirecutter review, of the best headphones under $30.00. I'll give you the thunder, right up front. These are Great head phones......but Their review made them sound like they were about the best headphone at Any price. Well, that's not true, but they are the best I have found for this price.It has good, not great bass response. They don't rattle under the stress of allot of bass, but, I'm a classic rock listener. When listening to a Dio song, they washed out, just a bit under the strain of, "Last in Line, Live," and Lincoln Park's "It Doesn't Even Matter," Now that might not be fair, because I was really testing the limits of a pair of $16.00 headphones, but my $149.00 Dre's don't wash out under anything.My point is, if you're expecting the Wirecutter review to be gospel, with the, "Best at any price," claim, it might be a bit of an stretch.I'd say it was a Great guideline.They have a nice mid, tone, treble, without being too tinny. Nice full sound, and yes, they do have a good bass response. They do sound like headphones that cost much, much more, but don't, (As I did), think you're getting Bose, or Dre quality bass.Very pleasant tones, easy to listen to, without cracking your ear drums.Should you buy them? Absolutely, especially if you don't want to drop too much dime on a pair of phones, we'll all lose one day anyway. Don't misunderstand what i'm saying, These are Very, very good headphones, and are the best of several worlds. Pleasant sound, a Very good working microphone, and comfortable fit.Sound is also a very subjective thing, like a fine wine, or cigar. Everyone has their favorite. I'm just saying don't expect a set of Bose, or Dres.Wire cutter got a little too excited.Just one guy's opinion, so please don't slam me too hard, but I just wanted to warn the next person who's expectations might have been just a tad too high.

title: I have been using this product for a good 3 months now
body: This is my first review on Amazon. So, please bare with me as I will be a bit liberal in my tone.Pros:I have been using this product for a good 3 months now. Previously, I owned Sennheiser MX 300, Sennheiser 275 and Philips Ear headphones. From my past experiences, Panasonic lived up to my expectations. This piece gets better with time. Although it took some time to burn (app. 70-80 hours), but after that it works like a charm. It gives a neat bass and clarity with a decent pitch. I listen to heavy metal most of the time. So, I can say that this product provides excellent bass and I can easily distinguish between the high notes and the lower ones. The plugs fit neatly inside the ear and are quite comfortable. I would say it is not perfect but rather smooth in terms of listening. The chords are decent in length and looks firm since it has a closed joint at the end making the wires rugged.Panasonic did a great job by putting these ear phones in an excellent price range. I can vouch for the fact that one will not get any other decent product in this range. I am a student and it doesn't hurt my pocket which is again a plus.Cons:1) Not suitable for running as the plugs go inside your ear canals which may be fatal if you accidentally pull them off.2) The ear buds provided by panasonic are little bit rough and with much use, they worn off real quick.3) I am not satisifed with the treble as sometimes I feel itchy when I raise the treble and dim the bass.Overall, Panasonic provide a great product in an excellent and affordable price range. If you want to experience greater sound without hurting your pocket, this one is a must buy. I consider myself an audiophile and as far as technical qualities are concerned, I would say 4.5 out of 5 since it provides a great bass with a decent treble and is fit for any musical genre. In the end, a good product which will put a smile on your face when you use it.

title: Good value for very low-priced earphones for voice
body: I use my earbuds primarily for recorded books, podcasts, and radio, so I am interested in good, crisp sound quality but do not require the kind of nuance I would want if I were listening to music. As a result, I do not normally buy very expensive earbuds, and these certainly came at a good price.I tried both versions, with or without the microphone. The microphone control is quite small and not easy to find or use unless you are looking at it. In addition, the Microphone button is labelled “Call”, which is rather misleading. This button activates the app on you phone that accepts voice input, like Google or Alexa or Siri, but it does not control the calls at all, e.g., pressing the button during a call mutes your input but does not end the call. I like that they have a right-angle plug rather than a straight-line plug; the right-angle plugs lie closer to the device and are less likely to snag or be knocked off.They are easy to insert and light and comfortable to wear and have 3 sizes of earbuds in case your ear opening is larger or smaller than average. The earbuds are marked for Right and Left, handy if you have a magnifying glass to read the small print! The sound quality seems optimized for for voice and gives crisp clear audio for recorded books and phone calls but is probably not up to the performance I would want for music. The earphones I have been using, Betron BS10, deliver better sound quality for music but cost 50% more (in the version without a microphone).I do not look to my earbuds to make a fashion statement, so for me the bright color was not an attraction. I have come to like it, though, because it makes it easy to find my earbuds.Inexpensive earbuds like these are not built to last a long time, but the quality of the construction looks reasonable for the price. If you are looking for earbuds that deliver decent voice sound at a good price, these may be just what you are looking for.

title: Excellent earbuds (from an audio professional)
body: These are surprisingly good earbuds, and not just good “for the money.”I’m a sound designer and mixer by profession, so I appreciate the fact that these have clear, transparent mids and highs, but also pretty decent, well balanced bass.To be clear, you will find others out there that intentionally exaggerate certain high and low frequencies, which will make them sound “better” to many people, because that’s how psychoacoustics works - but you won’t be hearing the sound/music as it was mixed and intended to be heard. Headphones like that can be more “fun” to listen to, but if that’s what you’re looking for, then these aren’t for you.I can’t attest to exactly how flat (or “true”) the frequency response on these is, because I haven’t looked at the data - but I can tell you that when I listen to things I’ve mixed myself in the studio, these sound closer to what I mixed in the room than many other earbuds or headphones you can spend a lot more on.One important thing: do be sure to choose the rubber ear-pieces that fits you best, because if they aren’t seated firmly in your ears, making a good seal, you will definitely *not* hear enough bass. I can’t emphasize that enough.As for noise cancellation, that’s not what these were designed for, so there are no electronics inside actively creating that effect. However, once they are firmly seated in your ears, you will get a good bit of muffling of the ambient sound around you. But if noise cancellation is a high priority for you, you should get some specifically made for that - and expect to pay many times more for them (I believe the key phrase you’re looking for is, specifically, noise “cancelling”)For the record, I like these so much, and they’re so inexpensive, that I’ve bought quite a few pairs that I keep stashed in every jacket, every carrying bag, and in my car, so I always have a pair wherever I am.

title: Surprisingly Successful Purchase
body: When my latest pair of earbuds finally caved under the constant abuse I put them through, I began the task of finding a new pair. Not wanting to go to a store and pay more money than I absolutely had to, and wanting them ASAP and getting two-day shipping with Prime, Amazon seemed a natural choice. I spent a few hours reading reviews and comparing prices and finally settled on these Panasonics.They came in that impossible-to-open packaging; I was terrified trying to open it that I was going to damage the ear buds but once I got them out they were damage-free. They come with three sizes of rubbery-in-ear buds - the ones attached and two more pairs, one smaller and one larger. The cord isn't as long as some other ear buds, which may be a problem for some, but for what I've used them for hasn't presented too much of a problem. It is something I feel like I should mention, though. The audio quality is surprisingly good for $6 ear buds. Is it colossally awesome? No. Is it solid? Yep. No crackling or static to be heard. Not noticeably tinny, nor is bass over or underwhelming. Was I suitably impressed, enough to be satisfied with my purchase? Absolutely.I've always been wary of this style of ear bud. Previous encounters demonstrated them as being uncomfortable or painful, or just not capable of staying in my ears. I've worn this pair for hours at a time with no complaints, and they stay in my ears when I'm out jogging in the morning playing Zombies, Run!. I have to be careful not to put them in too tightly when I'm out running, because they seem to have a pretty serious noise-cancelling effect if you get them in tight enough and that seems to be tempting fate with cars. But even a "slightly less snug" fit will stay put during most exercise.Overall, very, very pleased with these ear buds. Will probably purchase another pair or two for backups in the very near future.

title: Unbelievable for the price
body: I had a pair of Skullcandy headphones that I really loved - they were comfortable (which has always been the most difficult thing for me), they had a small mic, and there was a controller on the wire (play, pause, repeat, move forward). All they were missing was a volume controller and they would have been a dream come true! Unfortunately, they died.I looked online and read review after review, but I couldn't seem to find a good pair that had all the features I wanted at a decent price. I needed headphones and when I saw these, the ridiculously low price combined with the extremely high rating made them seem like the perfect thing to get in the meanwhile, while I tried to make up my mind.Well let me tell you, I am sold on these and so happy that I bought them. At this point, these are the ones I'm sticking with.COMFORT: They are SO uncomfortable - by far the most comfortable headphones I've ever worn; they have 3 sets of covers of three different sizes. The trouble is always to find something small enough that doesn't hurt my ear, but big enough that it stays securely in my ear and does not fall out with every movement. These are perfect in this regard.PRICE: No comment needed; just look at it. It's wonderful.SOUND: They work really well - the sound of the music or news program is perfect and I really cannot notice any change in terms of quality, pitch, etc. I will also say that I can turn them up loudly enough that everything else is blocked out, but no one around me can hear the music coming out, which is great.I do really wish these had at least a mic - it is very annoying that they don't, and this fact alone will make it so that I keep checking every now and then to see if "the perfect" headphone set is now on the market.They are great though, and I definitely recommend them.

title: My only complaint is the detachable parts...
body: I am one of those people who do not like investing a fortune on simple devices like this. Besides, I am not the most careful user when it comes to headphones. Occasionally, I lose them, step on them, get them tangled or stuck somewhere. Therefore, I want something that I won't be crying after and for this purpose these headphones are great.I am not a musical expert that can recognize small quality differences between headphones but I have noticed nothing bad in the sound quality so far (crystal clear sound and no noise). Each single one lasts around 6 months under my abusive use conditions and during that duration they offer a good quality experience.These earbuds, being priced less than $7 are the best value headphones on the market (excluding those unknown brand ones which I would not buy) and Panasonic brand together with the great price advantage are the main driving factors for me to keep purchasing them over and over for the last two years. (As a rule of thumb I go with the cheapest color no matter what...)My only complaint is that the detachable ear bids are pretty easy to lose and once you lose them it becomes a pain to wear them. it immediately turns yellow due to ear wax and looks like a used q-tip. Besides that it is not comfortable at all. Therefore, after having lost quite a few of those pretty easily (they can fall out very quickly without much force) I decided to dock one star from my rating. I wish they were sold one single piece like some sony earbuds [[ASIN:B00KC89YAC Sony MDR-E10LP Fashion Ear Bud In-Ear Headphones Stereo Earbuds - Black (MDRE10LP)]] or those detachable bits were designed in a better way so that they do not fall apart that easily.4 stars for their excellent value for money and good sound quality, yet minus one star for the detachable parts...

title: Kinda misleading product photo
body: These earbuds work great and have very acceptable audio quality considering their price, but I don't think I will be buying them again.The main picture of the earbuds actually make it hard to tell but they are actually designed with the rubber insert off-center from the rest of the earbud, making them specific to right or left ear exclusively. This isn't that annoying in practice, but after about an hour of wear the plastic edge that sticks out and is supposed to rest outside of your ear-hole while the rubber nubbin thing goes in starts to really hurt. It's kind of like that weird pain you get on your ears when you wear a beanie for way too long. The really annoying thing about this is that it seems to be done as an added feature, like they produce two different buds, when in my experience earbuds that are both the same symetrical shape without the plastic bit sticking out work just as well and are way more comfortable. And it seems like they know this isn't even a particularly desired feature becuase the picture shows the only two angles that obscure this asymmetry: if they showed an angle from above it would be really clear, or even one from the side looking into the speaker hole would make it clear because the appendage where the cord attaches wouldnt be obscuring it. Maybe they chose this view because it has the logo on it, but one more angle would be helpful.If you've tried earbuds that have this asymmetrical design, which rests hard plastic inside the valley of your ear instead of just being exclusively insertable rubber insulation, and have found them comfortable or secure or better for whatever reason then I would say these are great for the price. Personally in the future I'm going to look for the normal round ones, a preference I didn't even know I had before these guys.

title: Incredible budget-friendly in-ear headphones
body: (For the record, I don't have the "Verified Purchase" badge because I bought these at a time when I didn't have Prime, so I got them through my brother's Prime account.)** UPDATE 17.06.2015 ** -- Shortly after publishing this review, I realized that it had been some time since I did some serious music listening on these, since most of my listening has been done on my SOL Republic Tracks Air or Sennheiser HD 280 Pro. Upon more serious listening, I determined that my initial description of the sound profile could be misleading. The bass boost isn't as subtle as my initial review makes it seem. The increase is fine, even enjoyable, for more contemporary genres such as EDM, rock, pop, etc. However, the bass boost can give more instrumental tracks, especially orchestral or choral music, an unnatural feel. Most people won't care, but serious classical music listeners may want to look for an even more balanced pair.** Original Review ** -- For a company that has, historically, focused on music, Apple's OEM headphones have been routinely disappointing. While significantly better than their predecessor, the EarPods still lack bass response and have an overly harsh treble range. Some other phones also come with headphones, which are traditionally very disappointing. If you are currently using a pair of earbuds that came with your phone or iPod, do yourself a favor and get these headphones now. Right now. I'm not even kidding. Don't even bother with the #1 best seller, the similar [[ASIN:B003EM8008 RP-HJE120]]'s. They're not nearly as good as these. Oh, and The Wirecutter has recommended these as a close runner up and former top pick when it comes to the best in-ear headphones under $40.Not convinced yet? Here's a summary of my reasons:The Good :)+ Delightful sound+ Crisp, precise treble+ Strong, but not overpowering bass+ Incredibly comfortable, courtesy of Panasonic's Ergo-Fit design+ Good noise isolation+ Lots of colors!+ Small, low-profile design+ Dirt cheapThe Bad :(- Lower-quality, tangle-prone cable- Lots of cable noise, wind noise, etc.The Quirky :/~ If worn improperly, they will sound horribleStill skeptical? Very well; read on, and I shall elaborate. -- Sound Quality --When I first heard these headphones' cheaper brother, the RP-HJE120, I was amazed that such apparently good sound was available in such a cheap package. I say "apparently" because at that point in time, the best headphones I had ever heard were the $100 basshead-centric SOL Republic Tracks. Both had a very similarly skewed sound signature, with strong bass and scaled-back treble. When compared with the [[ASIN:B000065BPB Sennheiser HD 280 Pro]], a pair of reputable, professional-grade, over-ear studio headphones, their shortcomings became clear. Well, actually muddy, because after listening to the crystal clear treble of the HD 280's, the HJE120's sounded repulsively muddy. When I first got these, the 125's, I promptly compared them to my HD 280's. And I was amazed. I was amazed because of how similar they sounded. The treble was bright and crisp without being overpowering. The main difference in sound is actually one of the shortcomings of the 280's when it comes to general, everyday listening: bass.Studio headphones offer a flat or very nearly flat sound signature, meaning that every frequency is produced at an equal volume. This flat sound signature is exactly what you want for studio use, but for general listening, a subtle increase in bass can add a nice amount of warmth to orchestral music, a little extra thump to EDM, a little extra force to rock, etc. These in-ears have that sort of added bass, which is great. Purists may find it unpleasant, but the vast majority of average users, and even more discerning listeners like myself, will find it thoroughly enjoyable. In general, these headphones have studio-esque clarity with a pleasantly subtle boost to the bass.Now, it's not perfect. One of the main uses of my HD 280's is for private piano practice sessions with my [[ASIN:B00B67OPPW Korg SP-280]]. With such a relatively balanced sound signature, I was curious how these sounded on a piano. I'm glad I tried it out, because it brought out a subtle shortcoming in the sound signature. Notes in the upper half of the piano keyboard sound too thin. I expect this would be because the high-mids or low-highs are slightly scaled back, giving those high-mid piano notes less support. This shortcoming, however, is a very subtle one; most users will not notice it in everyday listening. The only kind of people I can imagine being dissatisfied with this sound signature is hardcore audio purists who only want the flattest, most impartial sound available. If this describes you, you probably already know what headphones you should be looking at. -- Differences from the 120's --The sound is the biggest difference between the low-end Ergo-Fit line, but there are also a few other differences. The 125's add a 1-button remote with a microphone, which is convenient if you like to use headphones for phone calls as well as music. The earpiece design is slightly different and feels a little better. The 3.5 mm plug is also a little nicer. Both have a right-angle plug, but the 125's plug is slightly smaller, working better with phone cases with a smaller headphone cutout. It's not a huge difference, but I expect it'll make a difference for some people. Besides that, they're essentially identical, so the rest of my review (except the conclusion) will be copied from from my review of the RP-HJE120. Feel free to skip to the end if you've read that review. -- Comfort and Design --Moving on to the fit, one of the major strengths of this pair, these sport Panasonic's Ergo-Fit design, which is just a fancy way of saying that the in-ear part, instead of being circular (as most in-ear headphones are), is elliptical. It's one of those "well, duh" innovations; it's a super simple change but it makes a world of difference. Since humans' outer ear canal is taller than it is wide, an elliptical design makes it fit a lot better than a circular design without the need to get a custom-molded earpiece. Until I tried these headphones, all in-ears I had tried required excessive force to put in, were uncomfortable while they were in, and were constantly trying to escape my ears. By contrast, Ergo-Fit headphones are easy to put in, are incredibly comfortable while they're in, and are generally quite good at staying in their place.It's worth mentioning here that the "In-Ear" in the product name is important; if these are not literally *in* your ears, they'll sound horrendous. The sound is designed to be the way it is when it has the benefit of a seal so it only has to fill the tiny cavity in your ear with sound. Without that seal, you'll still be able to hear stuff, but it will be tinny and completely devoid of any bass or low-mid response. If it doesn't feel like you're wearing earplugs, you're doing it wrong.By virtue of the in-ear design, they block out a good deal of outside noise and keep your tunes in your own ears. Sound leakage into the outside world is minimal; don't expect anyone to hear your tunes unless you listen at incredibly loud, unsafe levels. It's also nice that they come in a variety of colors, though not quite as many as the 120's. They're also incredibly convenient by virtue of their small size and incredibly low price!Keep in mind, however, internal sounds (chewing, etc.) are amplified dramatically. So, if you want something you can listen to while eating, you should probably get a pair of on-ear or over-ear headphones. And if you use them for running, it may be worth it to invest in a higher-end pair that is designed with a more secure fit. -- Build --Of course, "dirt cheap" usually means they cut back in some area. Well, if anything, the build would be mildly disappointing. The cable isn't very robust and, at times, can be very prone to tangling. Thankfully, major tangles are few and far between. Some other areas of weakness are cable noise and wind noise, but these have more to do with in-ear headphones in general than these ones specifically. -- Conclusion --If you're still using your OEM Apple earbuds, the choice is very easy: buy these! If you've considered more expensive headphones, you may want to start with the 125's, or maybe just skip them entirely. But regardless, these are one of the most, if not the most value-packed headphones on the market. If you've only listened to music on the headphones that your phone came with, these will revolutionize your music-listening experience. If you've already been introduced to the world of premium audio, these will still revolutionize your view of entry-level audio equipment.You can read my review of the RP-HJE120's here: http://www.amazon.com/review/R2U9L7W86SYD1Q/ref=cm_cr_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B003EM8008

title: Unbelievable!!!  But Not Centaur Proof.
body: Before you read this review I can save you a lot of time. Buy these. However if you want more info than that you can read on, but keep in mind this is not an in depth review on how these buds are technically. I could get into frequency range before and after break in with pink noise and all kinds of other crazy crap, but I'm not going to bore you with that. You might be able to find that type of review in one of the other 1900 five star reviews. I'm just going to give you, what I hear, aside from the voices. But seriously, you can save yourself a lot of time by just buying them. C'MON THEY'RE ONLY $6. Okay, waste your time if you want. Read on if you must. But you'll probably be disappointed.I normally don't write reviews because English is not my first language... bad English is, but on these I just had too. Let me start out by saying before these I had an awesome pair of Bose In Ear Headphones, the old ones. They cost about $100. I did not pay for them. Someone else who is dumb did, and then further proved their lack of intelligence by giving them to me. Actually, they were a, very generous, Christmas gift I got a few years back from the company I work for. Those ear buds were awesome, but like everything else I love, they died. I did manage to get several years of good use out of them, and I treated them like a red headed step child. The fact they lasted as long as they did only goes to show that Bose makes quality stuff. Well, now that I think about it, for $100 maybe they should have been impervious to damage by human means, or possibly even mythical means. I'm going to say it, they should have been Centaur proof.So after what I refer to as the "Great Loss of 2010." I then went on a search for a decent pair of replacement buds, in the $20 range; because as is proof by how I treated the Bose ear buds, I shouldn't be allowed to have nice things. I tried many different types of replacement buds from different manufacturers. I'm not going to list them all because I don't have that kind of time. I will say through all my testing that 50% were just okay, and the other 50%... well I would rather have listened to the final death rattle of a puppy. A really cute and sweet looking puppy. You know, the ones you see on the TV that make you feel guilty for not donating to the ASPCA.Well, after losing yet another pair of the okay ones, how I had those Bose for so long I have no idea, I needed a replacement and decided to try a different brand. I went again to the internet and performed the search, "good cheap headphones for someone who should not have good things." I hit enter, the search results came up, and clicked on a link that appeared to be a tech review site and let me tell you... they are really good at disguising porn sites to look like normal sites in search results these days. So after seeing something that I will never be able to unsee, I came across the Panasonic RPHJE120S and figured for $6 on Amazon I'll give them a shot. I've wasted $6 on far worse.They arrived the day before I ordered them, as Amazon has awesome shipping, especially if you are a Prime Member. I grabbed my Zune, no I don't have an IPOD because I believe Apple, and the late Bea Arthur, are trying to kill me. I put in the buds and selected a track to play, expecting either okay sound or sound so bad that I would black out and wake up naked in a quarry with a dead rodent in my mouth. The latter has only ever happened to me twice in my life... three times tops... four at the most... possibly every Friday. Once my selected track started playing I was just amazed, to find my neighbor on his roof trying to fix his satellite dish in a t-shirt, pair of boxers, rubber rain boots, and a tool belt, with no tools in it, unless you count him. The sound out of the ear buds was awesome too! Clear highs, nice bass, just great frigging sound. It sounded like the puppy was right in the room with me. Then I turned on some music and was further impressed. Initially the highs in certain songs seemed a bit, too high, but over time the buds broke in to be just pure awesome. A $6 dollar pair of headphones that competed with those $100 Bose buds I had. Don't get me wrong, Bose is Bose, but to even approach sound quality on par with those Bose buds in a $6 pair of Panasonic's is damned amazing. But be warned, these buds ARE NOT Centaur proof. I checked. They are fairy resistant though.I have also found them pretty comfortable, and they stay in my ears pretty well. I have fallen asleep with these things in. I did an experiment in which I tried to give myself night terrors using audio. It worked, I have never felt the same since. I may have also released something that can only be described as pure evil into the world. But like I said they were real comfortable and stayed in my ears even when I woke up screaming and thrashing about.Maybe if you are insane, or a complete audiophile, or both, because they usually go hand in hand, you can pick apart these headphones. I have friends who are audiophiles/insane/schizophrenic and they can find issues with even the best audio gear. If you are looking for a good set of buds to use in everyday use that have great sound you really can't beat these. My first pair of these I had for about year, and then I lost them. I actually think I know where I left them but promised I would never go back... DAMN YOU NEBRASKA! I just bought 2 more pairs and just got done making sure the they worked and thought to myself; "I have to review these, they deserve it. Also, I need to teach Amazon a lesson for giving me an outlet to share my opinions." I believe this is the first product I have ever reviewed on Amazon and if you have read this far you can probably see why. I'm an idiot.If you need ear buds... get these. If you don't need ear buds get them. Hell, if you don't like them you are only out $6. With almost two-thousand 5 star reviews, including this one, it's a pretty good sign that these are in fact good buds. If you don't buy these it's a pretty good sign that you are in fact an idiot. I would know, we can spot our own.Yes, I have read the bad reviews, that's what I do. I research a product to death and then look only at the bad reviews to see if the complaints really seem valid or sane. This may sometimes scare me away from perfectly good products like the Nokia N-Gage, HD-DVD, and reusable toilet paper. But sometimes, especially with bigger purchases, I really want to feel sure after ordering the product, that I have made a huge disastrous mistake. However, that being said, I have no idea what happened to some of the people that left bad reviews for these. Maybe they got defective buds, or they are really picky audiophiles, but having heard them myself I can't agree with those reviews. I also don't agree with the law of gravity or that 9/10 of the law involves possession.I have also beat the hell out of my first pair of these buds. I used them for everything, airplane, car rides, while I was cleaning around the house, and in the gym. Occasionally I have used these as a leash for my dog, and in two instances these saved my life from demons, that live in my head. Apparently head demons hate death metal, but absolutely love smooth jazz. Strange, I know. Seriously though, in the gym these things got ripped out of my ears a whole lot of times and they still kept working. Like I said, in the end I lost the first pair, but they still worked great when I lost them.I'll finish on a serious note and stop joking around for just a bit by saying this, at the end of the day everyone's hearing and preferences are different. You can hate something that I love and vice versa. That's just how it is. So in the end there is no guarantee, but if you do the math and you even count the 3 star reviews as bad, only 19% of everyone that bought these did not like them. The other 81% like me... nothing but love baby. These things sound great, fit in my ear comfortably, they stay in my ear pretty good, even when I am at the gym, and seem pretty durable.See, I told you could have saved a whole lot time if you would have just bought them, but you needed to keep reading.

title: Very Comfy and Durable!!
body: UPDATE July 2 2017:At some point, I ended up using these as my primary headphones. After adjusting to them, they are actually very comfortable. The first several times I wore them (see my original review, below) they weren't that comfortable but I evidently didn't give them enough of a chance.1. They are very comfortable to wear even over long periods of time. I use them as my headphones for all day at work and they don't bother my ears.2. They stay in just fine working, walking, etc. I haven't tried them while jogging but I've yet to have much issue with them falling out or coming too loose.3. The sound is great for the price. It's not "Bose" or anything obviously, but the sound is much better than my Coby earbuds and roughly equivalent to my SoundBot. In short, it's balanced and clear without sounding super bass-y nor tinny.4. I've just learned today that they are very durable! I was looking all over for them and couldn't find them so I just used some other headphones and what do you know - I had tucked them in a shirt pocket and accidentally washed (and dried) them! I thought for sure they were done for. The cable even felt a little bit softer, but nope! I plugged them into my laptop and started up a video and they still worked. They didn't sound any different than before I washed(!) them. They were very tangled but I can live with that.Overall, I would highly recommend these headphones now after giving them more of a chance myself. And, if you're like I was initially, try giving your ears a little more time to adjust.ORIGINAL REVIEW (November 2015):I read several reviews before buying these and got intrigued. My ears are small or shaped weird - especially my left one. I don't know what it is but I have never found ear buds comfortable. The original kind that have a Hershey Kiss speaker shape that goes in your ear - those are the worst. The soft in-ear kind are significantly better, but still not comfortable to me and they have a tendency to fall out. I prefer regular over the ear headphones but those aren't so acceptable at work.I bought a pair of SoundBot headphones that were super comfy, but after awhile the left side broke. It still works, but jiggling the wire pauses songs, skips songs, and even turns the volume up or down (which there isn't actually a control on the headphones for). Wanting to find a replacement (and not be locked in to one specific brand of style), I tried these.For starters, they are cute. I mean, for ear buds. The blue is a pretty blue, it is a bit brighter than say, royal/Superman blue.Also, they do actually stay in my ears pretty well. The ear piece sort of hooks into the shape of your ear in a similar manner as the SoundBot ones, locking it in place. It isn't stuck in there however, and still relies a bit on the shove-it-in-your-ear factor.The cord is decently long, a comfortable length in my opinion. However, it is thin. I feel like it could easily have a similar problem within a few months if I don't take extra good care of them. That said, there is no control for anything on these headphones so hopefully if it started getting damaged, it would have no effect.The sound is very good, especially for the price. I have a pair of cheap, Coby ear buds and the sound quality on those is noticeably lower than any others I have tried - so these are better than that, at least. These I would say are a notch below my SoundBot headphones and a few notches above the Coby. I would say my SoundBot headphones are about on par with my original Galaxy S5 headphones for sound quality, if that helps.One thing I dislike is that I can easily hear background noise through these headphones. It isn't crystal clear, but in a noisy office, I find myself having to turn the volume up further. It isn't to say that they are SUPPOSED to have noise-cancel, but I got used to it with my Galaxy S5 headphones and especially with my SoundBot ones. Galaxy S5 headphones block out the majority of background noise, and the SoundBot ones block out all but the loudest. Even without anything playing, they have a noise-cancel effect.OK, now for my primary complaint. All other factors the same, I would be very satisfied with these headphones if they were only more comfortable. It's strange, too, because on the right ear it's perfect. On the left ear, painful. I have avoided using my Galaxy S5 headphones because they aren't so comfortable to me, but I actually practically retired these ones in favor of those. I don't know what it is about my left ear. I tried switching the medium/default tip for the smallest and I might say that's even less comfortable? I can't quite put my finger on it. The speaker hole is narrower - it's an oval, not a circle, giving almost an appearance like the latest iPhone headphones. I thought that would have been perfect, and it is on my right ear. My right ear could probably wear it all day, but my left ear can't stand it.So, I know I have talked most this review about my broken SoundBot headphones, but that's only because they are the most comfortable ear buds I have ever found. For me, comfort takes precedence over pretty much all other features (save for price) because I just can't stand wearing most ear buds. That said, here is the fib ranking:Sound Quality:1st/2nd: Galaxy S5 and SoundBot (tie)3rd: These Panasonic headphones4th: CobyComfort:1st: SoundBot2nd: Galaxy S53rd: These Panasonic headphones4th: CobyAppearance:1st: These Panasonic headphones2nd: Galaxy S53rd: Coby4th: SoundBotPrice:1st: These Panasonic headphones2nd: SoundBot (by a few dollars)(I can't rank the Galaxy S5 because they can with my phone and the Coby came as part of a set with other headphones)Features/Functionality:1st: Galaxy S5 (play/pause/skip/phone and volume)2nd: SoundBot (play/pause/skip/phone)3rd/4th: These Panasonic headphones and Coby (tie - no controls)Overall, if my left ear wasn't so sensitive I would probably love these. Because it is so sensitive, I find them almost unusable. Otherwise, for the price and general quality, I would recommend them for less finicky ears.

title: LOOK NO FURTHER for excellent audio in an inexpensive pair of earphones by a ...
body: LOOK NO FURTHER for excellent audio in an inexpensive pair of earphones by a reputable manufacturer. I spent many hours researching every audio review I could find on the Internet and wound up purchasing these Panasonic RP-TCM125 's, the JVC HAFR37' s for the same price of $14, and then splurged in the same amazon order on Klipsch S4A II's for $64 bucks. Here's what I found out. Manufacturers change models so often it's ridiculous. The new 2014 reviews I found mentioned earphones that were marked on both their own websites and amazon as "No longer sold". Or manufactured. Not a very good confidence builder, from the consumers point of view, right? Long story short, don't let this fact dissuade you. Trust the review, not the manufacturer, who is not necessarily going to make a newer product better. They're catering to 12 year old girls who want earphones that match their favorite color makeup, and not into how they sound. Or any number of other reasons for a manufacturer to discontinue one product, such as product placement deals that the consumer knows nothing about. Call me old school, but I'd like to think I should be able to compare technical specifications, price, and a hands on trial before buying any audio equipment. Stir in reviews by unbiased techies who care, a manufacturers reputation for both their product and customer /technical support, and serve. Not today. Today it's almost completely hit or miss, and clearly today's consumer expects to have to try a few mail order experiments and experiences with return policies and returns before they land on an OK product for an OK price. This all has to do with the consumer being trained by the largest companies, who make deals with the largest retailers, for products made by the cheapest labor in third world countries... All of which has nothing to do with quality of goods and services, and the fact that products today are designed to fail in order to ensure the purchase/dispose/repurchase cycle ... But I digress... The other factor in my research was Android compatibility with cell phone earphones, a market currently dominated by Apple and ios. Let me save you alot of time, fellow Android fans... You won't find cellphone earphones with the inline button switch /Microphone that allows you to use basic music player functions together with basic phone call functions. Period. You might be able to take and end a call, and you can pause and play a song, but I have yet been unable to find an Android compatible product earphone that provides the functionality that Apple users enjoy. If you find different, please let me know! I spent five times as much money on the Klipsch earphones that advertised full audio cell functionality, together with an app designed to do this.
