There was no app, as I recall, indicated in the manual, and it didn't work as advertised. Furthermore, one side of the phones crapped out within two months anyway. I did buy them refurbished, though in the box with the manual. Klipsch no longer makes this product, and the fact they don't work is probably why. They don't make anything new supporting Android, either. Again, if I'm wrong, let me know. Bottom line is this. The Klipsch sounded good, but broke in two months. And, they didn't work as advertised. I used the Panasonic RP-TCM125 for awhile and thought they sounded OK. Then, my roommate borrowed then because his broke.. Common, right, and I used the JVC's. I thought they were OK. When I got the Panasonic RP-TCM125 back from my roommate, I was blown away. Here's why in a nutshell. They fit snuggly in the ear. They block out extraneous noise, yet you can still hear enough to be safe walking around. They don't fall out of your ear. They're angled for better fit. They have a handle, so to speak, so that you can hold each one when placing them in each ear quickly and easily. This little tiny handle which you pinch with your thumb and forefinger, allows you to identify which one is left or right. This is good because the angles on the ear part confuse you at first as to which one is for which ear. Once you use them a couple times it makes perfect sense and you don't have to think about it. You can place them properly in the dark or without looking at them, unlike the klipsch, which are also angled, but you have to look to see which is which and remember, they should face outwards for proper placement. You'll see what I mean when you try the Panasonic. And lastly, they sound great! Could be the round design of the rubber ball that makes a much better fit. Also the microphone is higher up the cord, which probably makes clearer phone conversations. Android users can pause and play music. Android users cannot take and end a call... I'm talking about the mic button. Other earphones I tested you could take and end a call. I believe it was the klipsch, but they couldn't do any audio function except pause play. So when my roommate gave me back the Panasonic RP-TCM125, and I had been using the JVC, I saw immediately what I was missing. Insanely better audio, the JVC's sounded like trebly screeching. The Panasonic sounded full and rich. They fit in my ear. Jvc did not, as well. They blocked out noise. Jvc did not. Sorry I took so long to say something simple. I just wanted to share my journey so others wouldn't have to! On yeah, and fourteen bucks?! Gimme a break. You can find fourteen bucks in the street these days! I'm buying another pair tonight, to stock up. I have black to hide that I'm on the phone, and I'm getting white to help keep people from bugging me and so less people will think I'm talking to myself. It still happens.

title: Very comfortable...great sound for the price
body: I recently lost my earbuds while traveling and where looking for replacements. I had been using a pair of JVC HAFX66 which I absolutely loved as well as my Apple earbuds which were only used for phone calls. So the search began looking for a set of earbuds that hopefully could solve my previous use of two different sets of earbuds to handle music while working out as well as being able to use it for phone calls. I am cheap when it comes to earbuds since I tend to lose or destroy them on a somewhat regular basis, so I try to spend under $30. I have a similar rule for sunglasses for the same reason.To note, my preference is for bass heavy sound. I listen to just about anything from rap, country, rock, classical, and anything else in between. It doesn't matter, I like bass.I was able to compare directly side-by-side the older M6P, M6, M7P, and the RP-TCM125.**MEE M6PI have had these since 2010, but had never used the MIC on them. I just used them at work and at the gym for music for many years. These were very bass heavy which I liked, but not quite as bright in the mids and highs. For the price, these could not be beat at the time. I am pretty sure I paid under $15 for these.+comfortable+decent sound quality+bass heavy+stayed put in my ears+included carrying case-tips came off easier than I would have liked (I have newer M6 that seem to not have this issue)-cable failure in one pair (many years of use and abuse)-MIC voice quality (far end stated I sounded muffled)-MIC issues on iPhone6 (lots of popping, clicking...remember this is a 6 year old set)**JVC HAFX66I had owned the JVC HAFX66 since around 2011 and had gone through 2-3 pairs due to either losing them or getting them run through the washer/dryer accidentally. It appears they are no longer available as well as I would like something with a MIC on it.+very comfortable (used them for background music on a 12hr flight)+sound quality was more balanced the the M6P (strong bass, but mids/highs were smoother)-no MIC**JVC HAETR40 Extreme Sports Pivot Motion FitThese were my first replacements to try since I could pick them up at a local store. In the end, I ended up returning them mostly due to the lack of bass. If these had been cheaper, I probably would have kept them as backup since they were quite functional and comfortable.+fit good+very clear sound quality+MIC worked, no complaints from the far end about my voice quality+short cord+included cord extension-short cord (was almost too short, but then too long with the extension)-lack of bass....serious lack of bassThe lack of bass is what ultimately doomed these for me. The bass was lacking so much, it was like they weren't seated properly within my ear...yet they were. I really wanted to like these.**Panasonic RP-TCM125These were ordered based upon reviews I found for best bang for your buck. These were just to be music earbuds since they don't have a MIC. I have not used these yet while exercising so unknown how well they will stay put. For sure, these are the most balanced sounding out of all of the earbuds I have so far. These overall seem to me to have the best all around sound.+comfortable fit+sound quality is wonderful (voices are warmer, bass is still strong)-cable seems to be pretty cheap, weak (reminds me of the cheapo dollar store ones)**MEE M7PI was still looking for a proper earbud with MIC that can do it all. The price was right and my history with MEE was good so why not.+fit good+good sound quality (bass not as strong as M6)+stay in place while running+comes with carrying case+MIC worked, no complaints from far end about my voice quality (while sitting still)-MIC was popping or dropping out when I was up and moving around (I have a support ticket open on this)**MEE M6I went ahead and also purchased a set of M6 since I pretty knew I would like these.+fit good+stay in place no matter the activity+sound quality decent (strong bass, but mids and highs aren't as powerful)+comes with carrying case-no MICSo all of this....which pair do I use the most? The M7P will end up in my laptop bag for sure for travel purposes...but more than likely I will end up using the M6 the most since I know it stays put while running.

title: Unexpectedly good!
body: I am 18 years old and a bit of an audiophile. I have been searching for a while to find a good quality, cheap, and amazing sound quality ear buds. Recently I bought three pairs from different brands to compare, this pair as one, and this is how they played out:1) [[ASIN:B003EM800S Panasonic RPHJE120S In-Ear Headphone, Silver]]Bass: 9/10Very clear and strong, yet not overpowering. Personally I like my bass very loud and powerful, but these are actually amazing despite not having ear-shattering bass.Test the bass of your earbuds with these two songs that have very powerful bass:http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WyWQDhF4suohttp://www.youtube.com/watch?v=eqnSP0tUhIgOther sounds clarity: 10/10I was very, very impressed by the clear, gentle yet crisp sound of the highs, treble, and vocals from these earbuds.Test the sound quality of your earbuds with these two songs that have clear and crisp vocals:http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-0aLE0nIuAohttp://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_l-yjKdWpj8Comfort: 10/10Again, I was impressed by these earbuds again for their comfort. They come with two other sizes of softgel covers, and I would recommend trying out each size before discarding them in frustration! I wear mine for long periods at a time, 3 - 4 hours, and these did not give me a headache or make my ears itch at all.Durability: 9/10So far, these have held up very very well (I've had them for about a week). I am very impressed. I will keep this review updated about their durability as time goes on.2) [[ASIN:B004PEIHRK iLuv iEP322GRN City Lights In-Ear Earphones - Ultra Bass - Green]]Bass: 10/10By far the best bass I have ever experienced. Absolutely earth-shattering. Though again I would recommend trying each of the three sizes of softgel covers before getting upset. I tried the smaller and medium sized and could barely hear the sound. After putting on the largest size, I was blown away by the power of the bass. Again, test the bass with the two songs I linked to up above!Other sounds clarity: 9/10Again, very impressive. The sounds are clear and powerful, and the bass does not drown them out. Test with the songs linked above!Comfort: 7/10Here is where these earbuds fail. Upon first putting these in, they felt very large and bulky. I got used to it quickly, but after about an hours' wear, my ears and head began to ache. After about two hours use, my ears began to itch, and it only got worse. The sound of these are AMAZING, the best I have ever experienced in an earbud, but if you are going to wear them for more than an hour, it might go well to choose something else.Durability: 9/10These are very well-made, and so far have given me no issues with wear and tear (I've had them for about a week). Again, I will keep this review updated of their condition.3) [[ASIN:B004P1ITV2 Skullcandy INK'D Ear Buds with In-Line Microphone and Control Switch S2INDY-101 (Blue/Black)]]Bass: 8/10I was excited to try these Skullcandy earbuds after hearing so much about the brand. However, after trying these out with both softgel cover sizes, I was not incredibly impressed with the bass. It didn't blow me away. I felt it could have been more powerful. The other sounds overpowered it, and it lacked that boom boom and rich deep sound that most people like. Test with the songs above to see the difference; it surprised me! Though again, I would definitely suggest trying both sizes of softgel covers. The bass was much more pronounced with the larger covers than with the softer ones.Other sounds clarity: 8/10I was impressed by the clear sounds of the music through these earbuds, though as I mentioned above, the bass was slightly drowned out by the other sounds. Test with the songs above if you wish.Comfort: 9/10These earbuds were very comfortable after long hours of wear, and I have no complaints about that aspect.Durability: 9/10No complaints yet, and I will keep this review updated.My conclusion is the best overall earbud is this Panasonic. It won out in all the categories, and I am very happy with them :)

title: $99 Bose vs. $6 Panasonic - seriously? Read on...
body: $99 Bose vs. $6 Panasonic - seriously guys? Come on... Well, I've had my Bose for about 6 years and they're falling apart. I need new ones, but I'm short on cash. Six bucks, sure I can swing that, but I can't stand tinny sound. The audio from iPhone speakers just grates on me. That's about the best you can expect from $6 earbuds, right? Well, you guys/gals were right. No, these are not "quite" Bose quality sound, but they come really close! Not that I've heard many $6 headphones, nor would I want to, but I can safely say these are the best $6 earbuds I've ever heard. I also have an expensive set of JBL that I really don't care for, so I certainly can't imagine these beating JBL. Well, they beat the JBL in virtually every aspect!You will have to play with these a bit to figure out how to get a tight seal in your ear. Once you do, you'll be amazed. Yeah, you do kind of have to jam them in, but they're not uncomfortable. Now my Bose I often forget I'm wearing them. These are not quite that comfortable, but they're not bad. Once you find the sweet spot the bass is strong and clean. The highs are still crisp, clear, and present. Overall the sound is very natural. Nothing is overly exaggerated, nor is anything notably missing. I'd happily pay ten times the price for these, and still feel like I got a bargain! I'd buy these again in a heartbeat. I know you're skeptical. Just try these before you drop a Franklin or two. I guarantee you'll be surprised.Update 1/1/15 - These are now my favorite headphones! The Bose are more comfortable, but these actually sound better! With the Bose, and an EQ set to flat, the Bose sound a little hollow. I typically reduce the midrange just a bit. I put on the Panasonic and it didn't sound right. I set the EQ to flat and the sound is perfect. Deep bass, crisp clear highs, and not too much midrange to sound hollow. I'm still amazed, but very happy. Thank goodness I didn't have to shell out another Franklin to replace my worn out Bose, and thank you Amazon users for directing me to these! It's reviews like these that help me find the best deals. Hopefully someone will find my review helpful too, and save some money.Update 1/12: I'm still amazed! I'm listening to "This is David Bowie" on Spotify with their highest bitrate. (Rest in Peace) He's never sounded so good. The bass is so deep on some tracks you'd swear you were standing directly in front of a massive concert speaker system. Some of the highs are a little subdued. I did cheat and set an EQ to boost slightly at 10 and 20 KHz. In my younger years I considered myself a bit of an audiophile. I've owned many expensive headphones. I still can't believe these little el cheapo $6 Panasonic outperform them all! As for comfort, just as I suspected my ears quickly became used to them. I've fallen asleep with them in a few times now. The Bose are still more comfortable, but these sound just a little better. I've compared these to several models from Koss, Bose, JVC, and Beats. Panasonic wins... I still can't believe it. I'd like to compare them to more esoteric brands. If I do, I'll post the results.Update 3/25/18 - Time for another pair. I was watching a video and the sound from the Left bud was just garbled enough that I couldn't understand the person in the video. I grabbed another pair, and they're perfect. I figured out what happened. I had them connected to my computer and had the volume at full when I was not wearing them. It blew out the Left earbud. I still say these are my favorite headphones. I still say they beat others that cost over ten times as much! Years later, and I'm still amazed at the sound quality. Panasonic better than Bose? under $10 better than Bose? No way... Uh, WAY! WAY better cost with slightly better sound. WAY to go Panasonic. WAY to go Amazon reviewers. WAY... you get the idea... but did you get the earbuds? What are you waiting for, a personal invitation? This is it. I invite you to give these a try. I invite you to compare them to any other earbud, and any cost? Etymotic "might" sound better, but I'm not willing to drop $350 to find out. Assuming they are better, how much? $340 better? Highly doubtful. $60 Etymotic? I would wager these are better.

title: Great sound, construction, and price; as good as a $25 pair
body: Update (2/14/17): Still going strong after almost 10 months of heavy, daily use.I bought these to replace my pair of Symphonized NRG noise-isolating headphones, headphones that I really liked until the construction of the product started to fail on me after almost 2 years of daily use (the glue that holds the wooden earbud pieces together wore away, and the piece where you hold/push the earbuds in began to become very loose to the point where the cord would detach and "fall out" unless held by tape).Frustrated by this, I decided to look for another product, figuring that once I found a cheaper option, having to buy replacement 'buds wouldn't be so costly in the long run, should the need arise. And so far, I'm impressed with the Panasonic ErgoFits for several reasons:1. Product came a day earlier than expected.2. While not noise-isolating, they block out external sounds pretty well, which is very important to me as I live in a somewhat-noisy apartment building and like my silence. There's some slight (emphasis on slight) noise when I touch/brush against the upper part of the cord, but something like noise-from-cord-movement never bothers me when it comes to headphones. When I set my mp3 player, laptop, or YouTube video at 50% volume, I generally can't hear anything except whatever I'm listening to at the time.3. The part where you hold/push the earbuds in is made of some type of hard, inflexible plastic, another important reason why I bought these 'buds. Because the plastic protects the wire leading up into the earbuds themselves, I hope that this will mitigate any construction failures similar to the ones I had to deal with in my old NRG's. Alongside the protective plastic covering, the silicone tips (in small, medium, and large) are extremely comfortable and soft to the touch. I'm gonna assume that because they're so new, removing/putting on the silicone tips are a little difficult to do due to the oval-shaped speakers, but not impossible; this tight fit ensures the silicone tips aren't going to detach and fall off.4. Comfortable to wear. I've had these 'buds in for the past 45min-1hr, and have yet to feel any sort of discomfort or fatigue from them, which is good considering I listen to my headphones almost 24/7. They also form a solid seal once they're in your ears, and will not come out unless I deliberately pull them out.5. Disclaimer: I am not an audiophile. I don't know what what makes "bad" headphones any different from "good" ones other than how they PERSONALLY sound to me. As long as my headphones fit into my laptop/mp3 player, don't sound like they're drowning in cotton balls or were dunked in syrup, I don't care what they're made from, who made them, for what specs they have. All I'll say is audio quality is this: the ErgoFits sound awesome, and I'd compare their quality to my old NRG headphones for their clarity (musical/vocals don't sound muddy, muffled, or distant) and bass (contrary to what other reviewers have wrote, these babies DO have bass, you just have to make sure the earbuds are in right.Hears how I put in my 'buds: First, I take a single earbud and turn it so that it's upside down so that the Panasonic logo is facing straight up. I then line the silicone tip up with my ear canal, then, using a gentle but firm pressure, twist the 'buds down so that the 'bud is right way up, the logo now facing down like how the product image shows. As I twist, I VERY GENTLY push the 'bud into my ear, and I can feel my ear latching onto the 'bud and holding it. If you're having trouble, I recommend listening to a bass-heavy song or a bass test on YouTube on loop while you practice putting these in.)The only (extremely trivial) drawback I can possibly think to say about this product is the packaging it comes in. It's one of those standard, hard-as-hell-to-open clam shell designs meant to deter potential thieves from being able to easily open the package without making a huge scene. A pair of surgical scissors easily remedied this deterrent.

title: A Comparison Of 4 Top Budget Headphones On Amazon
body: I’ve got 4 sets of budget (less than $20) headphones, 3 of which are very popular on Amazon. I wanted to see how they stacked up against each other, along with a newcomer I had heard good things about.For those in a hurry, here’s the ranking. First place is a tie, depending on what features you need.1. [[ASIN:B003EM8008 Panasonic ErgoFit In-Ear Earbud Headphones RP-HJE120-K]] (the product featured on this page)1. [[ASIN:B01CI2RK2S AUKEY In-ear Earbuds Earphones Wired Headphones Ergonomic Comfort-Fit with Mic]]3. [[ASIN:B005VEZ58C JLab JBuds J5 Metal Earbuds Style Headphones]]4. [[ASIN:B0038W0K2K MEE audio Sport-Fi M6 Noise Isolating In-Ear Headphones with Memory Wire]]The Panasonic headphones have over 32,000 reviews with an average of 4.5 stars. Turns out there’s a reason for this. They sound like much, much more expensive headphones. The highs and vocals are very clear, the bass is much more responsive than expected, and they just all-around sound good. They’re comfortable in your ears, look pretty decent, and come with several tips so you can get the best fit & sound (although switching out the tips on these is a pain in the butt). They also happen to be the cheapest. These are the winners for anybody who just needs a basic pair of headphones, without any extra features.To be honest, I was surprised that the Aukey headphones were good enough to tie for first. Aukey isn’t known for their headphones, but perhaps they should be. The sound quality is just slightly below the Panasonics, but these have several extra features that make them just as good. The bass was actually better on these than the Panasonics, but the highs weren’t quite as clear (although not by much). They also come with a wide array of tips. Perhaps more importantly, their connector is slim and straight-in (not angled), so if you have a thick phone case or something like that, these will be more likely to fit – sometimes the angled connectors don’t leave enough length to firmly connect to your phone’s headphone jack. What pulls them firmly into a tie for first, though, is that they also have a microphone and multi-function button, which allows you to answer or end calls, mute/pause/resume playback, and skip forwards and backwards a track. If you need any of these features, the Aukey headphones are the ones for you. Unless you’re actively comparing them side-by-side to the Panasonics, you’ll never know the different in sound quality exists.I’ve heard lots of people swear by their JLab headphones, but those must have been a higher-end model. There’s really no comparison here. Both the highs and lows are muddy, and the vocals just don’t pop like they do with the Panasonic or Aukey models. These are really what I’d expect from a random set of budget headphones – not bad, but definitely not good either. I won’t waste any more space on them – you’ve got two much better options listed above.The MEE headphones are similar to the JLabs – if I had never used the Panasonic or Aukey set, and I paid $15 for them, I’d probably be fairly happy with my purchase. But side by side, it becomes obvious that the highs and vocals are pretty poor (comparatively), and the bass response is lacking. They’re a little behind the JLab set in terms of sound quality, but they do come with the best selection of tips (6 different pairs in 3 different shapes), and a very convenient, sturdy case. But again, unless you specifically need those things, stick with the Panasonics or Aukeys.I hope this comparison has been helpful. Full disclosure: I did receive some of these products from the manufacturers in order to test them. The manufacturers had no idea I was going to end up doing a full product comparison like this, though. I can’t say which items were provided to me, but I can say this: those rated highest were not necessarily the ones I was given to test. At least one of those sets didn’t fare so well in my rankings.

title: 4 Year Durability and Comfort Fit Tests
body: If you're looking for a testament on durability, reliability, and fit, here's my experience with these earphones.Durability and Reliability test - Passed. (Four years of daily use and still functioning)Been using this since 2012 during the day while I work to listen to music. These are the only earphones I use that do have not an in-line microphone because it is not needed for my office work. This gets rolled and tossed into my bag every weekday, yet.. no tears, no loose wires, earpads in tact. Fully functioning. Sound still crisp and clear. It can definitely stand the mild abuse.Comfort test - Passed.The fit is perfect for my ears - very ergonomic as the bud rests nicely in the concha (bowl-shaped cavity) of the ear, cradled by the intertragal notch - like a snug hug. The earpads insert easily into place for a gentle cushioned feel, loosely 'locking' into place very naturally. I've worn these for hours at a time without any aching of the ear or hint of tension so the comfort is definitely there. I have also used them while walking, running, cleaning, and they rarely fall out - thank goodness! It's so bloody annoying when earbuds slip out!Sound test - Passed.Let's be real. Don't expect Sennheiser quality audio on a earphone that costs less than popcorn at the movie theaters. But also don't brush it off either. The sound quality is rather good. To the average user, one most likely won't pick up the nuiances between amazing full bass, mid, or treble. These are definitely not bass-enhanced so don't expect these to pump out your favorite bass-powered beats to your liking, but they will do an acceptable job at nice highs and clear mids.General notes:Because of the fit of the earpad into the ear canal, it does block out some noise. Because I do use these for work, I normally just keep one of these earbuds in for the music while still being attentive within the environment. These earphones also do a decent job at keeping the audio 'localized', meaning if you are jamming music at full blast, the sound will leak from the earphones, but not too much. I can be listening to music at a somewhat loud scale and no one around me can hear it at all.Comfort/fit comparison:I am an android user and faithful Samsung Galaxy series smart phone user. I have the old Samsung Galaxy S2 earphones and the new Samsung Galaxy S6 Edge earphones to compare comfort/ergo fit to. You'll see in my picture the stark difference in overall shape of the earbud from just three earphones. Yet despite my loyalty to Samsung Galaxy smartphones, the fit of their earphones are always terrible, even the latest S6 Edge earphone accessory. Here is why the fit and comfort of the humble Panasonic make my ears happier than most earpieces:- See Samsung Galaxy S2 earphones. Nice soft silicone gel earpad but poor earbud shape that doesn't 'lock' into place with the natural shape of the outer ear. Thus, it frequently comes out like a loose, thick q-tip.- See Samsung Galaxy S6 Edge earphones. Better quality silicone gel earpad that attempts to cushion the supposed natural shape of the ear canal entrance, but utterly fails for anyone with normal sized ears. Even with the smallest size gel earpads, the earphones frequently pop out and is very annoying. Not only that, the gel earpads frequently loosen from the buds itself and falls off. I got my S6 Edge summer of 2015 and I have already used all spare gel earpads + another set of replacement earpads (if you look closely at the photo, I had to resort to a Left earpad for my Right earbud because I had no more replacement earpads to use). I am now down to a single earpad and am giving up on these stupid earphones in general.Verdict:WORTH! Four years on a product that works on a daily and fits great for price of $6-$10? No brainer. Incredibly reliable and good quality.

title: One of the best products I've purchased on Amazon.
body: These are, without question, the best headphones/earbuds I've ever owned. I'll keep this review short, but here are the highlights.Pros:* Affordable: You can buy 10 pairs of these for the price of a single pair of their competitors. After trying out my first pair, I've since bought a pair for my bedroom, home office, and office at work to complement the pair that I bring with me everywhere when out-and-about. So now I always have a pair of headphones right where I need them, and this was achieved for under $40. In fact, I just checked, and when I purchased them in October 2013, these were on sale for $7.50 apiece. So while I'd still HIGHLY recommend them at the current $10-$11 price range, you can probably save a few bucks by waiting to buy them (and, say, using a price tracking website like CamelCamelCamel).* Surprisingly high-quality audio: these little earbuds reliably output beautiful, crisp audio with shockingly deep bass that I can feel in my toes. I honestly was shocked to hear so much more in some of my favorite songs with these than my previous, more expensive headphones.* Comfort: This is of course very personal and what works for me may not work for you, but I have had no issue comfortably wearing these through long plane flights, while running on a treadmill/outdoors (connected to my beloved Sansa Clip), gaming, and listening to podcasts/youtube videos at home. I've worn them for 5+ hours before without my ears getting sore.* Noise-cancellation: I forget if they're even billed as doing this, but the earbuds fit snugly enough to REALLY drown out sound. I've been watching a movie on my laptop before when my roommate and several of his friends arrived at home, greeted me, sat down behind me, and scared me half to death after realizing I'd heard none of the above. They're phenomenal for when you need to drown out your surroundings to relax or just get some things done.* Durability: I've been wearing the hell out of the pair that I take with me everywhere, averaging at least an hour of wearing them per day since I purchased them back in October 2013 (I'm writing this review in mid-August 2014), and they still sound just as good as the day they arrived. This includes not only walking around town with them on but also biking and 3 - 4 days at the gym per week. These little guys just don't seem to quit.Cons:None. Seriously these are among my all time most satisfying purchases, and I highly recommend them to anyone looking for earbuds.The only thing that has been a slight inconvenient for me a few times is cord length. These are about 3.6 feet long according to the listing (I was going to guess 3 or 4 feet), which means, for example, they're not going to stretch behind your desk and to the backside of a PC tower sitting on the floor. While there are easy ways to solve these problems (like buying a male-to-female 3.5mm cord (for about $5 or less) and keep the female end on your desk), it is something to keep in mind if you're not intending to use them with a nearby MP3 player or phone.At the same time, the relatively short cord length makes these headphones *ideal* for using while active. I can use these with my Sansa Clip attached to my running shorts when exercising very comfortably. While there is inevitably still some give in the cord (and the cord does sway a bit while I jog), I don't have to worry about it flailing wildly and or having to bundle up the slack cord before I get to exercising. For reference, I'm about 6'2". So if you're in the 7' range you should probably look to use them with an MP3 player/phone on an armband!I'll update this review if and when one of my 4 pairs finally gives out, but at this point you can rest assured that I'll be replacing them with a new pair of these same earbuds!

title: Survived me and even my mom for an extended period of time
body: First this review is for the Panasonic earbuds with the mic.• I’ve had several pairs of these and they sound ok but why I keep buying them is how well they hold up. I’m rough on haedphones. Really rough. I washed these headphones in my jeans pocket and they still worked. And dried them, in the dryer. They still worked. We have those paddle doors handles and I’m forever catching these headphones on them while walking through. They get ripped from my ears super hard at least once a week and they still work. I once got them caught on the gas stove burner handle which yanked me so hard I poured boiling water from my pasta pot onto myself and part of the cord. I had a 2nd degree burn on my right boobage but my headphones still worked. I’d probaly still have them but I lost them. My other pairs as tough as they were did not survive my mom. But my mom is an earbud series killer. Actually has a pair right now that I bought her many months ago and they still work. She tends to fall asleep with her iPad and when the iPad falls from her lap it dangles from her earbuds. Or she gets them caught in the wheels of her reading table. Or steps on them. That’s how they die. The earbud itself gets cracked. But every other earbud I’ve bought from other brands had the cords go bad in one or both sides of the ears in some invisible way. These don’t do that. I can’t tell you how many pairs of these earbuds I’ve continued to use once one side gets cracked. I literally just cut off the offending side and continue to use for listening to podcasts and stuff like that. (Doesn’t work for music.) Everyone of my pairs of these headphones survived me, I either lost them or lent them to my extremely hard of hearing mom and when hers break you will want to give her yours after a day of her iPad blaring nonstop.I used to have a back up pair but bc I always lose my headphones I’d grab the backup so as not to have to look for them. Then I’d lose the backup and have to search for both. Or my kid or mom would steal them. Even with all the calamity and propensity for losing them, in the last four years I’ve only had four pairs. Maybe five. But I’ve lost two pairs while out of the house. So they were replaced not bc of them breaking but bc they were lost for good. I’ve only had one pair of the ones without a mic and that pair didn’t hold up. Could have been a bum pair but as they were bought by accident in the first place I don’t buy them for myself anymore. As for comfort they’re better then some but I’ve yet to find any ear bud or headphone for that matter that doesn’t bother me after awhile. All earbuds tend to give me a headache after a bit. I think my ear holes are really small and even with the smallest tips they start to hurt me. And agin for sound, they sound good. Not amazing. Don’t expect killer base but I can turn the music up louder then is recommended and not have it have that tinny sound. I’m not an audiophile. I can tell when I have expensive headphones on but couldn’t explain why they sound so much better. But these sound good enough that I’m not taken out of that daydream state I like to go into when listening to music. I gave them five stars bc they’ve lasted when other broke long before but realistically they’re prob more of a four star bc they aren’t going to give you an astounding sound experience. But what they will give you is good enough sound quality and they won’t break in a month. So for that I will bestow five stars. Also the blue on blue mic’d earbuds are quite pretty. So going to buy another pair now.

title: Best sub $10, Second best sub $80 earbuds
body: I have bought four of these guys over the course of several years for several reasons: their excellent quality, construction, performance and price. I am usually wary of companies that generalize in a broad line of products and markets. Panasonic is like sony and samsung when it comes to their foot-in-the-door presence in many markets. The glowering reviews are correct and justified. These earbuds are something to stop and consider.Not that my background matters, but I'm a college student on a budget, an avid music listener and appreciator of quality sound, have a pair of studio-quality sony over the ear headphones that cost over a hundred bucks, and firmly believe that brand does not always equal expected sound. Though I recommend these earbuds over even an $80 pair, I cannot recommend them if you are explicitly looking for NOISE-ISOLATING buds. I bought these headphones to compare with other buds around the $10 range1. JLab JBuds Hi-Fi Noise-Reducing Ear Buds (Black / Electric Blue) JLab JBuds Hi-Fi Noise-Reducing Ear Buds (Black / Electric Blue)2. Sony MDREX10LP/BLK In-Ear Headphones Sony MDREX10LP/BLK In-Ear HeadphonesI owned a pair of jLabs in the past and thought they were decent and ordered them again, while, being impressed with sony's line of over-the-ear headphones, decided to buy a pair of in-ear buds to test them out. I also listened to apple lossless quality music as a control.The short version:#1 Panasonic: They are the most well-rounded-sounding earbuds I've ever worn#2 Sony: decent, but nothing really stood out. They have an inherently low sound level, so you need to adjust your volume up a lot.#3 JLab: These are really washed-out sounding. They over-emphasize base. very low treble, very poor clarity.The slightly longer version:#1 Panasonic: I really liked these earbuds and was even more surprised when compared with the Sony's (which I thought would sound better). They have amazing clarity. Extremely crisp and sharp-sounding. The base was excellent as well. Very well balanced and not over-valued. The bass is not decent, its excellent. There is no overemphasized frequency when listening with these buds.#2 Sony: these were a huge disappointment. Being a sony fan, and owning an amazing pair of headphones, I thought I would get impressive results. Sadly I did not find anything about these ear buds that impressed me. They are just average. They have a subdued bass and do not have as high a clarity as the panasonics. If they were half the price of the panasonics, i'd get them as a "beat up pair". But they cost more than the panasonics.#3 JLab: I also thought this would be on par with Sony. I bought a pair of JLabs many years ago and remember being impressed. These were definitely way more pricey than the panasonics and sonys so I had higher expectations (though I shouldn't have). These buds were the worst because they sounded like you were listening to a live concert in seats a mile away. The sound is completely washed out, not clear at all, and has literally no treble. BUT, these earbuds have heavy bass. If you like to listen to your music with the bass maxed out or don't care about clarity and high frequencies, then these are for you.I am not sure how exactly they can create hardware to do the job of what software equalizers can do, but all these buds are tailored for certain individuals.

title: great, if you get the "real" ones
body: UPDATE: The replacement pair I received had none of the problems I had described below. That replacement pair worked well until this month (February 2021), when the left earbud stopped working. I just ordered another pair, and it is again working well, not suffering from any of the problems I had described below.So, I am giving these a five-star rating, but I do note, along with many other reviewers, that Amazon and/or Panasonic seem to have trouble differentiating the "real" pairs of these earbuds from the apparently fake knock-offs.I think the "real" pairs deserve a five-star rating because1. the audio quality is very good,2. the build quality is fairly good (each pair has lasted about a year for me, with nearly daily use),3. the microphone works fairy well, and4. the price is unbeatable.These were previously The Wirecutter's topic pick for wired earbuds. They now say "this pair simply lacks the detail and the deep low notes that the JBL Live 100 achieves" (https://www.nytimes.com/wirecutter/reviews/best-headphones-under-40/#other-under-50-earbuds-we-like-panel). The JBL Live 100, however, cost about four times more. And, speaking as someone who does care about audio quality but isn't an audio professional, these earbuds sound great to me in terms of detail and low notes.* * *PREVIOUS REVIEW:I have purchased these earbuds four times. The first three times (February 2017, October 2017, November 2018), they were of consistent quality. This fourth time (September 2019), however, I noticed four problems:1. The left and right audio channels are reversed, which is obviously unacceptable and the primary reason for my one-star review.2. The audio quality is worse. One of the earbuds of my previous pair still works, allowing me to compare them directly. The new pair had much less bass and sounded tinny.3. The microphone and control button are now on the left side, which is unusual. (I wonder whether this is related to the left and right channels being reversed.)4. The control button was much stiffer and difficult to press.I have requested a replacement pair and will update this review once I test it.The following are other reviews, of verified purchases, mentioning the same problems.July 9, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/R2DEWIX82PTI5M/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOJuly 13, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/RCUSQ68BGC02/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOJuly 30, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/R14MP4XIUWKFUD/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOAugust 16, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/R2HGBJTRZCV5YG/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOAugust 17, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/R2QLMUCD4V0LNZ/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOAugust 20, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/R1CW73WNAN8IJ1/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOSeptember 2, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/R3KSUE5Z889URZ/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUOSeptember 12, 2019: https://www.amazon.com/review/REXA4FEHV6HZI/ref=cm_cr_srp_d_rdp_perm?ie=UTF8&ASIN=B00E4LGVUO

title: BEST HEADPHONES/EARBUDS EVER!!!
body: These are the best earbuds in the whole wide world. I'm 16 when I'm writing this review and I've go to say, these are the best earbuds in the whole world.My ears are quite sensitive to other earbuds, I would have to take normal buds out for a little bit and let my ears get better since the normal shape of them would stretch them as I would say. But these are different, I was looking for new earbuds to get since all the other ones I've tried have broke. I've tried ones from the dollar store and they suck so bad that I can't believe it. I went on Amazon to look for new earbuds for my mom to buy back in October 2015 and these came up first. I made sure to look at the reviews first before I made my choice on them. The first review I read about them said they were the best ever and they comfortable enough for you to lay on your side which was a big thing for me at the time since my computer was broken and I laid on my bed a lot with a tablet as my source of internet, mostly on my side. When I first used them the night they came, I laid on my side to see if that review held up and IT DID.It felt like I was able to go to sleep in them without even thinking I had anything in my ears. I still have the same pair today.When I used them on the first night I got them, the sound level I had set for the headphones I was using before I got the earbuds I'm still using today, was loud enough that I had to turn it down quite a bit. That proves that these earbuds don't need to be on a high level on a tablet to be able to hear it well.I'm able to leave these in my ears for a long time and my ears will not hurt one bit. These are so good that they can block out outside noise so you can focus on what you're listening to and not be distracted by other noisesThe reason I'm buying another pair is because in case they break because the netting as I call it where the speakers are, fell out when I was trying to clean the gunk from it since I had them in my ears a lot with a wet Q-tip and a little bit of soap. It came out because I was too rough on it and it's staying out so I don't have to keep trying to put it back in backrubbing it on a glue stick and carefully putting it on.If you're looking for good ear buds, these are the ones. I know some people might use the bad parts I said to maybe not buy these but face it, I'm a teenager and you know I'm going to use them a lot. I use them at school, home, and for my tablet that I have. I use them everyday of my life since I got them and they haven't failed me at all for my age. Some earbuds would lose sound in one of the earbuds after using them for a while but these haven't yet for me.These are truly a livesaver for me and for any teenager or anyone who uses headphones or earbuds a lot. I'm glad these can come in my favorite color: Pink. These have gone through quite a bit like me accidently pulling them out of them headphones slot in my computer but they haven't broke yet although I want to keep them from doing so.So in conculsion to what I said, these are the best earbuds you can get for really cheap and not pay over $100 for Beats by Dr. Dre head phones/ear buds. These are truly a dream come true for people like me who use headphones/earbuds a lot

title: Best damn headphones for the price
body: I have tried various styles of headphones meant for working out and cocoon music listening at the coffee shop. Around the neck, over the head, and earbuds. I have tried many brands (Sony, Koss, Skullcandy and on). These are the best ones in all relevant areas.I don't like around the neck headphones because when I lean back into a bench/seat they tend to push off. They also inadvertently work as earmuffs keeping my ears unnecessarily insulated and adding to me burning up. Over the head ones look goofy, mat down my hair, and have the same problem adding to my body heat. So that leaves earbuds.Of the earbuds I've tried, I found that the cushioned ones fall out. The plastic covered ones alternate from acting as earplugs or hurting my ears due to sound distribution or their design. These come with three different sizes of plastic for that right fit in my ear. I actually went with a smaller plastic earbud cover than necessary because I want to hear a little bit of outside noise. I'm not interested in canceling all noise. If you are, you can choose that right size.The next area where these excel is their wiring. Two important positives: the wire doesn't have that problem of generating static if moved around like some other (even more expensive brands). The wires themselves have these great bendability where they goddamn stay put. I can't tell you how many times other pairs of headphones have caught on exercise machines, door handles, and even on the arms of passerbys. Gone is that frustration. These will allow you to bend them and happily stay in place.The plug itself is an "L" shaped jack, which is great for positioning correctly depending on what I'm working out on, as opposed to the straight jack that's easily inferior. Also worth noting is that these contain the sound well to your ears so nearby patrons won't be disturbed by your terrible choice in music.So much to love about these headphones. The only cons, and these are small, is they do have a relative lack of impressive bass. There is some respectable bass but I'm a junkie for hard hitting bass so I want MOAR for my dubsteps, electronica, rap, etc. when I work out. YMMV though, I'm not using the exact fitting plastic piece so that could affect it.Also you have to spend time wrapping them for storage. No biggie though, I just want a carrying case for my lovelies. Quick storage tip: place the earbuds themselves so they're dangling down between the meaty part (yum) of your outstretched palm between your index finger and thumb. Next, wrap five times around your hand, leaving about a 1/2 inch. Pull them off your hand, and tighten the bundle of wire you've made. Finally, wrap the excess cord around that bundle and stick the input jack through the remaining loop. Viola, they're easily folded up and ready to be stored.I love these headphones. My quest is over. I am going to order a few more pairs because EVERY TIME I LIKE SOMETHING THEY MAKE IT GO AWAY. I also have to order some for the woman and other friends due to my demonstration of their awesomeness. Buy now! And Panasonic you can contact me to find out where to send more free ones for shilling for your company.Keep it real. Real sexy.

title: The Best Earbuds I Have Ever Used
body: *PROs: -Very loud (I never go louder than 25% on my iPhone) -Sound quality (Clear stereo sounds) -Great bass (Easy to hear all of the low frequencies) -Noise isolating (At 10% volume, near impossible to hear people talk) -Affordable (I got mine for $8) -Purple (Love the color. I'm sure the others are great, too) -Great fit (Has yet to fall out of my ear)*CONs: -Hurts ears (After hours of use, ears may start to feel sore) -Noise isolating (Touching the cord sounds loud) -I didn't buy sooner (Super great earbuds!)*Detailed Explanation:These earbuds are quite loud; I always check the volume before pressing play, just in case it's at 50% volume or higher. It's possible that they produce just as much noise as many other earbuds, but these isolate a lot of noise due to the way they are designed, which I'm sure enhances the volume.When I say noise isolating, I don't mean that they have any sort of isolation built in; rather, they fit so well in the ear that most outside noise isn't even able to make it to your eardrum. I've had people having normal conversations directly next to me, and I could barely hear them at 10% volume. At 5%, it was still really hard to make out any word they said. This may be a problem for some though, because many internal noises, such as breathing and swallowing are heard more. I'm used to it now, but at first it was weird. Similarly, if you move your head or move the cord, it produces some noise. I've also tried mowing with these earbuds. With my old, Apple earpods, I had to crank the volume up all the way, which is not only extremely loud, but bad for your ears. With these, I had the volume at less than 50%, clearly hearing every note while maintaining a comfortable volume.I've noticed some people in the reviews commented on the bass. Now these aren't huge speakers or even a full headset, so the bass isn't overpowering, but it is definitely there. I hear every frequency sound that I hear with my turtlebeach headset. It's not something that makes your chest reverberate like at a concert, but it's more than I expect from any speakers the size of a dime.Most earbuds I have owned don't seem to like my left ear. I'm not sure why, but they have a tendency to either fall out or lay at a slight angle, making the volume louder in my right ear. With these, the left (and the right) feels natural, and the earbud has yet to fall out. Conversely, if I leave the earbuds in my ears for over an hour, then take them out, they feel slightly sore (this is more of a problem with my right ear than my left). It's not a bad pain, just kind of like someone flicked your ear (we'll say in the summer, when you're not cold); it goes away after a few minutes.*Final Thoughts/TL;DR: I'm very happy that I purchased these earbuds. They are affordable, trendy, and - more importantly - excellent quality. I love the way they make everything sound and that they fit both of my ears. The noise volume is fantastic, especially when coupled with the bonus noise isolating effects. Unless you want the highest quality earbuds on the market, I highly recommend buying these earbuds. 9.5/10-Sknowman

title: Impressive set of In-Ear Headphones !
body: All I have to say, is that I wasn't really expecting much out of 7$ in-earphones made by Panasonic. However, I was blown away when I first listened to them.These are comfortable, they do not fall out of my ears at all, they fit perfectly (although your ear[s] may differ) and they sound tremendously better than any other "cheaper" headphones i've used at even higher prices. iPod headphones are more than this and they're not nearly even ALMOST in the same ballpark in terms of quality-- these are FAR better. These provide GREAT bass (i'm usually listening to electronic music on my set) and quality is marvelous. I cannot believe I did not discover these sooner.I am quite the audiophile but I like to keep a cheap set of headphones (usually skullcandy) because i constantly tear the wire(s) after a short period of time at work with my chair because my PC resides under my desk.So-- skullcandies usually get the job done; while mediocre, they're quite cheap (usually less than or 20$).This time, I decided I would shop around for something else so I could try out other low-end headphones that wouldn't upset me as much if I ruined them, but still provide similar quality.For 6.95$ I usually wouldn't even think about it, because generally speaking with quality in audio you should expect to pay more. Most decent sets are over 100$. This is not the case with these.I will buy several more of theses for future instances where i need a decent set of back-up headphones or even my "private listening" in-earphones because they plug nicely into your ears and do not disturb others around you because they're quiet on the outside while they're in your ears (my over-the-ear sets are very loud and can disturb people around me if i listen at even half volume; with these, you will not disturb anyone). Even at the highest volume, I don't hear any crackling or anything. Impressive work, Panasonic.These are amazing for the price. I most definitely see more of these in my future and I will recommend these to ANYONE in the market for cheap headphones for temporary use or even constant use. I even accidentally dunked one of the buds in my coffee cup at work-- with very favorable results: they work just fine still, after a bit of patting down-- but to say the least, i wasn't that upset when i had done it anyways, because HEY they were only 6.95$!The only thing I have to say, is with my wonderful cord-tearing habit I have, the cord on these is kind of lightweight/fragile/thin seeming. I imagine after a while it will be destroyed very easily. That would be the only benefit a skull-candy set would have over this set. This wire won't bother you though or catch on many things and it's really easy to just hide and run down a shirt or other- it doesn't curl up and tangle like a skull candy set would either due to the material used to cover the wiring. For only 6.95$ I cannot even be upset with that, they sound great and i will try to take care of them, but if need be, i will just buy a new set. it's affordable enough and the quality of these in-earbuds are amazing. Thanks Panasonic for making an affordable, decent product!Get these !

title: Great Low Budget Earbuds
body: I purchased this to replace a pair of JBuds J3s that I've been using for the past three years. I sometimes fall asleep with my earbuds on so I must have yanked the wiring a little too hard while I slept and one side went dead. Based on amazon reviews, I ordered a pair of Sennheiser CX 300B MK II and these Panasonic RPHJE120K to replace the JBuds. I figured these Panasonics were cheap enough where I could wear them in bed and not worry too much.When I received my earbuds, I compared them to a Sony MDR-EX51LP Fontopia and a Skullcandy Smokin that I also had laying around. I'm not an earbud aficionado so my comparison is only between these rather inexpensive choices.Not surprisingly, the Sennheiser, which was the most expensive of the four in the mid $30 range, sounded the best. Its highs, lows, and even the mids were clear and audible. It also required the lowest level of volume on my iphone for comfortable listening.To my surprise, the Panasonic RPHJE120K was not too far off in sound quality and was the cheapest of the four by far at around $7. It didn't have the same level of clarity which was more noticeable in the highs but it was pretty good. It still provided good bass response that was tight and not distorted even at higher volumes. Without comparing these four earbuds side-by-side, I might not have noticed the difference between these Panasonics and the Sennheisers. It required only one extra volume level to produce the same amount of output.I found the Skullcandy Smokin and Sony MDR-EX51LP to fall way behind in sound quality and it wasn't even close. Maybe the best way to describe the difference is the Panasonic and especially the Sennheiser sounded like you were in the room with the musicians while the Skullcandy and Sonys sounded like the music was being played in the next room over. The clarity was bad at all levels and you needed to crank the volume up to twice the level of the Sennheiser and Panasonic to get a similar output level. While the Skullcandys had some bass, the Sony's were horrible. I know people always mention that some reviewers must not be using the correct sized earpiece or are not positioning the earbuds correctly but believe me, I understand that and made sure they were all positioned in my ear for the optimal sound quality. I used to use the Sonys prior to the JBuds and I never really thought the sound quality was that bad. Without something better to compare it to I never knew what I was missing.Other things I liked about the Panasonic earbuds was that the cables do not transmit as much bumping and rubbing sounds as the Skullcandys and the JBud J3s that I had, maybe because the cables are thinner. I also like that the earpieces are offset so I can easily distinguish the right from the left in the dark. The earpiece is also oval shaped which seems to fit more comfortably in my ears. The Sennheisers had better clarity and I'm sure there are other more expensive earbuds with better sound but all things considered, I think these Panasonic earbuds are an exceptional value for the price. I plan to buy a pair for my wife and another as a my backup.

title: Hard to beat for the price.
body: All I can say is "Amazing"! I have been using the non-microphone version of these earbuds for several years now [[ASIN:B003EM8008 Panasonic ErgoFit In-Ear Earbud Headphones RP-HJE120-K (Black) Dynamic Crystal Clear Sound, Ergonomic Comfort-Fit]] and when I saw that they now had a version with a microphone I couldn't wait to try them out. They did not disappoint.Before I continue, remember that these are under $20 and the non-microphone version are under $15. With that price, your first impression will usually be "cheap and crappy". While the parts are of the cheaper variety and the cable could stand to be a bit heavier, the sound quality is amazing - for cheap earbuds. I have many varieties and classes of in-ear monitors with these being the cheapest. These cheap in-ear monitors are within about 95% of the sonic quality (to my ears) of my more expensive Etymotec in-ear monitors. The have great bass response and smooth mids and highs due to the large drivers. No harsh tones. They are of the newer oval "interface" design meaning the parts you put in your ear canal are actually more closely to the shape of your ear allowing for a fantastically comfortable fit. It also helps that that they come with interchangeable silicon tips for a perfect fit. I have worn these on a regular basis for very long periods and have never had issues with sore ear canals. I've also worn these many times while sleeping.I currently use these with a Samsung Galaxy S7 and have no issues with microphone or earphone quality. I'm impressed at how clear the microphone sounds although I can't speak to any noise-cancellation qualities.From a build quality perspective, they are of descent quality for the price. I've had several sets of the non-microphone version in daily use for over a year. The only issue I've ever experienced is a small vent hole on the side that faces the ear getting plugged up and requiring a small needle to clear it out restoring bass to it's proper level.There have been complaints that these in-ear monitors lack bass response and sound "muddy". These are primarily the symptoms of improper placement in the ear canal leading to a broken seal. In-ear monitors require that the business end be sealed against air leaks when properly placed. This is not to block out external noise - although that is one way to tell when the seal is in place. In-ear monitors require a sealed airspace to couple the in-ear monitor's driver with the eardrum. It there is a leak, pressure from the driver leaks out of the ear canal which leads to poor base response and other frequencies that become "unbalanced". The best thing you can do if you purchase these or any other in-ear monitors is to spend 30 minutes test fitting using the different size ear tips. They should fit snugly but not forced and they shouldn't feel like they want to pop out on their own. When the in-ear monitors have been correctly fitted, they should almost feel like they aren't there.

title: Great Sound and Good Enough Fit,  see my comparisons
body: Very good sounding, tied for favorite, see comparisons below to other earbuds. You must pay attention and use the right tip for your ear, as this design does not go deep in ear and thus needs the right rubber tip to get good sound isolation.I compared the Panasonic ergo-Fit on cell phone and laptop outputs, 6 different songs and the with other earbuds listed below:PANASONIC RP-HJE120-K Ergo-Fit ~$6.These are one of my two favorites due to better fit than HAFX1X and almost as good of sound, slightly better sound than Ink'd but not as good of fit -Bass-Excellent, not too boomy -Mid, Good -Highs, Good, not too much (like Ink'ed) -Comfort, Good, a little bit noticeable, but you can wear for many hours. Noise Isolation fit is good, you need experiment with tips to get it right as these leave no margin for error as they do not go deep into ear canal. However they tend to stay in place.SKULLCANDY Ink'd - $11 Ok, no distortion, sometimes short on bass, good for vocals -Bass, good, but a little weak, if positioned well in ear bass can be good -Mid, Excellent, very clear vocals -Highs, Good, perhaps a little too much volume highs -Fit, Very comfortable and best at noise isolation, fitting deep in ear canal, making them my favorite along with the PanasonicJVC HAFX1X $25 - Beautiful sounds, well balanced, nice bass -Bass, OK, the best you'll ever experience in earbuds, almost too much. -Mid, Good, very clear vocals, despite a very rich bass -High, Excellent -Fit, I find it difficult to get them just deep enough to get good noise isolation, using the medium tips. But this made them to be uncomfortable and they WORK THEMSELVES LOOSE (from tight noise isolation fit). In general not uncomfortable, but very noticeable in ear due to large sizeMEELECTRONICS M6 - 18$ Very good,clear sound, no distortion, well balanced -Bass, Excellent, rich and natural sound, almost booming -Mid, Excellent, very clear vocals -Highs, Good, clear no distortion -Fit, Only seam to get good noise isolation fit with long triple flanged tip, but these are not comfortable to wear more than an hour or two.PHILIPS SHE6000/28 ~$14 -Balanced high, mid, and low ranges -Fit, comfortable since they don't go into ear much, 4 different size tips. Don't fit tight in ear, as not designed to go deep enough. Thus little noise isolation, and not suited to noisy environment. None-the-less, even though the do NOT seal-in the sound via tight fit, they seem to have good frequency balance. Also, not good to use for activities, as they easy to fall out. -Nice carry caseSONY MDR-EX300SL - 25$, Horrible, booming bass, distorted -Bass, Ok, booming, too much -Mid, horrible, distorted vocals -Highs, Bad, not present, no highs at all

title: Five Stars for Now, It Has Only Been a Couple Wks
body: I'm reviewing the Panasonic RP-HJE120-K .... I was looking for a replacement headphone set due to my original with the phone, Samsung one, started failing on the right ear.I was searching through tons of listings here on Amazon only and then had to narrow my search by going through only '4 stars and up' rated products. I then went through those filtered products that had more than 50 reviews because this usually means that 'hopefully' enough people have tried them and verified their function or quality. Mind you, I know if a product has too many all 5 star reviews, that more than likely there are many fake reviews within them. I do go through the bad reviews too, just to get a feel of the flaws or weak points on the product. Especially on electronic items. If there are too many people pointing out the same weakness, I will weigh it out... 'is it something I can modify or fix?'. I have modified many products... sometimes all it takes is using a better glue or a sealing adhesive.Ok, so all that put aside... I do like these. The only downfall is that I was expecting a Mic & Volume control on the wire itself, like my old one. My oversight, so I can't penalize the product for my lack of verifying those options. These are available with a Mic (no volume on either model), but I must've rushed through with my order, so be sure you select the correct one. They also come with different sized silicone buds that can easily be changed due to your particular ear canal size. I believe they come with the medium sized ones installed, and then come with a larger and smaller pair. Can't beat that. The over all quality? Not great, but for the cost and how light they are, I think they're worth it as of now. The wire is very thin, so you must take care on how you handle it or store it. Don't just stuff it in a pocket or wrap it around a device too tight. I use a little pouch (like those for coin change or maybe rosary beads). This keeps the lint out of the ear pieces and less tangling (I loosely wrap the headphones around 3 fingers and stick it in the pouch; rarely are they tangled). I tried using them for sleeping with music or falling asleep while watching videos on my phone. They are NOT comfortable if sleeping on your side or on your belly (where your ear is on the pillow). They're great if you can lay on your back though, hardly feel them. Unfortunately, I can't sleep like that...lol At this point, I'd purchase these again if these were to fail at least 6 months from now. I do use earbuds often enough. Maybe not daily, but I do use them for exercise (jogs or runs) or while working to block out noise, like weed whacking or mowing. I bought this from the seller "Amazon.com LLC", which I feel is important to list this info. I've come across sellers with variances in their items, including knockoffs, even if listed as same brand. I'll post an update with a durability review.

title: More than a Bang for Your Buck!
body: I've been without earbuds for almost 2 weeks now; well, sorta. During this time, I've been stuck using my very flimsy headset/earbud headphones that I did use solely as a headset until my Pure Sound Hi-Definition 8mm Stereo Earbuds were stolen.My Pure's costed me about $50 at Fry's Electronics at the time of purchase and I loved them. They never tangled, bass response always worked well when I listened to hip-hop and it was clear and crisp enough for all the other genres of music that were produced in an even range of frequencies. I was really disappointed to have them stolen and don't really have the budget right now to spend over $20 on headphones. Also, just about getting sick and tired of using my headset/earbuds for music that deserves to be outputted through something that actually sounded good.I was a little skeptical about these Panasonic buds being under $7 and yet, received so many positive reviews on Amazon. I've known Panasonic to be a brand of quality products as I have grown up with Panasonic TVs and VCR's in the 90s. However, never have I owned a pair of earphones/buds engineered by them.I've never been a fan of Skullcandy and found out through other reviews that I wasn't alone. There was an option for Sony earbuds under $20 but I decided to spice things up and go out of my norm, as I have always purchased Sony earbuds before my Pure's and never had an issue with them. Bose are out of my budget, and other reviews for Shure's and Sennheisers weren't good enough for me to spend a little more.I just got my package 10 minutes ago and tried them out instantly.Really???? For under TEN(!!!!!) bucks, these headphones are actually great?? Wow. The bass response isn't at apparent as it is in my old Pure's but I think I loved them so much because I just got so used to them. I enjoy my acoustic/singer-songwriter music to not be ruined by the overbearing bass response (as my Pure's often times did). The frequency response in what I've listened to so far on these Panasonics is fairly even which is also really helpful and preferred for my taste in headphones/buds. Not too bassy but just enough, the mids are where they should be and the highs aren't too high and shrill as some headphones can be.Obviously if you're purchasing Bose, the quality differs immensely and you can hear it off the bat, but again, you're paying 30x's more for that kind of engineering and quality (different drivers and such). I've seen Skullcandy's and other brands being priced at $20-$30 and they sound just awful. Yet, Panasonic is a widely-known brand and sold at $10-15 less than the latter.I am completely happy with these! But, that is until my budget goes up; then I might try some Klipsch or Shure earbuds but will definitely keep an extra pair or two of these for casual listening or backup!

title: Mic Sounds Good / Audio Sounds Good / Right-Angled Jack is Ideal for Smart Phones
body: Great microphone, great audio, and right-angle jack makes phone more pocket-friendly.I have bought sooo many (official/approved) Apple EarPods, and they all break. Mostly because when I put my phone in my front pocket and then sit down, the cable juts out from the phone in a straight line, and eventually it wears out, so that signal starts cutting out to one of the ears, or the microphone fails.And I've tried to buy knock-offs, and those sound terrible, or their mics are awful.Today, I received this Panasonic ErgoFit. I've tested the sound and the microphone. Both are excellent (for this price). And, best of all, the right-angled jack means that now my phone will sit easier in my pocket, and the cable will be less likely to get damaged.I'm hoping these last a long time.If you're looking for an affordable set of ear-buds that sound good, have a good microphone for calls/dictation, and a right-angle jack, I heartily recommend this. And I'm someone who *rarely* writes reviews._______UPDATE !I love these earbuds so much, I keep buying them when I lose my last pair. I forgot I wrote this review, then I wrote another review, but amazon wouldn't let me post it because this review already exists. Anyhow, this is what I wrote today May 21st 2019 :_________I used to think Apple Earbuds were the best until I tried these. These sound incredible, and the microphone sounds great to whoever is calling you. The rubber fittings on the earbuds feel great and block out just the right amount of ambient noise. They're so comfortable.I'm a professional musician & producer, and I've been so disgusted by other earbuds terrible speakers -- often way too sibilant, or distorted. I fear they inflict ear damage. Not these. These sound well balanced across the EQ spectrum.My only complaint is with myself : I keep losing earbuds. I've literally bought these 4 times. For Christmas and birthdays sometimes family gives me earbuds, but those always sound terrible, and I keep coming back to this pair. For example, my mom bought me Apple Earbuds for Christmas, assuming I'd prefer them. But then I did a test. I called her from the other side of the house, and I switched the earbuds back and forth between the Panasonic and the Apple, and asked her which one sounded better when I was speaking through the microphone. She chose these ones! Even though they're much more affordable! She was shocked.I'm not loyal to any brands, but, wow, Panasonic really got this one right. THANK YOU. Seriously, the market is flooded with terrible sounding earbuds, and it's really hard to find one that:- Earbuds sound great- Microphone sounds great- Comfortable- AffordableI give these my highest recommendation.Enjoy!

title: Edit : Let me take you back!
body: These earbuds really have so many awesome things about them. I've used them for about 2 years and have purchased three pairs within the time span. The sound quality is great, and I love the shape of the ear bud - you don't have to squish it in your ear, it just glides right through! Also, while being comfortable, they don't fall out easily, and they also aren't bad for working out from my experience. Aside from that, they have also lasted me for quite a bit, the longest being 8 months.My complaint is that out of the blue, my earbuds are having this horribly loud static noise coming from the left ear after putting them in. I've had these for 8 months, so I'm shocked they kept up so long, but normally when my earbuds start "dying", one of the buds does not have any sound. I would have taken that over the horrible static. It was so loud you could feel it, and my ears are a little sensitive, so it was extra unpleasant. So that's the big reason for the 4 stars, but also because I feel that they could improve on the audio jack shape (if that's what it's even called?) for the cable. It's a bended L shape for these earbuds, and they may not suite some phone cases. I recently changed cases, and the only way it would fit was by me positioning it a certain way. It was bothersome having to keep it that way, because I move around a lot and like to keep my phone in my pocket while I clean or exercise. Suddenly, my music stops since I moved a bit, and I keep having to repostion it. My new phone case probably wasn't designed for this type of cable shape in the first place, but I feel that if they stuck with just a long, non bended shape, it could be better suited for all cases. If they won't change it, it would be nice for them to add a photo of that to show the buyers the shape of the cord so they know what they are getting.I suggest buying 2 pairs after you try them for the first time and getting one of those two year warranty things. That way, you can still have one pair to listen on while the other is being repaired (this option is for the die hard music lovers like me who need it everyday and night!). Now I'm switching over to another brand that may suit my phone case and my preferences, but I definitely recommend these. I'll probably come back if I'm not satisfied with my next pair.EDIT: Hands down best earbuds you'll get on Amazon. My last pair lasted 8 months, much longer than others I've had. Other earbuds here that are at a decent price don't sound as great as these. I tried moving onto another brand, but they were about the same price, and they were horrible! I'll take Panasonic back, so sorry for ever switching. Now and forever my go to for earbuds. Also, the audiojack fits perfectly with the case, I'm assuming they changed something, at least for the earbuds with the mic. Love Panasonic, never leaving again!

title: I'm impressed.
body: I'm a bit of a stickler for audio, but am not fortunate enough to buy a pair of headphones without selling those I used before it, so headphones breaking is a devastating outcome for someone such as me. When they do though, you're forced to the budget market. Now, I don't like IEM's, but these deliver. Especially for the price.Now, the quality of the build isn't anything particularly satisfying to feel or look at, but I'm someone who fiddles with things. I've become accustomed to swinging the earbuds, and despite the several times I've smacked these on the ground or even stepped on these, they refuse to break.A big problem I have with IEM's are comfort. I find sticking something in my ear really kind of gross. You could imagine that the design of these would be an instant turn off, as they're designed to literally go into your ear canal. Didn't matter. I used the smallest size pads and stuck them in. For 30 minutes I could have them in and feel no discomfort, which is crazy for IEM's. I'm happy with the comfort of these for what they are, but don't expect to want to have them on for over an hour.I've tried to divide this into sections of build, comfort, features and sound, but these really don't have any features. Regularly this would be a degradation, but they don't need to. They don't have multimedia controls, which was a bit saddening, but the sound quality makes up for that.Now, the sound quality is what really surprised me. The Panasonics are bass-heavy, and they're not very refined. However, it's not overbearing or boomy, which makes me okay with it. The bass is enjoyable. It can excite you. The mids are definitely the mid-range and low-mid range is really where these things shined. The clarity and separation still surprise me at times, and in a good way. The highs are rolled-off a little too soon for my liking, but they aren't sibilant, and they're audible. The audio is enjoyable at all frequencies. Another things that surprised me about the audio was the soundstage, which was surprisingly large for something literally inside of your head. Larger than the M50x's at least, and some low-end Bose headphones. Definitely worth the 10 dollars just for that. The imaging is lackluster, but they can't all be winners. It's just not immersive enough. Then again, I'm using these on the go for school and the bus. I can imagine people talking literally inches away from my ears would make for a rather grounding experience. Noise cancellation is solid. Not much to say there. Passive; it's good. Isolation really surprised me though. Using it in a classroom I can have my music cranked up to max, too loud for my ears, and no one could possibly hear me. Great.Worth ten bucks. Worth 25. Arguably worth 40-50. Buy them if you need them, and you wont be disappointed.

title: Nice volume...and flat response.
body: I bought these Panasonics and also a pair of JBuds for my daughter's iPhone. The ones she had broke, so I logged into my Amazon account, and started poking around. The reviews on both were very good, and since she's a little rough on earbuds (she's 15-1/2) I went ahead and bought both. Got them the very next day which was amazing!I don't normally use earbuds...as I mostly use headphones for online gaming, or for recording music. I have Sony studio headphones, and love them...so I wasn't expecting much from earbuds. I should note that my only experience with earbuds is a cheap $2.99 pair from BigLots played very seldomly through my Android tablet when listening to Pandora.When we got the package with these in it, I couldn't wait to see if there was a difference between these and the ultra-cheap pair I had. WOW! As soon as I plugged them in....I had to immediately turn down the volume. They are MUCH more responsive than the cheap ones! And the fidelity was simply amazing. Crystal clear, very balanced. The bass wasn't overwhelming...kinf of like listening to old JBL studio monitors years ago. Very flat response (which I prefer). Really impressive and very comfortable fit.Don't know about build quality, as we just got them...but they seem to be built very well, and for the price, I'm not sure they can be beat!So next, I plugged in the JBuds. Remember what I mentioned above about having to turn the volume down with the Panasonics? Well, at the same volume level on my daughter's iPhone...I had to hurriedly find the volume and turn it down again. These things are INCREDIBLY LOUD!!! Kind nice so that you're not constantly having to play music at max volume just to have it sound full and rich.My daughter prefers the JBuds, but I personally found that they're a bit "boomier" than I like. The bass seems definitely "enhanced"...but possibly too much so, unless you're into that. Reminded me of a car next to me at a stop light with a subwoofer pounding away. I'm sure at lower volumes, the extra bass would be nice...and it's also nice to know that you have that extra "oomph" if you ever want it.
